topics.title: Pneumatic circuit simulation with single acting and double acting cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Pneumatic circuit simulation with single acting and double acting cylinders # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What is a Pneumatic Circuit? A **pneumatic circuit** is a system that uses **compressed air** as the working medium to transmit and control energy for performing mechanical work. In industrial automation, pneumatic circuits act as the "muscles" of automated systems, driving linear or rotary motion through actuators such as **cylinders** and **motors**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** > A pneumatic circuit is an arrangement of pneumatic components — including power source, air preparation unit, control valves, actuators, and interconnecting pipelines — designed to perform a specific sequence of operations by controlling the flow of compressed air. ## 1.2 Single Acting Cylinder (SAC) — The Simple Pusher ### Formal Definition A **Single Acting Cylinder** is a linear pneumatic actuator that uses compressed air to produce motion in **only one direction**. The return stroke is accomplished by an internal **mechanical spring** (or by an external load such as gravity). It has **only one air port** for compressed air entry. ### Intuitive Analogy — The Bicycle Pump Think of a bicycle pump in reverse. When you push the handle (compressed air enters), the rod shoots out doing work. When you release, the **internal spring** pulls the rod back automatically. You only "blow" air one way — the spring does the return trip. ### Key Specifications - **Working Pressure Range:** 2 bar to **10 bar** (standard industrial air) - **Bore Diameter:** 8 mm to 200 mm - **Stroke Length:** 10 mm to 500 mm - **Force Output:** typically **50 N to 25,000 N** ## 1.3 Double Acting Cylinder (DAC) — The Power Stroke Specialist ### Formal Definition A **Double Acting Cylinder** is a pneumatic actuator that uses compressed air to produce motion in **both directions** (extension and retraction). It has **two air ports** — one at the cap end and one at the rod end — allowing independent control of each stroke. ### Intuitive Analogy — The Syringe with Two Hoses Imagine a syringe connected to TWO separate air hoses instead of one. Push air into the left side → piston moves right. Push air into the right side → piston moves left. No spring needed! Both directions are "powered strokes." ## 1.4 Why Pneumatic Circuits? (Engineering Utility) > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Pneumatic systems are preferred in industries involving: > - **Hazardous environments** (mining, oil & gas) — no spark risk > - **Clean rooms** (pharma, food) — oil-free operation possible > - **High-speed repetitive tasks** — packaging lines run at >100 cycles/min > - **Low-cost automation** — components are 3–5× cheaper than hydraulics ## 1.5 Symbols vs Real Components (ISO 1219 Standard) | Component | ISO Symbol | Real Appearance | |-----------|-----------|-----------------| | Single Acting Cylinder | Rectangle with spring zigzag | Cylindrical metal body | | Double Acting Cylinder | Rectangle with piston rod both sides | Cylindrical metal body | | 3/2 DCV (Spring Return) | Two squares with arrow + spring | Square block, 3 ports | | 5/2 DCV | Five squares with arrows | Square block, 5 ports | | FRL Unit | Filter-Regulator-Lubricator symbols | Modular block assembly | # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Components of a Complete Pneumatic Circuit ### A. Air Supply & Preparation Stage 1. **Compressor** — converts mechanical energy to pneumatic energy 2. **Receiver Tank** — stores compressed air and dampens pulsations 3. **FRL Unit** (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator): - **Filter:** removes dust/moisture (5 µm typical) - **Regulator:** maintains constant downstream pressure (typically **6 bar**) - **Lubricator:** adds fine oil mist for component longevity ### B. Control Stage - **Directional Control Valves (DCV)** — dictate airflow path - **Flow Control Valves (FCV)** — regulate speed using **meter-out** or **meter-in** techniques - **Pressure Control Valves** — relief valves, sequence valves ### C. Actuation Stage - **Single Acting Cylinder** — one air port + spring return - **Double Acting Cylinder** — two air ports, bidirectional force ## 2.2 Directional Control Valve Nomenclature The naming convention is **PORTS / POSITIONS**: - **3/2 Valve:** 3 ports, 2 positions — used with **single acting cylinders** - **5/2 Valve:** 5 ports, 2 positions — used with **double acting cylinders** - **5/3 Valve:** 5 ports, 3 positions — used for mid-position control (e.g., closed center) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize that **3/2 DCV is the standard choice for SAC** and **5/2 DCV is the standard choice for DAC**. This is a guaranteed short-answer question. ## 2.3 Working Logic — Step-by-Step ### Single Acting Cylinder with 3/2 DCV (Spring Return, Push Button Actuated) 1. **Initial State:** Spring holds valve in closed position; air blocked; cylinder rod retracted by internal spring. 2. **Actuation:** Push button pressed → valve shifts → supply air flows to cylinder port. 3. **Extension:** Compressed air enters cap end → piston overcomes spring → rod extends and performs work. 4. **Exhaust:** Air in spring side exhausts through valve to atmosphere. 5. **Release:** Button released → spring returns valve → supply blocked; cylinder port connected to exhaust. 6. **Retraction:** Spring force pushes piston back; air exhausts through valve. ### Double Acting Cylinder with 5/2 DCV (Solenoid Actuated) 1. **State 1:** Solenoid A energized → air to cap end (port A) → rod extends. 2. **Simultaneously:** Rod-end air exhausted through port B → valve → atmosphere (port R or S). 3. **State 2:** Solenoid B energized → valve shifts → air to rod end (port B) → rod retracts. 4. **Simultaneously:** Cap-end air exhausted through port A → valve → atmosphere (port S or R). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application | |---------|---------------|-------|-------------| | $F = P \times A$ | $F$ = Force, $P$ = Pressure, $A$ = Piston area | N, bar ($\times10^5$ Pa), m² | Theoretical force output | | $A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | Cap-end (full) piston area | m² | Extension stroke | | $A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ | Rod-end annular area | m² | Retraction stroke | | $F_{ext} = P \times A_{cap}$ | Extension force | N | SAC extension, DAC extension | | $F_{ret} = P \times A_{rod}$ | Retraction force | N | DAC retraction only | | $v = \frac{Q}{A}$ | Piston velocity | m/s | Speed calculation | | $Q = A \times L \times n$ | Air consumption (per cycle) | m³/s | Air consumption estimation | | $W = F \times s$ | Mechanical work per stroke | J | Energy output | | $P_{pneumatic} = \frac{F \times v}{\eta}$ | Required pneumatic power | W | Compressor sizing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note:** Always convert **bar → Pa** by multiplying by $10^5$. Example: **6 bar = $6 \times 10^5$ Pa = 600,000 Pa**. This is the #1 unit-conversion mistake in KTU papers. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | |----------|-------------| | **Automotive** | Robotic welding clamps, paint shop door actuation | | **Packaging** | Box sealing, label pressing, bottle capping | | **Food Processing** | Sorting, cutting, conveying | | **Textile** | Loom shuttles, fabric clamping | | **Pharmaceutical** | Tablet pressing, vial filling | | **Assembly Lines** | Pick-and-place, screw driving | ## 2.6 Speed Control — Meter-In vs Meter-Out - **Meter-In:** Throttle valve on supply line → controls extension speed (used when load tends to push faster than desired) - **Meter-Out:** Throttle valve on exhaust line → controls retraction speed (more common, prevents jerky motion) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** Meter-out is preferred for double-acting cylinders because air compressibility makes meter-in less stable for retracting loads. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Force Output of a Cylinder ### Given - Piston diameter $D$ = 50 mm = $0.05$ m - Rod diameter $d$ = 20 mm = $0.02$ m - Supply pressure $P$ = **6 bar = $6 \times 10^5$ Pa** ### Step 1 — Cap-end (Full Bore) Area $$ \begin{aligned} A_{cap} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \\ &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.05)^2 \\ &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0025 \\ &= 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ *Conversion logic:* The cap-end sees the full circular piston face because no rod is present there. ### Step 2 — Rod-end (Annular) Area $$ \begin{aligned} A_{rod} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (D^2 - d^2) \\ &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times \left((0.05)^2 - (0.02)^2\right) \\ &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.0025 - 0.0004) \\ &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0021 \\ &= 1.6493 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ *Conversion logic:* The rod-end is annular (ring-shaped) because the rod occupies the central area. ### Step 3 — Extension Force $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ext} &= P \times A_{cap} \\ &= (6 \times 10^5) \times (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 1178.1 \text{ N} \\ &\approx 1178 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Retraction Force (Double Acting Only) $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ret} &= P \times A_{rod} \\ &= (6 \times 10^5) \times (1.6493 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 989.6 \text{ N} \\ &\approx 990 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Force Asymmetry Ratio $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Ratio} &= \frac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} \\ &= \frac{989.6}{1178.1} \\ &= 0.840 \; \text{or} \; 84\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The retraction force is approximately **14% less** than the extension force. This asymmetry is a defining characteristic of double-acting cylinders and must be accounted for in load calculations. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Air Consumption per Cycle ### Given - Stroke length $L$ = 200 mm = 0.2 m - $n$ = 30 cycles/min - Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$ = 1.013 bar - Working pressure $P_{work}$ = 6 bar (gauge) ### Step 1 — Free Air Delivery (FAD) Conversion Factor Air consumption must be expressed in **free air** (at atmospheric pressure), not compressed air. $$ \begin{aligned} P_{abs} &= P_{gauge} + P_{atm} \\ &= 6 + 1.013 \\ &= 7.013 \text{ bar} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Volume of Air per Stroke (Cap End) $$ \begin{aligned} V_{stroke} &= A_{cap} \times L \\ &= (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.2 \\ &= 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \text{ (compressed volume)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Free Air Equivalent $$ \begin{aligned} V_{free} &= V_{stroke} \times \frac{P_{abs}}{P_{atm}} \\ &= (3.927 \times 10^{-4}) \times \frac{7.013}{1.013} \\ &= (3.927 \times 10^{-4}) \times 6.923 \\ &= 2.719 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \text{ per stroke} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Free Air Delivery per Minute $$ \begin{aligned} FAD &= V_{free} \times n \\ &= (2.719 \times 10^{-3}) \times 30 \\ &= 0.0816 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \\ &= 81.6 \text{ liters/min} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Required Compressor Size (with 20% safety factor) $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{comp} &= FAD \times 1.20 \\ &= 0.0816 \times 1.20 \\ &= 0.098 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Piston Velocity & Cycle Time ### Given - Flow rate $Q$ = 10 L/min = $1.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s - $A_{cap} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$ m² - Stroke $L$ = 200 mm = 0.2 m ### Step 1 — Extension Velocity $$ \begin{aligned} v_{ext} &= \frac{Q}{A_{cap}} \\ &= \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-4}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= 0.0849 \text{ m/s} \\ &\approx 85 \text{ mm/s} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Time for One Extension Stroke $$ \begin{aligned} t_{ext} &= \frac{L}{v_{ext}} \\ &= \frac{0.2}{0.0849} \\ &= 2.356 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Total Cycle Time (extension + retraction) $$ \begin{aligned} t_{cycle} &= t_{ext} + t_{ret} \\ &= 2.356 + 2.356 \times \frac{A_{cap}}{A_{rod}} \\ &\text{(retraction is slower because annular area is smaller)}\\ &= 2.356 \times \left(1 + \frac{1.9635}{1.6493}\right) \\ &= 2.356 \times (1 + 1.190) \\ &= 2.356 \times 2.190 \\ &= 5.160 \text{ seconds per full cycle} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Single Acting | Double Acting | |-----------|---------------|---------------| | Air ports | **1** | **2** | | Direction of force | One direction only | Both directions | | Return mechanism | Internal/external spring | Compressed air | | DCV required | 3/2 | 5/2 or 5/3 | | Air consumption | Lower (one stroke uses air) | Higher (both strokes use air) | | Force output | Lower (must overcome spring) | Higher (no spring load) | | Stroke control | Limited by spring length | Unlimited | | Cost | Lower | Higher | | Typical application | Clamping, marking, ejection | Pushing, pressing, conveying | | Speed control | Difficult (spring side) | Easy with meter-out | | Position holding | No (spring retracts) | Possible with 5/3 closed center | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Complete Pneumatic Circuit — Single Acting Cylinder ```mermaid flowchart LR COMP[Compressor
7-10 bar] --> RCVR[Receiver Tank
Storage] RCVR --> FRL[FRL Unit
Filter Reg Lubricator
Regulated to 6 bar] FRL --> DCV1[3/2 DCV
Push Button
Spring Return] DCV1 --> SAC[Single Acting
Cylinder
Cap End] SAC --> EX1[Exhaust to
Atmosphere via Valve] DCV1 --> EX2[Valve Exhaust
to Atmosphere] SAC -.Spring Return.-> SAC ``` **Signal Flow Interpretation:** 1. Compressor supplies high-pressure air (7–10 bar). 2. Receiver tank smooths pulsations and stores reserve. 3. FRL cleans, regulates to **6 bar**, and lubricates. 4. 3/2 DCV controls air admission on operator command. 5. SAC extends when valve is actuated; spring retracts when released. 6. Exhaust air safely vented through silencer. --- ## 4.2 Complete Pneumatic Circuit — Double Acting Cylinder ```mermaid flowchart LR COMP[Compressor
7-10 bar] --> RCVR[Receiver Tank] RCVR --> FRL[FRL Unit
6 bar regulated] FRL --> DCV2[5/2 DCV
Double Solenoid] DCV2 --> CAP[Cap End Port
Air In for Extension] DCV2 --> ROD[Rod End Port
Air In for Retraction] DCV2 --> EX3[Exhaust Port 3
Atmosphere] DCV2 --> EX5[Exhaust Port 5
Atmosphere] CAP --> DAC[Double Acting
Cylinder] ROD --> DAC DAC --> WORK[Work Output
Linear Force] FCV1[Meter Out FCV
on Cap End Exhaust] -.-> DCV2 FCV2[Meter Out FCV
on Rod End Exhaust] -.-> DCV2 ``` **Signal Flow Interpretation:** 1. Two solenoid coils control bidirectional motion. 2. Energizing Coil A → supply to cap end → extension. 3. Energizing Coil B → supply to rod end → retraction. 4. Opposite side exhausts through flow control valves (meter-out speed regulation). 5. Exhaust ports vent through silencers to atmosphere. --- ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Pneumatic Circuit Simulation Environment ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph USER[User Interface Layer] UI[Graphical Workspace
Drag and Drop Components] LIB[Component Library
Cylinders Valves FRL] SIM[Simulation Engine
Real time Animation] end subgraph LOGIC[Logic Processing Layer] PARSER[Circuit Graph Parser] SOLVER[Pressure Flow Solver
Steady state Analysis] SEQ[Sequence Validator
Cycle Logic Checker] end subgraph PHYSICS[Physics Layer] GAS[Ideal Gas Law
PV equals nRT] DYN[Piston Dynamics
F equals MA] THERMO[Thermodynamic
Energy Balance] end UI --> PARSER LIB --> PARSER PARSER --> SOLVER SOLVER --> SEQ SEQ --> SIM SOLVER --> GAS SOLVER --> DYN SOLVER --> THERMO ``` **Interpretation:** - **UI Layer** allows student to drag ISO symbols onto workspace. - **Logic Layer** parses the circuit topology and validates against simulation rules. - **Physics Layer** applies ideal gas law, Newton's laws, and thermodynamic principles to compute realistic motion. - **Simulation Engine** renders animated piston motion in real time. --- ## 4.4 State Transition Diagram — DAC with 5/2 DCV ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Retracted: Initial State Retracted --> Extending: Coil A Energized Extending --> Extended: Piston Reaches End Extended --> Retracting: Coil B Energized Retracting --> Retracted: Piston Returns Home Extended --> Extended: Hold Position No Air Loss for Double Acting Retracted --> [*]: System Off ``` **Key Insight:** Unlike SAC, a DAC with 5/2 double-solenoid valve **holds its position** when neither solenoid is energized — a critical advantage for mid-stroke positioning applications. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Differentiate between a single acting cylinder and a double acting cylinder. Mention the directional control valve used with each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Single Acting Cylinder | Double Acting Cylinder | |---------|----------------------|------------------------| | Air ports | One port only | Two ports | | Return mechanism | Internal spring | Compressed air | | Force direction | One direction | Both directions | | DCV used | **3/2 DCV** | **5/2 DCV** | | Air consumption | Lower | Higher | | Application | Clamping, ejection | Pressing, pushing | **Marks distribution:** 1 mark for definition difference + 1 mark for DCV identification + 1 mark for application/force direction. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **List any six components of a pneumatic circuit and state the function of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks — list any 6):** 1. **Compressor** — converts mechanical energy into pneumatic energy (compressed air). 2. **Receiver Tank** — stores compressed air and dampens pressure pulsations. 3. **FRL Unit** — filters contaminants, regulates pressure, lubricates air. 4. **Directional Control Valve** — controls direction of airflow to actuator. 5. **Flow Control Valve** — regulates actuator speed using meter-in or meter-out. 6. **Cylinder** — converts pneumatic energy into linear mechanical motion. 7. **Pressure Gauge** — indicates system operating pressure. 8. **Silencer** — reduces exhaust noise. --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Common Pitfall 1:** Writing SAC and DAC as "the same" — examiners deduct 2 marks immediately for missing the spring vs no-spring distinction. > - **Common Pitfall 2:** Forgetting unit conversion from bar to Pa — loses 1 mark in numerical problems. > - **Common Pitfall 3:** Drawing valve ports incorrectly (using 5/2 ports for SAC) — circuit symbol mismatch costs 2 marks. > - **Common Pitfall 4:** Not labeling both air ports in DAC diagram — full marks only for correctly numbered ports (1, 2, 3, 4, 5). --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a pneumatic circuit using a **single acting cylinder** controlled by a **3/2 DCV**. State two applications. **(7 marks)** **b)** A double acting cylinder has a piston diameter of **80 mm** and rod diameter of **40 mm**. Supply pressure is **5 bar**. Calculate the **(i)** extension force, **(ii)** retraction force, and **(iii)** force asymmetry ratio. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(a): **Working Principle (5 marks):** **Components:** 1. Compressor 2. FRL Unit (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) 3. 3/2 Directional Control Valve (spring return, push-button actuated) 4. Single Acting Cylinder 5. Silencer on exhaust **Operating Sequence:** - **Initial (OFF) Position:** The 3/2 DCV is held in its normal position by the spring. Port 1 (supply) is blocked; Port 2 (cylinder) is connected to Port 3 (exhaust). The cylinder rod is fully retracted by its internal spring. - **Actuation:** When the push-button is pressed, the valve spool shifts against the spring. Now Port 1 connects to Port 2. Compressed air at regulated pressure (**6 bar**) flows to the cap end of the SAC. - **Extension:** Air pressure acting on the piston overcomes the spring force. The rod extends outward, performing the useful mechanical work (e.g., clamping a workpiece). - **Exhaust During Extension:** The air on the spring side of the piston is pushed out through the same valve (now via the exhaust path) to atmosphere through a silencer. - **Release:** When the push-button is released, the spring returns the valve to its normal position. Port 1 is blocked; Port 2 is again connected to Port 3 (exhaust). - **Retraction:** The internal spring force drives the piston back. Air in the cap-end is expelled to atmosphere. **Two Applications (2 marks):** 1. Workpiece clamping in machining fixtures. 2. Marking / stamping operation in assembly lines. 3. Ejecting finished parts from molds (injection molding). --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(b): **Given Data:** - Piston diameter $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$ - Rod diameter $d = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$ - Pressure $P = 5 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ **Step 1: Cap-end Area** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{cap} &= \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \\ &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.08)^2 \\ &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \\ &= 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Rod-end (Annular) Area** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{rod} &= \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) \\ &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times ((0.08)^2 - (0.04)^2) \\ &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.0064 - 0.0016) \\ &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0048 \\ &= 3.7699 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Extension Force** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ext} &= P \times A_{cap} \\ &= (5 \times 10^5) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 2513.3 \text{ N} \\ &\approx 2513 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Retraction Force** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ret} &= P \times A_{rod} \\ &= (5 \times 10^5) \times (3.7699 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 1884.9 \text{ N} \\ &\approx 1885 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Force Asymmetry Ratio** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Ratio} &= \frac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} \\ &= \frac{1884.9}{2513.3} \\ &= 0.750 \; \text{or} \; 75\% \end{aligned} $$ **Result Summary:** - Extension Force = **2513.3 N** - Retraction Force = **1884.9 N** - Force Asymmetry = **25%** (retraction force is 25% lower) --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **a)** Explain with a circuit diagram the operation of a **double acting cylinder** controlled by a **5/2 DCV**. Discuss the role of flow control valves for speed regulation. **(7 marks)** **b)** For a pneumatic system, the cylinder bore is **63 mm**, stroke is **150 mm**, working pressure is **6 bar**, and cycle rate is **40 cycles/min**. Calculate the **(i)** theoretical extension force, **(ii)** piston velocity if flow rate is **15 L/min**, and **(iii)** free air consumption per minute. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(a): **Circuit Components (3 marks):** - 5/2 Double Solenoid DCV (with two solenoids 'A' and 'B') - Double Acting Cylinder with cap-end port and rod-end port - Two Meter-Out Flow Control Valves (one on each exhaust line) - Silencers on exhaust ports 3 and 5 - FRL Unit providing clean regulated air at **6 bar** **Working (3 marks):** - **Position 1 (Coil A energized):** Supply port 1 connects to port 4 (cap end). Air enters cap end → rod extends. Simultaneously, air from rod end exits through port 2 → port 3 → exhaust (via meter-out FCV → silencer). - **Position 2 (Coil B energized):** Valve shifts. Supply port 1 connects to port 2 (rod end). Air enters rod end → rod retracts. Simultaneously, air from cap end exits through port 4 → port 5 → exhaust (via meter-out FCV → silencer). - **Mid-position (Both coils OFF):** With double-solenoid valve, last position is held. Cylinder remains at current position — useful for clamping during machining. **Role of Flow Control Valves (1 mark):** Meter-out flow control valves regulate exhaust air flow, controlling piston speed in each direction independently. They prevent sudden jerky motion caused by compressibility of air, providing smooth, controlled acceleration and deceleration. Meter-out is preferred over meter-in because compressed air entering the cylinder is highly compressible, while exhaust air being released can be precisely throttled for stable speed control. --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(b): **Given:** - $D = 63 \text{ mm} = 0.063 \text{ m}$ - $L = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$ - $P = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ - $n = 40 \text{ cycles/min}$ - $Q = 15 \text{ L/min} = 15 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ **Step 1: Piston Area** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \\ &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.063)^2 \\ &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.003969 \\ &= 3.1172 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Theoretical Extension Force** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ext} &= P \times A \\ &= (6 \times 10^5) \times (3.1172 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 1870.3 \text{ N} \\ &\approx 1870 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Piston Velocity** $$ \begin{aligned} v &= \frac{Q}{A} \\ &= \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.1172 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= 0.0802 \text{ m/s} \\ &\approx 80.2 \text{ mm/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Free Air Consumption per Cycle (cap end)** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{compressed} &= A \times L \\ &= (3.1172 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.15 \\ &= 4.6758 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Free Air Equivalent** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{abs} &= 6 + 1.013 = 7.013 \text{ bar} \\ V_{free} &= V_{compressed} \times \frac{P_{abs}}{P_{atm}} \\ &= (4.6758 \times 10^{-4}) \times \frac{7.013}{1.013} \\ &= (4.6758 \times 10^{-4}) \times 6.923 \\ &= 3.237 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \text{ per cycle} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Free Air Consumption per Minute** $$ \begin{aligned} FAD &= V_{free} \times n \\ &= (3.237 \times 10^{-3}) \times 40 \\ &= 0.1295 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \\ &= 129.5 \text{ liters/min} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Results:** - Extension Force = **1870.3 N** - Piston Velocity = **0.0802 m/s** - Free Air Consumption = **129.5 L/min** --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam** ### ✅ Key Definitions - **SAC** = Single port, spring return, force in one direction only. - **DAC** = Two ports, air-powered both ways, higher force output. - **3/2 DCV** = Used with SAC; 5/2 DCV used with DAC. ### ✅ Critical Formulas - $F = P \times A$ — always convert bar to Pa ($\times 10^5$). - $A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ — full circular area. - $A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ — annular area. - $v = \frac{Q}{A}$ — piston velocity. - $V_{free} = A \times L \times \frac{P_{abs}}{P_{atm}}$ — free air per stroke. ### ✅ Numerical Constants to Memorize - **Standard regulated pressure:** 6 bar - **Compressor output:** 7–10 bar - **Atmospheric pressure:** 1.013 bar - **Force asymmetry of DAC:** typically 15–25% less retraction force. ### ✅ Diagram Essentials - Always number DCV ports (1 = supply, 3, 5 = exhaust, 2, 4 = working). - Always show FRL upstream of valve. - Always include silencer on exhaust. - Spring symbol must appear on SAC and on 3/2 spring-return valve. ### ✅ Common Mistakes to Avoid 1. ❌ Using Pa when given bar → multiply by $10^5$ first. 2. ❌ Forgetting rod area in DAC retraction force. 3. ❌ Confusing meter-in with meter-out speed control. 4. ❌ Drawing DAC with only one air port. 5. ❌ Neglecting FRL unit in circuit diagram. ### ✅ Lab Simulation Tips (for PCPEL607 Practical Exam) - Use **Festo FluidSIM** or **Automation Studio** software. - Verify circuit by **step-by-step valve actuation simulation**. - Always check **piston direction** matches **load direction**. - For sequential circuits, identify **time delay** vs **pressure dependent** vs **position dependent** logic. - Record **pressure readings** at regulator and confirm they match setpoint.

topics.title: Sequencing of multiple cylinder actuation in different combinations - pneumatic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sequencing of multiple cylinder actuation in different combinations - pneumatic

topics.title: Sequencing of multiple cylinder actuation in different combinations - electro-pneumatic
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Electro-Pneumatic Sequential Circuit:** A control system in which two or more pneumatic cylinders (actuators) are actuated in a predetermined order or sequence, governed by **electrical control signals** (generated by limit switches, push buttons, or sensors) and processed through **electromagnetic relays, contactors, or PLCs** acting on **solenoid-operated directional control valves (DCVs)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab syllabus for **PCPEL607 – Industrial Automation and Robotics Lab**, *Module Experiment 2* specifically deals with the **sequencing of multiple cylinder actuation in different combinations** using electro-pneumatic principles. A "combination" refers to a particular pattern in which the piston rods of the cylinders extend (`+`) and retract (`−)`, e.g., `A+B+B−A−`, `A+B+A−B−`, or `A−B+B−A+`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – PCPEL607):**
> The experiment requires the student to (a) draw the **displacement–time (step) diagram** for the given sequence, (b) derive the **pneumatic power circuit** with 5/2 DCVs, (c) derive the **electrical ladder / relay logic circuit** that activates the solenoids in the correct order, and (d) physically realise the circuit on a **electro-pneumatic trainer kit** and verify the sequence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Think of a **traffic signal system** at a crossroads:
- The **red, yellow, and green lights** are like the **piston rods** of three cylinders (A, B, C).
- Each light must glow in a **fixed, repeating order** (Red → Green → Yellow → Red). You cannot let Green and Yellow glow at the same time.
- The **timer/relay** that switches the lights corresponds to the **electrical relay logic** that energises the solenoid of the next cylinder only *after* the previous one has completed its stroke.
- The **pneumatic supply line (compressed air)** is the energy source that physically pushes each cylinder, just as electricity powers the bulbs.

In the same way, in an electro-pneumatic sequencing problem, **compressed air provides the muscle (power)**, while **electricity provides the brain (logic)**. The student must design the "brain" so the "muscles" work in a controlled order.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why electro-pneumatic (and not pure pneumatic)?**
> - In *pure pneumatic* sequencing, the entire logic is built using air-pilot signals and limit valves. This becomes bulky and unreliable beyond 3–4 cylinders.
> - *Electro-pneumatic* logic uses compact **relays, timers, and contactors** that can handle complex sequences (8, 16, or more steps) cleanly. It is the **industry-standard** for modern automation cells.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities, Constants and Reference Metrics

- **Standard working pressure:** $P_{supply} = 6 \text{ bar} \ (\pm 1 \text{ bar})$ — bold constant.
- **Solenoid coil voltage:** $24 \text{ V DC}$ (most trainer kits) or $230 \text{ V AC}$ (industrial).
- **Cylinder bore sizes (typical):** $25 \text{ mm}, 32 \text{ mm}, 40 \text{ mm}, 50 \text{ mm}$.
- **DCV type:** 5/2 way, single-solenoid or double-solenoid, spring-return / detented.
- **Limit switch type:** **Mechanical roller-lever** (SPDT — single pole, double throw) or **Magnetic reed / Proximity sensor**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of an Electro-Pneumatic Sequencing Circuit

An electro-pneumatic system is the **hybrid** of two sub-systems:

| Sub-system | Function | Key Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Power section (Pneumatic)** | Generates the linear motion | Compressor, FRL unit, 5/2 DCV, double-acting cylinder, silencers |
| **Control section (Electrical)** | Decides *when* and *which* cylinder moves | Push buttons, limit switches (a0, a1, b0, b1), relays (K1, K2, K3), solenoid coils (Y1, Y2, Y3), indicator lamps (H1, H2) |

### 2.1.1 Double-Acting Cylinder
A cylinder with **two ports** — compressed air on one side extends the piston, on the other side retracts it. Each end has a **limit switch** to detect end-of-stroke:
- `a0` → cylinder A fully retracted.
- `a1` → cylinder A fully extended.
- (Similarly `b0, b1` for cylinder B, `c0, c1` for cylinder C.)

### 2.1.2 5/2 Way DCV (5 ports, 2 positions)
- **Position 1 (Normal):** Port 1 (supply) → Port 2 (bore side); Port 4 → Port 5 (exhaust).
- **Position 2 (Actuated):** Port 1 → Port 4; Port 2 → Port 3 (exhaust).
- **Actuation:** Solenoid on one side (single-solenoid spring-return) OR solenoids on both sides (double-solenoid bistable / detented).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **Karnaugh–Veitch (KV) and relay-based sequencing** in KTU labs, **double-solenoid 5/2 DCVs** are preferred because each stroke requires a *discrete electrical pulse*, avoiding the "holding current" problem of single-solenoid valves.

## 2.2 Sequencing Methods (KTU-Exam High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with the following **three** classic methods:

### Method 1 — Cascade Method
- Uses **memory elements (cascade modules)** to remember which step is active.
- Each "step" output energises the next cylinder's solenoid and simultaneously advances the cascade to the next step.
- Suited for *strict A-B-C-A* type linear sequences.

### Method 2 — Step-by-Step (Overlapping Groups) Method
- A **step counter** is incremented by limit-switch signals.
- Each step's output energises one cylinder's solenoid.
- Cylinder returns to home position before the next cylinder actuates (non-overlapping) or remains extended (overlapping).

### Method 3 — Karnaugh–Veitch (KV / Boolean) Method ⭐
- The **most analytical** and **most asked in KTU exams**.
- Each cylinder's actuation condition is expressed as a **Boolean sum-of-products** of limit-switch and start-signal states.
- The Boolean function is **minimised using a Karnaugh map**.
- The minimised function is realised using **electrical relay contacts** (NO / NC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping — KTU 2024:**
> - **CO2:** Design electro-pneumatic circuits for multi-cylinder automation.
> - **CO3:** Apply Boolean logic and Karnaugh maps to industrial sequencing problems.
> - **CO5:** Demonstrate the working of automated systems on trainer kits.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Symbol | Formula or Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Cylinder thrust (extension) | $F_{ext} = P \cdot A_1$ | $\text{N}$, where $A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ |
| Cylinder thrust (retraction) | $F_{ret} = P \cdot A_2$ | $\text{N}$, where $A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$ |
| Air consumption per stroke | $V_{stroke} = A \cdot L$ | $\text{m}^3$, $L$ = stroke length |
| Air consumption per minute | $Q = V_{stroke} \cdot n$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{min}$, $n$ = cycles/min |
| Boolean sum-of-products (SOP) | $Y = \sum m_i$ | AOI logic for solenoid |
| KV-Map wrap-around | Adjacency on $2^n$ map | Gray-code ordering |
| Relay hold condition (KV method) | $Y = (\text{start} \cdot \text{prev}) + \text{latch}$ | Latching / sealing contact |
| Latching contact identity | $Y = (S \cdot \overline{R}) + (Y \cdot \overline{R})$ | $S$ = set, $R$ = reset |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exam answers, students **must draw** the **step diagram (displacement-time chart) FIRST**, then the **KV map**, then the **relay schematic** — in that exact order to score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Sequence `A+ B+ B− A−` (The KTU Classic)

This is the most frequently asked sequence in KTU 2024 lab viva and ESE papers. Two cylinders (A, B), four strokes, return to home. Let's derive the full electro-pneumatic control logic.

### Step 1 — Displacement–Time (Step) Diagram

```
Position
   A+ |        ┌────────┐
      |        │        │
   A  |────────┘        └────────
      |
   B+ |             ┌───┐
      |             │   │
   B  |─────────────┘   └──────────
      |
      └──────────────────────────────── Time
         t0  t1    t2   t3   t4
         S1  S2    S3   S4   S5   (S1 = start)
```

Reading the diagram from left to right:
- **Step 1 (Start → t1):** Cylinder **A extends** (a1 becomes 1).
- **Step 2 (t1 → t2):** Cylinder **B extends** (b1 becomes 1). A is still extended.
- **Step 3 (t2 → t3):** Cylinder **B retracts** (b0 becomes 1). A still extended.
- **Step 4 (t3 → t4):** Cylinder **A retracts** (a0 becomes 1). System back to home.

### Step 2 — Identify the Transition Signal Between Steps

| Step Transition | Cylinder Moving | Solenoid Energised | Transition Signal (cause) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Step 1 → Step 2 | A+ → B+ | Y2 (B+) | `a1` is pressed (A reached extended end) |
| Step 2 → Step 3 | B+ → B− | Y3 (B−) | `b1` is pressed (B reached extended end) |
| Step 3 → Step 4 | B− → A− | Y1 (A−) | `b0` is pressed (B returned to home) |
| Step 4 → Home | A− → Stop | — | `a0` pressed (cycle complete) |

### Step 3 — Write the Boolean Equation for Each Solenoid (SOP form)

Solenoid $Y_i$ must energise only when its **trigger** is true AND no conflicting condition exists.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_1 \; (A^{-}) &= \overline{a0} \cdot b0 \cdot S \\
Y_2 \; (B^{+}) &= a1 \cdot \overline{b1} \cdot S \\
Y_3 \; (B^{-}) &= b1 \cdot \overline{b0} \cdot S \\
Y_4 \; (A^{+}) &= S \cdot \overline{a1} \cdot \overline{Y_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $S$ is the start push-button (NO, momentary). The factors $\overline{a0}, \overline{b1}, \overline{b0}, \overline{a1}$ are **safety interlock conditions** ensuring the cylinder has not already reached the corresponding end.

### Step 4 — Minimise Using Karnaugh Map (KV-Method)

For $Y_2$ (B+), variables are $a1, b1$ (2-variable KV map):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{KV-Map for } Y_2:
\quad
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
b1 \backslash a1 & 0 & 1 \\
\hline
0 & 0 & \mathbf{1} \\
\hline
1 & 0 & 0 \\
\hline
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

The single `1` at cell $(a1=1, b1=0)$ is **isolated** and **cannot be combined** with any neighbour. So:

$$
Y_2 = a1 \cdot \overline{b1} \cdot S
$$

(no simplification possible — already minimal). For longer sequences, adjacent `1`s would merge into 2-cell or 4-cell prime implicants, giving a **smaller relay contact count**.

### Step 5 — Derive the Latching Condition (Memory)

Because double-solenoid valves need only a *pulse* (not a continuous signal), the relay coil $K_2$ (which drives $Y_2$) should latch using its own NO contact:

$$
K_2 = (S \cdot a1 \cdot \overline{b1}) + (K_2 \cdot \overline{b1})
$$

The first term is the **set** (trigger), the second is the **seal-in (latch)** that holds $K_2$ energised until $b1$ is reached (which becomes the **reset** via the $\overline{b1}$ NC contact).

### Step 6 — Realise the Circuit

- **Pneumatic power section:** Two double-solenoid 5/2 DCVs feeding two double-acting cylinders. A and B each have a roller-lever limit switch at both ends.
- **Electrical control section:** A relay panel with $K_1, K_2, K_3, K_4$ (24 V DC coils). Start push button `S1`. Each relay's NO contact drives the corresponding solenoid coil `Y1…Y4`. Each relay's NC contact provides the reset (de-latch).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Sequences

| Sequence | Number of Cylinders | Solenoid Count | Special Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| `A+ B+ B− A−` | 2 | 4 | Classic, basic-level exam question. |
| `A+ B+ A− B−` | 2 | 4 | Overlapping group — B+ must wait for A+, A− must wait for B+. |
| `A+ B+ C+ C− B− A−` | 3 | 6 | Extension of classic. Exam-friendly. |
| `A+ B+ B− A− A+ B+ B− A−` (repeating) | 2 | 4 | Needs a **cycle counter** or latched end-signal. |
| `A− B+ A+ B−` | 2 | 4 | Starts with A *retracting* — counter-intuitive; tests conceptual clarity. |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Electro-Pneumatic Sequencing System)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    comp[Air Compressor 6 bar] --> frl[FRL Unit Filter Regulator Lubricator]
    frl --> manifold[Pressure Manifold]
    manifold --> valveA[5/2 DCV - Cylinder A]
    manifold --> valveB[5/2 DCV - Cylinder B]
    valveA --> cylA[Double-Acting Cylinder A]
    valveB --> cylB[Double-Acting Cylinder B]

    cylA -. limit switch a0 a1 .-> relayPanel
    cylB -. limit switch b0 b1 .-> relayPanel

    startPB[Start Push Button S1] --> relayPanel[Relay Logic Panel K1 K2 K3 K4]
    relayPanel -- 24 V DC --> relayPanel
    relayPanel -- Y1 Y2 Y3 Y4 --> valveA
    relayPanel -- Y1 Y2 Y3 Y4 --> valveB

    relayPanel --> lampA[Indicator Lamp H1]
    relayPanel --> lampB[Indicator Lamp H2]
    relayPanel --> lampRun[Run Lamp H0]

    subgraph Power Section Pneumatic
        comp
        frl
        manifold
        valveA
        valveB
        cylA
        cylB
    end

    subgraph Control Section Electrical
        startPB
        relayPanel
        lampA
        lampB
        lampRun
    end
```

## 4.2 Sequence Flowchart for `A+ B+ B- A-` (KV-Method Logic)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Step1: S1 Start Pressed
    Step1 --> Step2: a1 = 1 limit switch A extended
    Step2 --> Step3: b1 = 1 limit switch B extended
    Step3 --> Step4: b0 = 1 limit switch B home
    Step4 --> Idle: a0 = 1 limit switch A home
    Step4 --> Step1: Repeat Cycle if Auto Mode Selected
```

## 4.3 Ladder Logic Realisation (24 V DC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Line L +24V] --- X1
    X1[S1 NO Start] --- X2
    X2[a1 NO] --- X3
    X3[b1 NC] --- K2A[K2 Relay Coil]
    K2A --- L2[Neutral N 0V]
    K2A -. parallel seal-in .-> K2NO[K2 NO contact]
    K2NO --- X4
    X4[b1 NC] --- L2
    K2NO --> Y2[Solenoid Y2 B+]
    Y2 --> L2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a *fragment* of the full ladder. The full schematic for a 2-cylinder sequence has **four such rungs** (one per solenoid) plus a **master stop / emergency rung** and a **lamp indication rung**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define electro-pneumatic control. List any four components used in an electro-pneumatic circuit.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Electro-pneumatic control is a hybrid automation technique in which *electrical signals* (from sensors, limit switches, or PLCs) are used to actuate *pneumatically-powered* actuators (cylinders) via solenoid-operated directional control valves.
- **Four components (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
  1. **Solenoid-operated 5/2 DCV** — converts electrical signal to pneumatic direction.
  2. **Double-acting cylinder** — the linear actuator.
  3. **Limit switch (mechanical roller-lever or magnetic reed)** — feedback sensor.
  4. **Electrical relay (24 V DC coil)** — implements the Boolean logic.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the role of a Karnaugh map in electro-pneumatic sequencing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A Karnaugh (KV) map is a **graphical Boolean minimisation tool** (1 mark).
- It is used to reduce the **sum-of-products (SOP) expression** that defines when a solenoid must energise (1 mark).
- The minimised expression uses **fewer relay contacts**, making the circuit cheaper, faster, and more reliable (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam, Dec 2023 (Model)

**(a) Draw the displacement-time (step) diagram for the sequence `A+ B+ B− A−`. Hence identify the limit-switch signals that trigger each transition.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
The step diagram (already shown in Section 3) has four active strokes:
- t0 → t1: A extends. Solenoid Y_A+ energised. Transition at t1: signal `a1` goes HIGH.
- t1 → t2: B extends. Solenoid Y_B+ energised. Transition at t2: signal `b1` goes HIGH.
- t2 → t3: B retracts. Solenoid Y_B− energised. Transition at t3: signal `b0` goes HIGH.
- t3 → t4: A retracts. Solenoid Y_A− energised. Transition at t4: signal `a0` goes HIGH.

**Transition Table (must be drawn in the answer sheet):**

| From | To | Cylinder in Motion | Trigger (limit switch) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Home | A+ | A | S1 (Start) |
| A+ | A+ B+ | B | a1 |
| A+ B+ | A+ B− | B | b1 |
| A+ B− | B− | A | b0 |
| B− | Home | A | a0 |

Full marks require the **step diagram + transition table** to be drawn and labelled clearly. *(7 marks — diagram 3 + table 2 + correct logic 2.)*

**(b) Derive the Boolean expression for each solenoid using the KV method and draw the relay schematic.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**
From the step diagram and transition table, the four Boolean equations are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{A+} &= S \cdot \overline{a1} \cdot \overline{Y_{A-}} \\
Y_{A-} &= b0 \cdot \overline{a0} \cdot S \\
Y_{B+} &= a1 \cdot \overline{b1} \cdot S \\
Y_{B-} &= b1 \cdot \overline{b0} \cdot S
\end{aligned}
$$

**KV-Map minimisation example (Y_B+):**
A 2-variable KV map over $(a1, b1)$ yields a single minterm at $(1, 0)$ — the term is already prime, so $Y_{B+} = a1 \cdot \overline{b1} \cdot S$.

**Relay realisation (textual schematic for the answer sheet):**
- **Rung 1 (Y_A+):** `L+` → `S1` (NO) → `a1` (NC) → `K1` coil → `L−`. Parallel: `K1` (NO) — `a1` (NC) for seal-in.
- **Rung 2 (Y_A−):** `L+` → `S1` (NO) → `b0` (NO) → `a0` (NC) → `K2` coil → `L−`. Seal-in: `K2` (NO) — `a0` (NC).
- **Rung 3 (Y_B+):** `L+` → `S1` (NO) → `a1` (NO) → `b1` (NC) → `K3` coil → `L−`. Seal-in: `K3` (NO) — `b1` (NC).
- **Rung 4 (Y_B−):** `L+` → `S1` (NO) → `b1` (NO) → `b0` (NC) → `K4` coil → `L−`. Seal-in: `K4` (NO) — `b0` (NC).
- **Solenoid outputs:** NO contacts of `K1, K2, K3, K4` drive solenoids `Y_A+, Y_A−, Y_B+, Y_B−` respectively (each through a 24 V supply).

Full marks: 2 for equations, 2 for KV simplification, 3 for relay schematic with correct seal-in. *(7 marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a) Explain the cascade method of electro-pneumatic sequencing with a suitable block diagram. List two advantages over the step-by-step method.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Cascade method (5 marks):** Uses a chain of **memory flip-flops** (cascade elements) — each element has an output that is `1` only when its step is active. The output of step $n$ energises the next cylinder's solenoid and, *simultaneously*, shifts the active state to step $n+1$ via a clock pulse generated by the corresponding limit switch. The first step is activated by the Start button; the last step resets the chain.
- **Block diagram (1 mark):** Show the cascade as a series of blocks `Step1 → Step2 → Step3 → Step4 → Step1 (cycle)`, each block having a `Set`, `Reset`, and `Output` terminal.
- **Two advantages (1 mark):**
  1. Simple to design and debug for *strictly sequential* (non-overlapping) operations.
  2. Each step output is isolated, so troubleshooting is step-by-step.

**(b) For the sequence `A+ B+ A− B−`, draw the step diagram and identify the four transition signals. Why does the sequence `A+ B+ A− B−` differ from `A+ B+ B− A−`?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**
- **Step diagram (3 marks):** A extends first; while A is still extended, B extends. Then A *retracts* first (while B is still extended), and finally B retracts. The two sequences have **the same four strokes** but in **different order**.
- **Transition signals (2 marks):** `a1` (A+→B+), `b1` (B+→A−), `a0` (A−→B−), `b0` (B−→Home).
- **Difference (2 marks):** In `A+B+A−B−`, cylinder A must *wait* for B+ before retracting; in `A+B+B−A−`, B must *wait* for B+ completion before retracting while A continues to hold. The Boolean equations change: e.g., trigger for A− is `b1` (not `b0`), and the safety interlock becomes `a0` (not `b0`).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose 3–5 Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the step diagram.** The step diagram is the *foundation* of the entire problem. Without it, the Boolean equations and relay circuit are considered incomplete. **−3 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing NO and NC contacts.** Limit switches that detect an *extension* end give an NO contact (closes on actuation), but in the Boolean expression they appear as `a1` (the *true* state). For the *next* step to start, the *previous* state must reset; this is done using NC contacts in the *seal-in* path. Drawing them backwards costs **−2 marks.**
> 3. **Missing the Start condition ($S$).** Every Boolean expression must include the start-signal $S$ to prevent accidental energisation when the system powers up mid-cycle. **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Not drawing the pneumatic power circuit.** The lab exam expects *both* the pneumatic AND electrical halves. Drawing only the relay logic loses **−3 marks.**
> 5. **Wrong cylinder notation.** Use `+` for extension and `−` for retraction *consistently*. Mixing them up confuses the examiner and loses **−1 mark.**
> 6. **No emergency stop / master reset.** A real circuit must have an E-stop that drops all relays. Missing it costs **−1 mark** in design questions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Always start with the step (displacement-time) diagram** before writing any Boolean equation.
- ⭐ Identify the **transition signal** for each step change (it is the limit switch at the *end* of the *previous* stroke).
- ⭐ Write the **sum-of-products (SOP)** Boolean expression for every solenoid, including the **start button $S$** and the **safety interlock (NC of the destination end)**.
- ⭐ Use a **Karnaugh (KV) map** to minimise the expression; the fewer terms, the fewer relay contacts.
- ⭐ Implement **latching / seal-in** using a parallel NO contact of the relay itself, broken by an NC limit-switch contact for reset.
- ⭐ The **pneumatic power section** uses 5/2 double-solenoid DCVs, double-acting cylinders, and an FRL unit at 6 bar.
- ⭐ The **electrical control section** runs on **24 V DC** for trainer kits (or 230 V AC for industrial panels).
- ⭐ Two cylinders = **4 solenoids**; three cylinders = **6 solenoids**; the pattern scales linearly.
- ⭐ **Cascade method** is best for *strictly sequential* circuits; **KV method** is best for *overlapping groups*.
- ⭐ Include **indicator lamps (H1, H2, H0)** and an **emergency stop** in the relay schematic for full marks.
- ⭐ The final answer must contain: **step diagram → transition table → Boolean expressions → KV maps (if any) → relay schematic → pneumatic schematic** — in that order.
- ⭐ Industrial extensions: **PLC-based sequencing** (using ladder logic in software) has now largely replaced relay-based electro-pneumatics, but the *concepts remain identical*.

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCPEL607 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic inspection systems using machine vision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Automatic inspection systems using machine vision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automatic Inspection Systems using Machine Vision** refers to the deployment of computer-based imaging architectures that emulate and extend human visual perception to perform non-contact, real-time quality control, dimensional verification, defect identification, and process monitoring tasks on manufactured components or assemblies without human intervention.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPEL607)**, Machine Vision is formally defined as:

> A sub-domain of industrial automation that integrates **optical sensing**, **digital image acquisition**, **algorithmic image processing**, and **actuator-driven decision feedback** to automatically extract, analyse, and interpret visual information from a physical scene for the purpose of inspection, measurement, guidance, or control.

A Machine Vision System (MVS) typically replaces or augments human inspectors on a production line, operating at speeds and accuracies far beyond human capability — generally achieving inspection cycle times between **10 ms to 500 ms** with positional repeatability of **±0.01 mm** to **±0.05 mm**, depending on the optics and stage mechanics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment in Module 2 is designed to give students hands-on exposure to the architecture, image acquisition, image processing primitives, and inspection decision logic of an industrial machine vision workstation. Students must record the experimental setup, capture sample images, perform thresholding/edge detection, and report the inspection outcomes as per the lab record format.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Robotic Eye" Concept

Imagine teaching a completely blind robot to inspect a chocolate bar on a conveyor belt. How would you do it? You would mount a camera (its eye), connect it to a computer (its brain), give it a flashlight (illumination), and write instructions on what a "good" chocolate bar looks like versus a "broken" one (the algorithm). When the camera sees the chocolate, the computer compares it to the "good" template and instantly makes a pass/fail decision — sorting defective products into a rejection bin.

That entire closed-loop arrangement of *light → lens → sensor → processor → decision → actuator* **is** a machine vision-based automatic inspection system.

### 1.3 Key Conceptual Building Blocks

| Building Block | Industrial Counterpart | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Eye** | Camera + Lens | Captures reflected light from the object |
| **Brain** | Processor / PLC / Industrial PC | Runs image-processing algorithms |
| **Flashlight** | LED / Laser / Structured Lighting | Enhances feature visibility |
| **Hand** | Conveyor + Reject Actuator | Physically separates OK / Not-OK parts |
| **Memory** | Image Database / Reference Template | Stores the "golden sample" for comparison |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant Reference:** The most commonly cited performance metric in machine vision is the **pixel resolution**, measured in **µm/pixel** or **mm/pixel**. Anything below **10 µm/pixel** qualifies as high-precision inspection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Decomposition — The Five Functional Stages

A machine vision inspection system can be functionally deconstructed into five sequential logical stages. Every industrial implementation — whether inspecting a pharmaceutical tablet or a solder joint on a PCB — passes through this same pipeline.

**Stage 1 — Illumination & Image Formation**
The part to be inspected is placed within the **Field of View (FOV)** of the camera. A controlled light source (LED ring, back-light, dome light, or laser line) projects light onto the part. The reflected or transmitted light passes through the lens and forms an image on the image sensor (CCD or CMOS).

**Stage 2 — Image Acquisition (Sensing)**
The image sensor converts the optical image into an analog electrical signal which is then digitised by an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** into a 2D pixel matrix. A standard industrial camera may output an image of **640 × 480** (VGA), **1280 × 1024** (1.3 MP), or up to **20 MP** resolution.

**Stage 3 — Image Processing & Feature Extraction**
The digitised image is processed in software (e.g., OpenCV, HALCON, LabVIEW Vision, or MATLAB Image Processing Toolbox). Common operations include:
- **Grayscale conversion** and **thresholding**
- **Edge detection** (Sobel, Canny, Prewitt operators)
- **Blob analysis** for counting connected regions
- **Pattern matching** using normalised cross-correlation
- **OCR / OCV** for reading printed codes

**Stage 4 — Decision Logic (Classification)**
The extracted features are compared against tolerance windows or reference templates. The decision can be binary (Pass / Fail), multi-class (Grade A / B / C), or continuous (dimensional measurement in mm).

**Stage 5 — Output & Actuation**
The decision is communicated to:
- A **PLC** via discrete I/O or industrial Ethernet (Profinet, EtherCAT)
- A **pneumatic / solenoid reject mechanism** that physically removes defective parts
- A **HMI / SCADA** dashboard for logging and visualisation

### 2.2 Why Each Stage Matters — The "Why and How"

- **Illumination** matters because *inadequate lighting is responsible for over 70% of failed vision applications.* Without proper contrast, even the most expensive camera cannot capture usable images.
- **Sensor selection** matters because the resolution of the sensor directly determines the smallest defect that can be detected.
- **Image processing** matters because raw images contain noise; algorithms extract only the relevant features (edges, blobs, colours, text).
- **Decision logic** matters because the wrong tolerance band leads to either **Type-I errors** (false rejects — rejecting good parts) or **Type-II errors** (false accepts — letting defective parts pass).
- **Actuation** matters because a perfect inspection is meaningless if defective parts are not physically removed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Field of View (FOV)** | $FOV = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size} \times WD}{f}$ | Sensor Size in mm, WD = Working Distance in mm, $f$ = Focal Length in mm | Determines largest object that can be captured |
| 2 | **Pixel Resolution on Object** | $R = \dfrac{FOV}{N_{px}}$ | FOV in mm, $N_{px}$ = number of pixels along that axis | Smallest detectable defect size (mm/pixel) |
| 3 | **Optical Magnification** | $M = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size}}{FOV} = \dfrac{f}{WD}$ | Dimensionless | Determines image scale on the sensor |
| 4 | **Working Distance** | $WD = \dfrac{f \times FOV}{\text{Sensor Size}}$ | All lengths in mm | Used for fixture design |
| 5 | **Focal Length (rearranged)** | $f = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size} \times WD}{FOV}$ | All lengths in mm | Used for lens selection |
| 6 | **Depth of Field (DOF)** | $DOF \approx \dfrac{2 \times N \times c \times M^{2}}{M^{2} + 1}$ | $N$ = f-number, $c$ = circle of confusion (mm), $M$ = magnification | Determines tolerance on part position |
| 7 | **Minimum Detectable Feature** | $D_{min} = 2 \times R$ | $R$ = pixel resolution (mm) | For reliable detection (Nyquist criterion) |
| 8 | **Number of Pixels Required** | $N_{req} = \dfrac{\text{Part Length}}{D_{min}}$ | Dimensionless | Used to select camera resolution |
| 9 | **Conveyor Throughput Constraint** | $T_{max} = \dfrac{Pitch \times 60}{v}$ (parts/min) | Pitch = centre-to-centre spacing (mm), $v$ = belt speed (mm/s) | Aligns camera exposure with line speed |
| 10 | **Frame Rate Requirement** | $FPS \geq \dfrac{v}{FOV_{along\;motion}}$ | Dimensionless | Avoids motion blur |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Warning:** Never confuse *image resolution* (pixels of the sensor) with *optical resolution* (mm/pixel on the object). KTU questions frequently test this distinction.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** 100% inspection of weld seams, paint finish, and engine block dimensions.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Verification of tablet shape, colour, and presence of embossing on pills moving at 100,000 tablets/hour.
- **Electronics:** Solder paste inspection (SPI) and automated optical inspection (AOI) of PCBs.
- **Food & Beverage:** Detecting foreign objects, checking fill levels, verifying cap presence.
- **Logistics:** Reading barcodes, QR codes, and 2D Data Matrix codes on packages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field of View (FOV) from First Principles

The lens forms an inverted image of the object on the sensor plane. By similar triangles between the object side and the image side of a thin convex lens:

$$
\frac{\text{Object Height}}{WD} \;=\; \frac{\text{Image Height (Sensor Size)}}{\text{Image Distance (}\approx f\text{)}}
$$

When the object distance is significantly larger than the focal length, the image distance approaches $f$ (Gaussian thin-lens approximation).

**Step 1:** Write the similarity ratio.

$$
\frac{H_{obj}}{WD} = \frac{H_{img}}{f}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $H_{obj}$, which by definition is the **Field of View**.

$$
H_{obj} = H_{img} \times \frac{WD}{f}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the standard variable names.

$$
\boxed{FOV = \frac{\text{Sensor Size} \times WD}{f}}
$$

This is the master equation. All other lens-selection equations are direct algebraic rearrangements of it.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optical Magnification and Its Dual Form

Starting again from the similarity of triangles:

$$
M = \frac{\text{Image Size}}{\text{Object Size}} = \frac{f}{WD}
$$

But the *same magnification* can also be expressed in terms of sensor size and field of view:

$$
M = \frac{\text{Sensor Size}}{FOV}
$$

**Step 1:** Equate both expressions.

$$
\frac{f}{WD} = \frac{\text{Sensor Size}}{FOV}
$$

**Step 2:** Cross-multiply.

$$
f \times FOV = \text{Sensor Size} \times WD
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to recover the FOV equation.

$$
FOV = \frac{\text{Sensor Size} \times WD}{f} \;\;\; \text{(verified)}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Camera & Lens Selection

**Problem:** A printed circuit board (PCB) of dimensions **200 mm × 150 mm** must be inspected in its entirety using a 1/3-inch CCD sensor of horizontal size **4.8 mm** with **640 horizontal pixels**. The camera will be mounted **300 mm** above the PCB. Compute the focal length required and the pixel resolution on the PCB.

**Given:**
- PCB horizontal length $= 200 \text{ mm}$
- Sensor horizontal size $= 4.8 \text{ mm}$
- Working Distance $WD = 300 \text{ mm}$
- Horizontal pixels $N_{px} = 640$

**Step 1 — Compute FOV requirement:**

The FOV must be at least equal to the PCB's longest dimension to capture it fully.

$$
FOV = 200 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange the master lens equation for focal length $f$:**

$$
f = \frac{\text{Sensor Size} \times WD}{FOV}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
f = \frac{4.8 \times 300}{200}
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the arithmetic:**

$$
f = \frac{1440}{200} = 7.2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Select the nearest standard lens (typically 8 mm or 6 mm).**
Choosing $f = 8 \text{ mm}$ gives a slightly smaller FOV (better zoom), while $f = 6 \text{ mm}$ gives a larger FOV (more area covered, smaller features).

**Step 6 — Recompute actual FOV with $f = 8 \text{ mm}$:**

$$
FOV_{actual} = \frac{4.8 \times 300}{8} = 180 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute pixel resolution on object:**

$$
R = \frac{FOV}{N_{px}} = \frac{180}{640} = 0.28125 \text{ mm/pixel}
$$

**Step 8 — Minimum detectable defect (Nyquist criterion):**

$$
D_{min} = 2 \times R = 2 \times 0.28125 = 0.5625 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:** With this setup, the smallest defect that can be reliably detected is approximately **0.56 mm**, and the required lens focal length is **7.2 mm**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Throughput and Exposure Timing

**Problem:** A conveyor carries biscuits at a centre-to-centre pitch of **150 mm** at a belt speed of **300 mm/s**. The camera has a vertical FOV of **100 mm**. Determine the maximum inspection rate and minimum required frame rate.

**Given:**
- Pitch $= 150 \text{ mm}$
- Belt speed $v = 300 \text{ mm/s}$
- Vertical FOV $FOV_y = 100 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Compute throughput:**

$$
T_{max} = \frac{\text{Pitch} \times 60}{v}
$$

$$
T_{max} = \frac{150 \times 60}{300} = 30 \text{ parts/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute frame rate to avoid skipping parts:**

$$
FPS \geq \frac{v}{FOV_y} = \frac{300}{100} = 3 \text{ frames/s}
$$

**Step 3 — To allow overlap and tolerate position variation, apply a safety factor of 3×:**

$$
FPS_{design} = 3 \times 3 = 9 \text{ frames/s}
$$

**Result:** The vision system must operate at a minimum of **9 FPS** to safely inspect every biscuit at the given belt speed.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Inspection Approach Selection

| Defect Type | Recommended Technique | Lighting | Typical Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface scratch | Bright-field front lighting | Ring light | Edge detection (Canny) |
| Missing hole | Back-lighting (silhouette) | Back-light panel | Blob count comparison |
| Wrong colour | Diffuse dome lighting | Dome light | RGB histogram matching |
| Cracked component | Dark-field side lighting | Low-angle bar light | Texture / gradient analysis |
| Text misprint | Coaxial red lighting | Coaxial light | OCR / template matching |
| Dimensional error | Telecentric lens + back-light | Collimated back-light | Sub-pixel edge measurement |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Machine Vision Inspection System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Part on Conveyor] --> B[Illumination Source]
    B --> C[Lens Optics]
    C --> D[Image Sensor CCD or CMOS]
    D --> E[Frame Grabber or Camera Interface]
    E --> F[Image Processing Unit Industrial PC]
    F --> G{Decision Logic Pass or Fail}
    G -->|Pass| H[Accept Actuator]
    G -->|Fail| I[Reject Actuator]
    F --> J[HMI / SCADA Display]
    F --> K[Data Logger / SPC Database]
```

### 4.2 Image Processing Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Raw Image Capture] --> P2[Grayscale Conversion]
    P2 --> P3[Noise Filtering Gaussian or Median]
    P3 --> P4[Thresholding Global or Adaptive]
    P4 --> P5[Morphological Operations Erode and Dilate]
    P5 --> P6[Feature Extraction Edges Blobs Contours]
    P6 --> P7[Measurement and Comparison]
    P7 --> P8{Within Tolerance}
    P8 -->|Yes| P9[Mark Part as OK]
    P8 -->|No| P10[Mark Part as REJECT]
    P9 --> P11[Log Result]
    P10 --> P11[Log Result]
```

### 4.3 Experiment Workstation Layout (Lab Setup)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Conveyor with Part] --> L2[Strobe LED Light]
    L2 --> L3[Industrial Camera on Stand]
    L3 --> L4[Lens with Adjustable Focus]
    L4 --> L5[Camera connected to PC via USB3 or GigE]
    L5 --> L6[PC with Vision Software OpenCV or LabVIEW]
    L6 --> L7[Monitor displays live feed]
    L6 --> L8[Output Relay or USB I/O Card]
    L8 --> L9[Indicator LEDs Green for OK Red for NG]
```

### 4.4 Functional Feedback Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Reference Template Stored] --> Q2[Compare with Captured Image]
    Q3[Live Captured Image] --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q4[Compute Difference or Feature Match]
    Q4 --> Q5[Apply Tolerance Check]
    Q5 --> Q6[Generate Pass or Fail Signal]
    Q6 --> Q7[Actuate Conveyor or Reject Mechanism]
    Q7 --> Q8[Update Production Statistics]
    Q8 --> Q1
```

### 4.5 Camera-Optics-Object Geometric Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph opt["Optical Geometry (Similar Triangles)"]
        WD[Working Distance WD]
        F[f Focal Length]
        SS[Sensor Size H_img]
        FOV[Object Size FOV H_obj]
    end
    WD --- F
    FOV --- SS
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024, 3 Marks]**
**Define Machine Vision. List any four industrial applications of machine vision-based inspection systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Machine vision is a branch of industrial automation that uses optical sensors, digital image processing, and computational algorithms to automatically inspect, measure, and identify objects without human intervention.

**Four Industrial Applications:**
1. **PCB inspection** – detection of soldering defects and missing components.
2. **Pharmaceutical tablet inspection** – colour, shape, and embossing verification.
3. **Automotive weld inspection** – checking weld bead continuity and surface finish.
4. **Bottle fill-level inspection** in beverage industry using back-light silhouette.

*(Any four valid industrial applications accepted.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**
**Differentiate between image resolution (pixels) and optical resolution (mm/pixel). Why is optical resolution more important in industrial inspection?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Image Resolution | Optical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Number of pixels in the sensor array (e.g., 1280 × 1024) | Real-world size represented by one pixel (mm/pixel) |
| Unit | Pixels (dimensionless) | mm/pixel or µm/pixel |
| Determined by | Sensor hardware | Sensor size, focal length, working distance |
| Relevance | Determines total information capacity | Determines the smallest defect that can be detected |

**Importance:** Optical resolution directly determines the **minimum detectable defect size** on the actual part. A camera with high pixel count but poor optics may still fail to detect small defects if the optical resolution is too coarse.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model – Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an automatic inspection system based on machine vision. Describe the role of each block. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An industrial camera with a **2/3-inch CMOS sensor** (horizontal size **6.6 mm**) and a horizontal pixel count of **1280 pixels** is fitted with a lens of focal length **16 mm**. The camera is mounted **400 mm** above a metal part. Determine:
   (i) The horizontal Field of View (FOV) in mm.
   (ii) The optical magnification $M$.
   (iii) The pixel resolution on the object in mm/pixel.
   (iv) The smallest defect that can be reliably detected (using Nyquist criterion). **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation [7 Marks]:**

A machine vision inspection system consists of the following sequential blocks:

1. **Illumination Unit:** Provides controlled, stable light (LED ring, back-light, dome light) to maximise feature contrast on the part. Without proper illumination, image quality degrades.

2. **Image Acquisition Subsystem:** Comprises the **lens** (focuses light onto the sensor) and the **image sensor** (CCD or CMOS converts light into electrical signals).

3. **Image Digitisation Unit (Frame Grabber / Camera Interface):** Converts the analog signal into a digital pixel matrix; transmits via USB3, GigE, or Camera Link.

4. **Image Processing Unit (Industrial PC / Embedded Processor):** Runs algorithms — thresholding, edge detection, blob analysis, template matching.

5. **Decision / Classification Logic:** Compares extracted features against reference templates or tolerance windows and produces a Pass/Fail signal.

6. **Actuator / Output Interface:** Sends the decision to a PLC, which triggers a pneumatic reject cylinder or moves a sorting diverter.

7. **HMI / Data Logger:** Displays results, logs SPC data, and provides operator interface.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:**
- Sensor size $= 6.6 \text{ mm}$
- Pixels $N_{px} = 1280$
- Focal length $f = 16 \text{ mm}$
- Working distance $WD = 400 \text{ mm}$

**(i) Field of View:**

$$
FOV = \frac{\text{Sensor Size} \times WD}{f} = \frac{6.6 \times 400}{16}
$$

$$
FOV = \frac{2640}{16} = 165 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Optical Magnification:**

$$
M = \frac{f}{WD} = \frac{16}{400} = 0.04
$$

(Equivalently, $M = \frac{\text{Sensor Size}}{FOV} = \frac{6.6}{165} = 0.04$, verified.)

**(iii) Pixel Resolution on Object:**

$$
R = \frac{FOV}{N_{px}} = \frac{165}{1280} = 0.1289 \text{ mm/pixel}
$$

**(iv) Minimum Detectable Defect:**

$$
D_{min} = 2R = 2 \times 0.1289 = 0.2578 \text{ mm} \approx 258 \;\mu\text{m}
$$

**Result:** The system can reliably detect defects as small as **0.26 mm**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the importance of **illumination techniques** in machine vision. Compare front-lighting, back-lighting, and structured lighting with suitable applications. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A conveyor belt moves bottles at a pitch of **200 mm** with a velocity of **500 mm/s**. A camera with a vertical FOV of **120 mm** is used for label inspection.
   (i) Calculate the maximum inspection rate in bottles/min.
   (ii) Determine the minimum frame rate required to avoid missing any bottle.
   (iii) If the camera operates at **20 FPS**, what percentage of time is the camera idle, assuming each inspection takes one frame? **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Illumination Techniques [7 Marks]:**

Illumination is the single most critical factor for reliable machine vision. It creates the contrast between the feature of interest and the background.

| Technique | Geometry | Best For | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Front / Bright-field** | Light from front of object | Reflective features, surface defects | PCB solder inspection |
| **Back / Silhouette** | Light from behind object | Outline measurement, hole detection | Bottle cap presence, dimensioning |
| **Structured (Laser line)** | Projected line/sheet | 3D profiling, height mapping | Weld bead profile, road surface |
| **Dome / Diffuse** | Hemispherical uniform light | Curved, shiny, complex surfaces | Polished metal, food items |
| **Coaxial** | Light through lens axis | Specular surfaces, mirror inspection | Polished metal, semiconductor wafers |

Proper illumination reduces algorithmic complexity and improves detection reliability.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:**
- Pitch $= 200 \text{ mm}$
- Belt speed $v = 500 \text{ mm/s}$
- Vertical FOV $FOV_y = 120 \text{ mm}$
- Camera FPS $= 20$

**(i) Inspection Rate:**

$$
T_{max} = \frac{\text{Pitch} \times 60}{v} = \frac{200 \times 60}{500} = 24 \text{ bottles/min}
$$

**(ii) Minimum Frame Rate:**

$$
FPS_{min} = \frac{v}{FOV_y} = \frac{500}{120} \approx 4.17 \text{ frames/s}
$$

To handle variations, design FPS $\approx 3 \times FPS_{min} = 12.5$ frames/s, so any FPS above 13 is safe.

**(iii) Idle Percentage at 20 FPS:**

Time taken by one frame at 20 FPS:

$$
t_{frame} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ s per frame}
$$

Inspection cycle time per bottle (from part i):

$$
t_{cycle} = \frac{\text{Pitch}}{v} = \frac{200}{500} = 0.4 \text{ s/bottle}
$$

Idle time per bottle:

$$
t_{idle} = t_{cycle} - t_{frame} = 0.4 - 0.05 = 0.35 \text{ s}
$$

Idle percentage:

$$
\%\,idle = \frac{0.35}{0.4} \times 100 = 87.5\%
$$

**Result:** The camera is idle **87.5%** of the time — useful for triggering or multi-frame averaging to improve image quality.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Confusing FOV with sensor size:** Students frequently write $FOV = \text{Sensor Size}$ — this is **wrong** unless WD = f (i.e., 1:1 magnification). The correct formula always involves WD and f.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in minimum defect size:** The Nyquist criterion requires at least **2 pixels** to define a feature. Many students compute $D_{min} = R$ instead of $D_{min} = 2R$, losing 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing units:** FOV in mm, WD in mm, focal length in mm, pixel size in µm — students often mix mm and cm without conversion. Always convert to a common unit **before** substituting.
>
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram in 7-mark questions:** A 7-mark "explain" question almost always carries **2 marks for the diagram**. Writing only text will cost you those marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing "image resolution" with "optical resolution":** A 3-mark direct question on this distinction appears frequently. Memorise the table.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Machine Vision = **Optics + Sensor + Processor + Decision + Actuator**.
- The five functional stages are: **Illumination → Acquisition → Processing → Decision → Actuation**.
- Master lens equation: $FOV = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size} \times WD}{f}$.
- Optical magnification: $M = \dfrac{f}{WD} = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size}}{FOV}$.
- Pixel resolution on object: $R = \dfrac{FOV}{N_{px}}$.
- **Minimum detectable defect = $2R$** (Nyquist criterion).
- Throughput (parts/min): $T = \dfrac{\text{Pitch} \times 60}{v}$.
- Frame rate requirement: $FPS \geq \dfrac{v}{FOV}$.
- Lighting types: **Front (bright-field)**, **Back (silhouette)**, **Dome**, **Coaxial**, **Structured**.
- Over **70% of failed vision applications** are due to **poor illumination** — always optimise lighting first.
- Camera interfaces: **USB3 Vision**, **GigE Vision**, **Camera Link**, **CoaXPress**.
- Common algorithms: **Thresholding**, **Edge detection (Canny)**, **Blob analysis**, **Template matching**, **OCR**.
- Decision outputs feed into **PLC / SCADA** via discrete I/O or industrial Ethernet.
- Always compute **camera + lens + lighting as a triplet**, not independently.
- For high-precision inspection (< 0.05 mm/pixel), use **telecentric lenses** to eliminate perspective distortion.
- Industrial-grade cameras offer **global shutter** to avoid motion blur on moving parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
