2. **Electromechanical Transduction Stage**
   The LVDT primary is excited by a **stable sine-wave oscillator** of frequency $f$ and rms voltage $V_p$. The two secondary coils are wound in *opposite phase* (series-opposing connection). The differential output is:
   $$V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$$
   where $V_{s1}$ and $V_{s2}$ are the individual secondary voltages.

3. **Signal Conditioning Stage**
   The differential AC output is amplified by an **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., AD620, INA128), then passed through a **phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)** followed by a **low-pass filter** to recover a **DC voltage proportional to displacement and signed with direction**.

4. **Data Acquisition Stage**
   The conditioned DC voltage is fed to an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — either a dedicated DAQ card (NI USB-6008/6009) or a microcontroller-based ADC (Arduino Uno's 10-bit ADC, or STM32's 12-bit ADC).

5. **Software Decision Stage**
   The PC runs a **graphical program** (LabVIEW / MATLAB / Python with PyDAQmx / pySerial) that:
   - reads digital samples at a fixed sampling rate $f_s$,
   - converts counts to engineering units (mm),
   - displays **real-time waveform** (displacement vs. time),
   - compares with the threshold and **fires an alarm** (LED, buzzer, or dialog box) when exceeded.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

- **Why series-opposing secondaries?** Because the *magnitude* of the differential voltage encodes displacement magnitude, while the **phase** (relative to the primary excitation) encodes **direction** (inward vs. outward). A single-ended output would lose directional information.
- **Why phase-sensitive demodulation?** It converts the AC signal back to DC *while preserving the sign* (direction). A simple diode rectifier would always give a positive output, losing the sign — which is unacceptable for a bi-directional slide.
- **Why oversampling with the ADC?** It improves the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** and allows digital averaging, mitigating electrical noise from the shop floor (VFDs, motors, contactors).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Differential secondary voltage | $V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2} = k \cdot V_p \cdot x$ | V | $x$ is displacement in mm, $k$ is sensitivity in V/V/mm |
| 2 | LVDT Sensitivity | $k = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_p \cdot x}$ | V/V/mm | Manufacturer-specified constant |
| 3 | Full-scale output | $V_{FS} = k \cdot V_p \cdot x_{max}$ | V | $x_{max}$ is the linear range (e.g., $\pm 5$ mm) |
| 4 | Displacement from output voltage | $x = \dfrac{V_{out}}{k \cdot V_p}$ | mm | Used by PC to display engineering value |
| 5 | ADC counts to voltage | $V_{ADC} = \dfrac{N \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n}}$ | V | $N$ is digital code, $n$ is ADC bit resolution, $V_{ref}$ is reference |
| 6 | Engineering value from counts | $x = \dfrac{N}{2^{n}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ref}}{k \cdot V_p}$ | mm | Final equation used in PC code |
| 7 | Sampling rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency content in displacement signal |
| 8 | Alarm condition (boolean) | $ALARM = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert x \vert > x_{limit} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | — | Implementation in software (use $\vert$ for absolute value to avoid breaking tables) |
| 9 | Instrument amp gain | $G = 1 + \dfrac{49.4\ \text{k}\Omega}{R_G}$ | — | For AD620 instrumentation amplifier |
| 10 | Phase-sensitive detector output | $V_{PSD} = V_{cond} \cdot \cos(\phi)$ | V | $\phi = 0°$ gives full positive output; $\phi = 180°$ gives negative output |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lathe carriage position feedback** — auto-compensation for thermal expansion.
- **CNC machine slide position verification** — closed-loop verification against CNC command.
- **Press-fit machines** — monitoring punch travel for quality control.
- **Damper testing rigs** — sub-millimetre deflection measurement.
- **Bridge expansion joint monitoring** — long-stroke (up to 600 mm) LVDTs in civil engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the LVDT Differential Output Equation

The LVDT consists of one **primary coil** (P) with $N_p$ turns and two **secondary coils** (S1 and S2) with $N_s$ turns each, wound on a common cylindrical former. A movable ferromagnetic core of length $\ell_c$ is positioned such that it overlaps the secondaries by distances $x_1$ and $x_2$ respectively.

**Step 1 — Mutual Inductance of Each Secondary**
The mutual inductance between primary and each secondary is proportional to the portion of the core coupled with that secondary.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= k_m \cdot N_p \cdot N_s \cdot x_1 \\
M_2 &= k_m \cdot N_p \cdot N_s \cdot x_2
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k_m$ is a geometric coupling constant that depends on core permeability, coil pitch, and bobbin geometry.

**Step 2 — Induced EMF in Each Secondary (Faraday's Law)**
The primary is driven by $v_p(t) = V_p \sqrt{2} \sin(\omega t)$. The induced voltages in the secondaries are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{s1}(t) &= -M_1 \dfrac{d i_p}{d t} = M_1 \cdot j\omega I_p \quad \text{(phasor form)} \\
v_{s2}(t) &= -M_2 \dfrac{d i_p}{d t} = M_2 \cdot j\omega I_p
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Series-Opposing Connection**
The two secondaries are connected in series with **opposite polarity** (this is the *differential* connection). Hence the net output is:

$$
V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2} = j\omega I_p (M_1 - M_2)
$$

**Step 4 — Null Position**
At the **null (central) position**, $x_1 = x_2$, so $M_1 = M_2$ and $V_{out} = 0$. This is the **electrical zero**.

**Step 5 — Off-Center Displacement**
Let the core move a distance $x$ from null. Then $x_1 = x_0 + x$ and $x_2 = x_0 - x$, where $x_0$ is the half-overlap at null. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 - M_2 &= k_m N_p N_s (x_1 - x_2) \\
          &= k_m N_p N_s \cdot 2x
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the magnitude of the differential output is:

$$
V_{out} = 2 \, k_m \, N_p \, N_s \, \omega I_p \, x
$$

**Step 6 — Definition of Sensitivity**
Grouping the constants (which depend on the specific LVDT) into a single constant $k$:

$$
V_{out} = k \cdot V_p \cdot x
$$

This is the **fundamental LVDT equation** that the PC ultimately inverts to obtain displacement.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Engineering-Value Conversion (ADC Counts → mm)

Let the LVDT be excited by $V_p$ volts rms, with sensitivity $k$ (V/V/mm), and the ADC have $n$ bits with reference $V_{ref}$.

**Step 1 — Generate analog voltage proportional to displacement**
The signal conditioner (instrumentation amp + PSD + filter) produces:

$$
V_{DC} = G_{amp} \cdot k \cdot V_p \cdot x
$$

where $G_{amp}$ is the gain set on the instrumentation amplifier.

**Step 2 — Digitize using ADC**
The ADC produces a digital code $N$ given by:

$$
N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{DC}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^{n} \right\rfloor
$$

For an ideal ADC (no quantisation error):

$$
N = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $x$**
Substituting the expression for $V_{DC}$ and solving for $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{G_{amp} \cdot k \cdot V_p \cdot x}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^{n} \\
\Rightarrow \quad x &= \dfrac{N \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n} \cdot G_{amp} \cdot k \cdot V_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **calibration equation** that the PC must implement (often via two-point calibration: zero and known reference).

### 3.3 Alarm Logic Derivation

The PC reads displacement at each sampling instant $t_k = k \cdot T_s$, where $T_s = 1/f_s$. Define:

$$
x[k] = \dfrac{N[k] \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n} \cdot G_{amp} \cdot k \cdot V_p}
$$

The alarm is a **latched boolean** state:

$$
A[k] = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \vert x[k] \vert > x_{limit} \text{ (alarm condition)} \\
0, & \text{otherwise (normal)}
\end{cases}
$$

> **Engineer's Note:** A simple latching logic $A[k] = A[k-1] \lor A_{instant}$ is often preferred in industrial systems so that a brief transient spike does not silence the alarm.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Measurement and Alarm System

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **complete functional architecture** from physical slide motion to PC-based alarm decision. Per the KTU protocol, all node IDs are alphanumeric and labels contain only clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    slide1[MACHINE SLIDE MECHANICAL INPUT] --> core1[LVDT FERROMAGNETIC CORE]
    core1 --> lvdtx1[LVDT PRIMARY AND SECONDARY COILS]
    osc1[SINE WAVE OSCILLATOR 5 KHZ 5 VRMS] --> lvdtx1
    lvdtx1 --> amp1[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER GAIN G]
    amp1 --> psd1[PHASE SENSITIVE DEMODULATOR]
    osc1 --> psd1
    psd1 --> lpf1[LOW PASS FILTER FC 100 HZ]
    lpf1 --> adc1[ADC 12 BIT OR 16 BIT]
    adc1 --> pc1[PC WITH LABVIEW MATLAB OR PYTHON SOFTWARE]
    pc1 --> disp1[REAL TIME DISPLACEMENT DISPLAY MM]
    pc1 --> plot1[GRAPHICAL WAVEFORM PLOT DISPLACEMENT VS TIME]
    pc1 --> cmp1[COMPARATOR WITH THRESHOLD XLIMIT]
    cmp1 --> led1[RED LED ALARM INDICATOR]
    cmp1 --> buzz1[BUZZER 5V DC]
    cmp1 --> msg1[PC SCREEN ALERT MESSAGE]
```

### 4.2 Hardware Interfacing Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSING_MODULE
        m1[LVDT BODY]:::sensor
        m2[CORE ROD ATTACHED TO SLIDE]:::mech
    end
    subgraph SIGNAL_CONDITIONER_BOARD
        s1[OSCILLATOR IC 8038 OR XR2206]:::gen
        s2[INSTRUMENTATION AMP AD620]:::amp
        s3[PHASE SENSITIVE DETECTOR AD630]:::psd
        s4[ACTIVE LPF 2ND ORDER BUTTERWORTH]:::filt
        s5[DC BUFFER OP07]:::buf
    end
    subgraph DAQ_AND_PC
        d1[DAQ CARD NI USB 6009 OR ARDUINO UNO]:::daq
        d2[PERSONAL COMPUTER WITH SOFTWARE]:::pc
    end
    subgraph ALARM_OUTPUT
        a1[BUZZER 5V]:::alm
        a2[LED INDICATOR RED]:::alm
        a3[PC DIALOG BOX ALERT]:::alm
    end
    m1 --> m2
    m1 --> s1
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s1 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> d1
    d1 --> d2
    d2 --> a1
    d2 --> a2
    d2 --> a3

    classDef sensor fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000
    classDef gen fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef psd fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef filt fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000
    classDef buf fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000
    classDef daq fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef pc fill:#0d6efd,stroke:#ffffff,color:#000
    classDef alm fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Software Flow Chart on the PC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([START PROGRAM]) --> init1[INITIALISE DAQ OR SERIAL PORT]
    init1 --> setlim1[SET THRESHOLD XLIMIT FROM USER]
    setlim1 --> setcal1[SET CALIBRATION CONSTANTS K AND VP]
    setcal1 --> read1[READ ADC COUNT N FROM CHANNEL]
    read1 --> conv1[CONVERT N TO DISPLACEMENT X IN MM]
    conv1 --> disp2[DISPLAY X ON SCREEN AND ON WAVEFORM]
    disp2 --> comp1{IS ABS X GREATER THAN XLIMIT}
    comp1 -- YES --> alarm1[ACTIVATE LED BUZZER AND ALERT DIALOG]
    comp1 -- NO --> clear1[DEACTIVATE ALARM OUTPUTS]
    alarm1 --> delay1[WAIT FOR SAMPLING PERIOD TS]
    clear1 --> delay1
    delay1 --> read1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an LVDT. Mention any two advantages over a potentiometric displacement sensor.*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **Construction:** Hollow cylindrical former; one primary (P) wound centrally; two secondaries (S1, S2) wound on either side, connected in **series-opposing (differential)** fashion. A movable **ferromagnetic core** slides inside the former.
- **Working:** Primary excited with AC voltage. When core is at **null (centre)**, $V_{s1} = V_{s2}$, so $V_{out} = 0$. When core moves towards S1, $V_{s1} > V_{s2}$, producing a net output voltage **in phase** with primary. When core moves towards S2, output is **180° out of phase**. Magnitude $\propto$ displacement; phase $\propto$ direction.
- **Advantages over potentiometer:** (1) **Frictionless / non-contact** (no wear, infinite life). (2) **Infinite resolution**. (3) **High reliability** under vibration, dust, oil. (4) **Bidirectional** measurement with single sensor.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is a phase-sensitive demodulator? Why is it required in an LVDT signal conditioning chain?*

**Model Answer:**
- A **phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)** is a circuit (commonly built using the **AD630** IC or an **analog multiplier** followed by a low-pass filter) that multiplies the LVDT output by a reference square wave (or sine wave) of the **same frequency and phase** as the primary excitation.
- It is required because the LVDT output is **AC**, while a PC/ADC needs a **DC voltage** proportional to displacement. A simple diode rectifier would lose the **directional sign**. The PSD converts AC to DC **while preserving the sign** (positive for one direction, negative for the other), enabling **bi-directional displacement measurement**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the interfacing of an LVDT with a PC for monitoring the displacement of a machine tool slide. Explain the function of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design the alarm logic. Given an LVDT with sensitivity $k = 2.2\ \text{mV/V/mm}$, excitation $V_p = 5\ \text{V rms}$, a signal conditioner gain $G_{amp} = 50$, an ADC with $n = 12$ bits and $V_{ref} = 5\ \text{V}$, calculate the displacement $x$ (in mm) when the ADC returns a count of $N = 2048$. If the threshold is $x_{limit} = 2.0\ \text{mm}$, determine whether the alarm should activate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
The block diagram has been presented in SECTION 4 above. The student should describe:

1. **Machine slide** mechanically coupled to the **LVDT core** via a non-magnetic rod.
2. **LVDT body** producing a differential AC output $V_{out} = k \cdot V_p \cdot x$.
3. **Signal conditioner board** containing: (i) a **sine-wave oscillator** (e.g., XR2206 / 8038 / Wien bridge using op-amp) at 5 kHz providing $V_p = 5\ \text{V rms}$ to the primary; (ii) an **instrumentation amplifier** (AD620) for differential amplification with CMRR > 100 dB; (iii) a **phase-sensitive demodulator** (AD630 multiplier + reference from oscillator) followed by a **2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter** with $f_c \approx 100$ Hz to extract a clean DC voltage; (iv) a **DC buffer** to drive the ADC.
4. **DAQ card / microcontroller-based ADC** (NI USB-6009 / Arduino Uno) digitising the DC voltage into 12-bit codes.
5. **PC software** (LabVIEW VI / MATLAB GUI / Python with PyQt) that: (a) reads ADC counts at a configurable $f_s$ (e.g., 100 Hz); (b) converts counts to mm using the calibration equation; (c) plots a real-time waveform; (d) compares with $x_{limit}$; (e) drives the **alarm outputs** (LED, buzzer, dialog).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Recall the calibration equation derived in SECTION 3.2:**

$$
x = \dfrac{N \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n} \cdot G_{amp} \cdot k \cdot V_p}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

Note: $k = 2.2\ \text{mV/V/mm} = 2.2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V/V/mm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= \dfrac{2048 \times 5\ \text{V}}{2^{12} \times 50 \times (2.2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V/V/mm}) \times 5\ \text{V}} \\
  &= \dfrac{10240}{4096 \times 50 \times 0.011} \\
  &= \dfrac{10240}{2252.8} \\
  &\approx 4.546\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the alarm condition.**

Since $\vert x \vert = 4.546\ \text{mm} > x_{limit} = 2.0\ \text{mm}$, the **alarm is ACTIVE (ON)**.

**Step 4 — Engineering comment:** The displacement reading exceeds the safe limit by 2.546 mm, which on a typical lathe corresponds to over-travel that could damage the tool or workpiece. The PC should immediately trigger the buzzer and stop the slide (via a relay-controlled contactor in an enhanced industrial system).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]**
**(a)** Draw the equivalent circuit of an LVDT and derive the expression for its differential output voltage in terms of displacement. Explain the role of the series-opposing connection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A machine slide has a linear range of $\pm 10$ mm. An LVDT with sensitivity $k = 1.5\ \text{mV/V/mm}$ is excited by $V_p = 6\ \text{V rms}$. The signal conditioner has a total gain (amplifier + PSD) of $G = 80$. Calculate: (i) the full-scale differential output voltage; (ii) the full-scale DC voltage at the ADC input; (iii) the minimum ADC resolution (in bits) required to resolve a displacement of $0.1$ mm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The LVDT equivalent circuit treats the primary as an inductor $L_p$ with resistance $R_p$, and each secondary as a mutually-coupled inductor $L_{s1}$ and $L_{s2}$ with resistances $R_{s1}$ and $R_{s2}$. The mutual coupling depends on the core position. Working through the derivation (as in SECTION 3.1), we obtain:

$$
V_{out} = 2 \, k_m \, N_p \, N_s \, \omega I_p \, x = k \cdot V_p \cdot x
$$

**Role of series-opposing connection:** The two secondaries are connected so that their EMFs subtract. At the **null** position, equal and opposite EMFs cancel exactly, giving **$V_{out} = 0$** — this is the electrical zero and acts as a stable reference. Off-null, the difference carries both **magnitude** and **directional (phase) information**, enabling bi-directional measurement.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Full-scale differential output voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{FS} &= k \cdot V_p \cdot x_{max} \\
       &= (1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V/V/mm}) \times 6\ \text{V} \times 10\ \text{mm} \\
       &= 0.090\ \text{V} = 90\ \text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Full-scale DC voltage at ADC input:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{DC,FS} &= G \cdot V_{FS} = 80 \times 90\ \text{mV} = 7200\ \text{mV} = 7.2\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** This is *larger* than a typical 5 V ADC reference. In practice, either $G$ must be reduced, or the ADC reference increased to 10 V (or auto-ranging used).

**(iii) Minimum ADC resolution to resolve 0.1 mm:**

A displacement of $\Delta x = 0.1$ mm corresponds to a DC voltage step of:

$$
\Delta V = G \cdot k \cdot V_p \cdot \Delta x = 80 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 6 \times 0.1 = 0.072\ \text{V} = 72\ \text{mV}
$$

For the ADC to "see" this as at least **1 LSB**, the LSB must be $\leq 72$ mV. With $V_{ref} = 5$ V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} &\leq 72\ \text{mV} \\
2^{n} &\geq \dfrac{5\ \text{V}}{0.072\ \text{V}} \approx 69.44 \\
n &\geq \log_2(69.44) \approx 6.12
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **minimum integer bit resolution is $n = 7$ bits**. In practice, an **8-bit or 10-bit** ADC is used to provide headroom against noise and non-idealities.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in sensitivity:** Forgetting to convert $k$ from mV/V/mm to V/V/mm is the **#1 mistake**. KTU examiners *specifically* test this.
> 2. **Phase of output:** Students often state that LVDT output magnitude is enough to determine displacement, forgetting the **phase encodes direction** — a 2-mark deduction in viva.
> 3. **Sign of displacement:** In the alarm logic, writing $x > x_{limit}$ instead of $\vert x \vert > x_{limit}$ loses 1 mark, because a *negative* over-travel is equally dangerous.
> 4. **Confusing full-scale range with total range:** An LVDT specified for $\pm 10$ mm has a **full-scale (peak) displacement of 10 mm**, not 20 mm. The total stroke is 20 mm.
> 5. **Forgetting LSB sign in ADC reading:** The 12-bit ADC on Arduino is *unipolar* (0 to $V_{ref}$), so a *bi-directional* LVDT signal must be **level-shifted** (e.g., biased to 2.5 V using a summing junction) before digitisation. Many students miss this in circuit design questions.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **LVDT = Linear Variable Differential Transformer** — passive, contactless, AC-excited inductive displacement sensor.
- **Core in null position → $V_{out} = 0$**; **core off-null → magnitude ∝ $x$, phase ∝ direction**.
- **Series-opposing (differential) secondaries** is what makes it a *differential* transformer.
- **Key formula (memorise):** $V_{out} = k \cdot V_p \cdot x$.
- **Signal chain:** LVDT → Instrumentation Amp → Phase-Sensitive Demodulator → LPF → ADC → PC.
- **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (AD630)** is mandatory for bi-directional measurement; a simple diode rectifier loses the sign.
- **Calibration equation (PC-side):** $x = \dfrac{N \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n} \cdot G_{amp} \cdot k \cdot V_p}$.
- **Alarm condition (use absolute value):** $ALARM = 1$ if $\vert x \vert > x_{limit}$, else $0$.
- **Sampling rate rule:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ (Nyquist); for slow machine-slide motion, $f_s = 50$–$100$ Hz is typical.
- **Typical industrial values:** $V_p = 5\ \text{V rms}$, $f_{exc} = 5\ \text{kHz}$, $k \approx 1$–$3\ \text{mV/V/mm}$, $n = 12$ bits, $V_{ref} = 5\ \text{V}$.
- **Applications:** Lathe carriage, CNC axis, press-fit, damper test, bridge expansion joint.
- **Advantages:** Frictionless, infinite resolution, rugged, bi-directional, high linearity ($\pm 0.25\%$ FS).
- **Limitations:** Requires AC excitation, limited stroke (typically up to $\pm 600$ mm for very long LVDTs), sensitive to external magnetic fields.
- **Viva-favourite question:** *"Why not use a simple diode rectifier after the LVDT?"* — Answer: It loses directional information; PSD is required.
- **Viva-favourite question:** *"What is the role of the low-pass filter after the PSD?"* — Answer: To remove the $2f_{exc}$ ripple component, leaving only the DC component proportional to displacement.
- **Safety note for labs:** Always *mechanically* verify zero and full-scale before trusting PC readings. A software-only calibration without mechanical reference is a common KTU record-killing mistake.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of speed, direction and number of revolutions of a stepper motor using a PC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Control of speed, direction and number of revolutions of a stepper motor using a PC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **stepper motor** is a brushless, synchronous electric motor that converts a train of digital input pulses into discrete mechanical shaft rotations, where each pulse advances the rotor by a precisely fixed angular increment known as the **step angle (α)**. Because the rotor locks magnetically into discrete angular positions dictated by the energised stator windings, the device operates as a true **open-loop incremental actuator** — no shaft encoder or position feedback is required to know or hold its angle.

When a **Personal Computer (PC)** is used as the command source, control pulses are generated in software, routed through a hardware interface (parallel printer port / USB-to-parallel converter / microcontroller with serial link), amplified by a **driver IC** (e.g., ULN2003, L293D, L298N, DRV8825), and delivered to the motor phase windings in the correct sequence. The PC therefore becomes the *programmable pulse generator and sequencer* that dictates:

1. **Speed** — by varying the **pulse frequency (f)**
2. **Direction** — by reversing the **phase excitation sequence**
3. **Number of revolutions** — by delivering an exact **count of pulses**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The lab experiment verifies these three independent control variables *simultaneously* using a PC, interface card, driver, and a standard 4-phase unipolar or bipolar stepper motor (commonly 1.8°/step, 200 steps/revolution).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase Wheel" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a watch's second hand that does *not* glide smoothly but **clicks** from one tick mark to the next, with audible and visible pauses in between. Now imagine you control the watch from your laptop — you can make the hand click **faster** (speed), **backwards** (direction), or stop it after exactly **ten full circles** (number of revolutions). The stepper motor behaves exactly like this: it never rotates continuously; it only ever *steps* from one locked position to the next, and your PC is the metronome that decides when each step occurs and in which direction.

A continuous DC motor is like a *smoothly rotating bicycle wheel*. A stepper motor is like a *Ferris wheel* that always locks onto the next tooth before the operator can release the brake. This discrete, lockable nature is what makes steppers indispensable in **3-D printers, CNC routers, pick-and-place machines, and camera-focus mechanisms**.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Performance Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Standard Lab Motor) |
|---|---|---|
| Step angle | α | **1.8°** per step |
| Steps per revolution | N | **200** |
| Holding torque | T_h | **0.4 – 5 N·m** (lab grade) |
| Rated phase voltage | V_ph | **5 V – 12 V DC** |
| Rated phase current | I_ph | **0.5 A – 2 A** |
| Step accuracy | ε | **±5 % non-cumulative** |
| Maximum pulse rate | f_max | **1 kHz – 20 kHz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The step angle and the pulse frequency are the *only two* software variables a PC must manipulate to control all three output behaviours (speed, direction, revolutions). Understanding this one-to-one mapping is the conceptual key to the entire experiment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Electromagnetic Detent

A stepper motor contains a **stator** with salient poles wound by phase coils (typically four phases labelled **A, B, C, D**) and a **rotor** that is either a permanent magnet (PM stepper) or a soft-iron salient structure (variable reluctance / hybrid stepper). When a particular phase (or pair of phases) is energised, the rotor aligns itself to minimise the magnetic reluctance of the flux path. By switching the energised phase in a defined cyclic order, the rotor is dragged through successive equilibrium positions — these are the *steps*.

## 2.2 Phase Excitation Modes

There are three canonical excitation strategies used to drive a 4-phase stepper:

1. **Wave Drive (1-phase-on):** Only one phase is energised at any instant.
   Sequence → **A → B → C → D → A …**
   Steps per revolution = **N** (same as full step count), holding torque is lowest.

2. **Full-Step Drive (2-phase-on):** Two adjacent phases are always energised simultaneously.
   Sequence → **AB → BC → CD → DA → AB …**
   Steps per revolution = **N**, but holding torque is **≈ √2 times** the wave-drive torque because the rotor sits midway between two pole alignments.

3. **Half-Step Drive (1-2-phase alternation):** Alternates between single-phase and two-phase states.
   Sequence → **A → AB → B → BC → C → CD → D → DA → A …**
   Effective steps per revolution = **2N = 400** for a 1.8° motor, doubling the angular resolution at the cost of torque ripple.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU lab experiments typically use **Full-Step (2-phase-on)** mode because it gives the strongest holding torque and the cleanest 4-state logic for teaching. Speed, direction, and revolution-count derivations below all use this 4-state model unless explicitly stated otherwise.

## 2.3 The Three Control Variables — Formal Relationships

### (a) Speed Control — Pulse Frequency to RPM

Each output pulse from the PC advances the shaft by exactly **one step angle α**. If the PC delivers **f** pulses per second, then the shaft executes **f** steps per second. Converting steps per second to revolutions per minute:

$$
\omega_{\text{RPM}} \;=\; \frac{\text{steps per second}}{\text{steps per revolution}} \;\times\; 60
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_{\text{RPM}} \;=\; \frac{60 \, f}{N}\;}
$$

where:
- $\omega_{\text{RPM}}$ = rotational speed in revolutions per minute
- $f$ = pulse frequency in **Hz** (pulses per second)
- $N$ = total steps per revolution of the motor

So *speed is a linear function of pulse frequency*. Doubling the pulse rate exactly doubles the RPM — this is the fundamental programmable lever the PC exploits.

### (b) Direction Control — Phase Sequence Reversal

Direction is dictated purely by the **order** in which the four phase lines (A, B, C, D) are toggled HIGH/LOW. The PC's software simply writes a 4-bit binary pattern to the parallel port's data pins (e.g., D0–D3):

| Step # | CW Rotation (Forward) | CCW Rotation (Reverse) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **1010** (A, C ON) | **1010** (A, C ON) |
| 2 | **0110** (B, C ON) | **1001** (A, D ON) |
| 3 | **0101** (B, D ON) | **0101** (B, D ON) |
| 4 | **1001** (A, D ON) | **0110** (B, C ON) |
| → repeat | … | … |

Reading down the **CW** column gives the forward bitstream; reading down the **CCW** column gives the reversed bitstream. The PC alternates between these two tables to flip the rotation sense at any instant.

### (c) Number of Revolutions — Step Count Accumulation

If the rotor must turn through **R** complete revolutions, the PC must deliver exactly:

$$
\boxed{\;n_{\text{steps}} \;=\; R \times N\;}
$$

For a **1.8°** motor (*N* = 200), one revolution requires **200** pulses, two revolutions require **400** pulses, and so on. A software counter (or a hardware timer interrupt) decrements from $n_{\text{steps}}$ down to zero; when it reaches zero the pulse stream is halted and the rotor stops on its last locked step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why stepper motors are called *open-loop positioners*: the absolute shaft angle is **always** equal to (steps delivered × α), with no encoder needed. The position accuracy is limited only by the step-angle tolerance of the motor itself.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | α = step angle; N = steps/rev | Sizing motor for required resolution |
| 2 | $\omega_{\text{RPM}} = \dfrac{60\,f}{N}$ | ω = speed; f = pulse frequency | Converting software timer values to mechanical RPM |
| 3 | $n_{\text{steps}} = R \times N$ | n = pulse count; R = revolutions | Determining required loop iterations in code |
| 4 | $T_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{R \times N}{f}$ | T = total motion time in seconds | Predicting cycle time of a positioning task |
| 5 | $f_{\min} \le f \le f_{\max}$ | f_min ≈ 10 Hz; f_max depends on motor inertia & driver | Selecting safe operating pulse range |
| 6 | $\theta_{\text{actual}} = n_{\text{steps}} \times \alpha$ | θ = mechanical angle in degrees | Verifying shaft position in observation table |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU valuation trap is to confuse **steps per revolution (N)** with **steps per electrical cycle**. For a 4-phase full-step drive they are numerically equal, but in half-step drive $N$ must be replaced by $2N$ everywhere. Always read the question stem carefully.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

PC-controlled stepper systems appear in every modern automated machine:

- **3-D printer axes** (X, Y, Z, extruder) — speed × step count = filament placement accuracy.
- **CNC milling machines** — synchronising tool feed (steps/sec) with spindle RPM.
- **Automated blood-analyzers** — a stepper drives the rotary sample tray by an exact R revolutions to align a cuvette with the optical sensor.
- **Camera pan-tilt heads in surveillance** — direction toggled in software; revolution count gives scan-angle limits.

The lab experiment therefore trains you in the *exact same algorithmic pipeline* used in industry: **PC → driver → stepper → controlled mechanical output**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation — Pulse Frequency from Desired RPM

**Goal:** Given a desired mechanical speed $\omega_{\text{RPM}}$ and a motor with known step count $N$, find the pulse frequency $f$ that the PC must produce.

### Derivation

By definition of rotational speed in revolutions per second:

$$
\omega_{\text{rps}} \;=\; \frac{\omega_{\text{RPM}}}{60}
$$

Each revolution of the shaft requires exactly **N** discrete steps. Therefore the steps per second (= pulse frequency) is:

$$
f \;=\; N \cdot \omega_{\text{rps}}
$$

Substituting $\omega_{\text{rps}} = \omega_{\text{RPM}} / 60$:

$$
f \;=\; N \cdot \frac{\omega_{\text{RPM}}}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f \;=\; \frac{N \cdot \omega_{\text{RPM}}}{60}\;}
$$

This is simply the algebraic rearrangement of the boxed formula in §2.3(a). Both forms are required depending on whether the question gives you *f* and asks for *RPM*, or vice versa.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Computing Pulse Frequency for a Given RPM

> **Problem (typical KTU numerical):** A **1.8° per step** stepper motor is to be driven from a PC through a parallel port. The driver requires a TTL-compatible pulse train. Calculate the pulse frequency needed to obtain a shaft speed of **120 RPM**. Also calculate the time delay that must be programmed between consecutive pulses.

### Given
- Step angle α = 1.8° → Steps per revolution $N = 360° / 1.8° = 200$
- Desired speed $\omega_{\text{RPM}} = 120$ RPM

### Step 1 — Convert RPM to revolutions per second

$$
\omega_{\text{rps}} \;=\; \frac{120}{60} \;=\; 2 \;\text{rev/s}
$$

**Logic:** 120 revolutions in 60 s means exactly 2 revolutions each second.

### Step 2 — Compute pulse frequency

$$
f \;=\; N \cdot \omega_{\text{rps}} \;=\; 200 \times 2 \;=\; 400 \;\text{Hz}
$$

**Logic:** Each revolution needs 200 pulses, so 2 revolutions per second need 2 × 200 = 400 pulses per second.

### Step 3 — Compute the software delay between pulses

$$
T_{\text{delay}} \;=\; \frac{1}{f} \;=\; \frac{1}{400} \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s} \;=\; 2.5\;\text{ms}
$$

**Logic:** A 400 Hz waveform has a period of 1/400 s = 2.5 ms; the PC must toggle the data line, wait 2.5 ms, toggle again.

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\;f = 400 \text{ Hz}, \quad T_{\text{delay}} = 2.5 \text{ ms}\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Pulse Count for a Specified Number of Revolutions

> **Problem:** Using the same 1.8° motor, how many pulses must the PC deliver to rotate the shaft through exactly **7.5 revolutions** in the forward direction? What total mechanical angle does this correspond to?

### Step 1 — Compute pulses per revolution

$$
N \;=\; \frac{360°}{1.8°} \;=\; 200 \;\text{steps/rev}
$$

**Logic:** A full circle is 360°; each step sweeps 1.8°; hence 200 steps fill one revolution.

### Step 2 — Multiply by required revolutions

$$
n_{\text{steps}} \;=\; R \times N \;=\; 7.5 \times 200 \;=\; 1500 \;\text{pulses}
$$

**Logic:** Each revolution requires 200 pulses; 7.5 revolutions therefore require 7.5 × 200.

### Step 3 — Equivalent mechanical angle

$$
\theta_{\text{actual}} \;=\; n_{\text{steps}} \times \alpha \;=\; 1500 \times 1.8° \;=\; 2700°
$$

Cross-check: $7.5 \times 360° = 2700°$ ✓ (sanity check passed).

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\;n_{\text{steps}} = 1500, \quad \theta_{\text{actual}} = 2700°\;}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Direction-Reversal Pulse Train

> **Problem:** Draw the first six pulse-pattern words the PC must write to its parallel-port data lines (D3 D2 D1 D0) for the **forward** direction, and then the next six for the **reverse** direction, using the **Full-Step (2-phase-on)** excitation table.

### Forward (CW) sequence (from §2.3(b) table)

| Pulse # | D3 D2 D1 D0 | Active Phases |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1010 | A & C |
| 2 | 0110 | B & C |
| 3 | 0101 | B & D |
| 4 | 1001 | A & D |
| 5 | 1010 | A & C |
| 6 | 0110 | B & C |

### Reverse (CCW) sequence — table read **upward**

| Pulse # | D3 D2 D1 D0 | Active Phases |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1010 | A & C |
| 2 | 1001 | A & D |
| 3 | 0101 | B & D |
| 4 | 0110 | B & C |
| 5 | 1010 | A & C |
| 6 | 1001 | A & D |

**Logic:** To reverse, we move along the same 4-state ring but in the opposite direction. The 4-bit word 1010 acts as the *anchor*; everything else mirrors across the vertical centreline of the table.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Total Motion Time

> **Problem:** With reference to Example 3.2 (f = 400 Hz), calculate the total time taken to complete the **1500 pulses** of Example 3.3. Comment on whether the rotor can keep up (assume the motor's maximum start-stop pulse rate is 500 Hz).

### Step 1 — Total time formula

$$
T_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{n_{\text{steps}}}{f} \;=\; \frac{1500}{400}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} \;=\; 3.75 \;\text{s}
$$

**Logic:** 1500 pulses at 400 per second take 1500/400 = 3.75 seconds.

### Step 2 — Acceleration check

The chosen f = 400 Hz is **below** the 500 Hz start-stop limit, so the rotor will faithfully track every pulse without losing steps. **Result: motion is safe and accurate.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *lost step* — caused by demanding a pulse rate faster than the rotor's mechanical ability to settle — silently corrupts the position count. This is the single most common failure mode in PC-controlled stepper systems and is the reason the experiment specifies both a *minimum* and a *maximum* test frequency.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Control Variable → Software Action Mapping

| Desired Output Change | Physical Variable Changed | PC Software Action | Hardware Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase speed | f ↑ | Decrease inter-pulse delay in timer | Pulses arrive faster → shaft spins faster |
| Decrease speed | f ↓ | Increase inter-pulse delay | Pulses arrive slower → shaft slows |
| Reverse direction | Phase order reversed | Walk CCW table instead of CW table | Stator flux rotates opposite way |
| Stop after R revolutions | Pulse count = R × N | Decrement counter; halt pulse train at zero | Shaft locks at final step |
| Smooth micro-stepping | Subdivide one full step into m micro-pulses | Sine-weighted PWM on phase currents | Finer angular resolution, lower vibration |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PC to Stepper Motor

The diagram below shows the complete signal flow from the application running on the PC down to the mechanical rotation of the motor shaft.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PC Application Program] -->|4-bit binary pattern| B[Parallel Port Data Register]
    B -->|TTL voltage levels 0V or 5V| C[Buffer or Opto-Isolator]
    C -->|Logic level signals| D[Stepper Motor Driver IC]
    D -->|High-current phase pulses| E[4-Phase Stepper Motor]
    E -->|Mechanical rotation| F[Shaft Output]
    G[DC Power Supply 5V to 12V] --> D
    H[Crystal Clock or Software Delay Loop] --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **PC** is the brain — it generates both the 4-bit *pattern* (direction) and the *timing* (speed).
- The **parallel port / USB-to-parallel converter** physically transports the 4 bits from the PC bus to the external connector.
- The **buffer / opto-isolator** protects the PC from voltage spikes reflected from the inductive motor windings.
- The **driver IC** (ULN2003 / L293D) amplifies the PC's logic-level signals to the higher current levels demanded by the stator coils.
- The **DC power supply** energises the driver; the **clock source** enforces the pulse cadence.

## 4.2 Software Flowchart — Control Algorithm

The PC-side program executes the loop below for every experiment run.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Program] --> A1[Initialise Parallel Port as Output]
    A1 --> A2[Accept User Input: Direction, RPM, Revolutions]
    A2 --> A3{Select Direction}
    A3 -->|CW| A4[Load Forward Sequence Table]
    A3 -->|CCW| A5[Load Reverse Sequence Table]
    A4 --> A6[Compute Pulse Frequency f from RPM]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Compute Total Pulse Count n = R times N]
    A7 --> A8[Set Pulse Counter = n]
    A8 --> A9[Send Next 4-bit Word to Data Port]
    A9 --> A10[Wait T_delay = 1 per f seconds]
    A10 --> A11[Decrement Pulse Counter]
    A11 --> A12{Counter equals Zero?}
    A12 -->|No| A9
    A12 -->|Yes| A13[Hold Final Step Energised]
    A13 --> A14[Stop Program or Prompt New Run]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The single inner loop **A9 → A10 → A11 → A12** is the heart of the experiment. Every other block is a one-time setup. KTU examiners frequently ask students to redraw or describe this inner loop in the viva — memorise it.

## 4.3 Phase Excitation State Diagram

The 4-state ring traversed by the driver logic in Full-Step mode is shown below. Each arrow represents one pulse from the PC.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> state1
    state1 : State 1: Phases A and C ON  1010
    state2 : State 2: Phases B and C ON  0110
    state3 : State 3: Phases B and D ON  0101
    state4 : State 4: Phases A and D ON  1001
    state1 --> state2 : Forward CW
    state2 --> state3 : Forward CW
    state3 --> state4 : Forward CW
    state4 --> state1 : Forward CW
    state1 --> state4 : Reverse CCW
    state4 --> state3 : Reverse CCW
    state3 --> state2 : Reverse CCW
    state2 --> state1 : Reverse CCW
```

## 4.4 Hardware Interfacing Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PC_BUS [PC Parallel Port]
        pinD0[D0 Pin 2]
        pinD1[D1 Pin 3]
        pinD2[D2 Pin 4]
        pinD3[D3 Pin 5]
        pinGND[GND Pin 18]
    end
    subgraph DRIVER [ULN2003 Driver IC]
        inp1[IN1]
        inp2[IN2]
        inp3[IN3]
        inp4[IN4]
        out1[OUT1]
        out2[OUT2]
        out3[OUT3]
        out4[OUT4]
        com[COM tied to V+ 12V]
    end
    subgraph MOTOR [Stepper Motor Coils]
        coilA[Coil A]
        coilB[Coil B]
        coilC[Coil C]
        coilD[Coil D]
    end
    pinD0 --> inp1 --> out1 --> coilA
    pinD1 --> inp2 --> out2 --> coilB
    pinD2 --> inp3 --> out3 --> coilC
    pinD3 --> inp4 --> out4 --> coilD
    pinGND --> DRIVER
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the **COM pin** of the ULN2003 — it must be tied to the motor's supply voltage (+12 V). This is the freewheeling-diode common rail that protects the IC from the inductive kick of the de-energising coil. Forgetting this connection is the #1 wiring mistake in the lab.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define a stepper motor. Why is it classified as an *open-loop* actuator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A stepper motor is a brushless synchronous electric motor that rotates in discrete angular increments called *steps*, with each step being a fixed fraction of one full revolution determined by the motor construction (typically 1.8° per step). It is classified as an **open-loop** actuator because the absolute shaft position can be deduced *purely from the count of input pulses* — no shaft encoder, resolver, or position-feedback sensor is required. The rotor magnetically locks to the energised stator poles, so once a pulse is sent the shaft *must* have moved by exactly one step angle. This pulse-counting principle gives accurate positioning without feedback.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]**
**List the three parameters of a stepper motor that can be controlled by a PC and state which software variable controls each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Controlled Parameter | Software Variable That Sets It |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Speed** (RPM) | **Pulse frequency f** (Hz) — higher f → faster rotation |
| 2 | **Direction** (CW / CCW) | **Order of the 4-bit phase excitation pattern** |
| 3 | **Number of revolutions** | **Total pulse count n** = revolutions × steps-per-revolution |

The PC therefore needs to control only two things — *timing* (for speed) and *bit pattern sequence* (for direction and count).

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete PC-based stepper motor control system. Label the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The system has the following blocks (refer §4.1 diagram):

1. **PC with Application Software** — generates the 4-bit data word (direction) and the inter-pulse delay (speed); maintains the revolution counter.
2. **Parallel Port / USB Interface** — physically transports the digital pattern from the PC to the external driver card. The data pins D0–D3 carry the four phase-control bits.
3. **Buffer / Opto-Isolator** — provides electrical isolation so that back-EMF from the motor windings cannot destroy the PC motherboard.
4. **Stepper Motor Driver IC (ULN2003 / L293D)** — current amplifier; converts TTL logic-level inputs (≈ 5 V, ≈ 10 mA) into high-current drive outputs (12 V, 500 mA – 2 A) capable of energising the stator coils.
5. **DC Power Supply** — provides the high-current rail to the driver IC's COM pin / Vs terminal.
6. **Stepper Motor** — the mechanical load; converts the sequence of phase currents into discrete shaft rotations.
7. **Shaft Output** — the final mechanical rotation whose speed, direction, and angular extent are under software command.

The signal flow is strictly: **PC → Port → Buffer → Driver → Motor → Shaft**. Each block is essential — removing any one breaks the chain.

---

**Q.A (b)** A **1.8° per step** stepper motor has to rotate at **90 RPM** for exactly **6 revolutions** in the forward direction. Calculate:
   (i) the pulse frequency required,
   (ii) the total number of pulses to be delivered,
   (iii) the total mechanical angle traversed,
   (iv) the total time taken. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Step angle α = 1.8° → $N = 360° / 1.8° = 200$ steps/revolution
- $\omega_{\text{RPM}} = 90$ RPM
- $R = 6$ revolutions

**(i) Pulse frequency:**

$$
f \;=\; \frac{N \cdot \omega_{\text{RPM}}}{60} \;=\; \frac{200 \times 90}{60} \;=\; \frac{18000}{60} \;=\; 300 \;\text{Hz}
$$

**(ii) Total pulses:**

$$
n_{\text{steps}} \;=\; R \times N \;=\; 6 \times 200 \;=\; 1200 \;\text{pulses}
$$

**(iii) Total mechanical angle:**

$$
\theta_{\text{actual}} \;=\; n_{\text{steps}} \times \alpha \;=\; 1200 \times 1.8° \;=\; 2160°
$$

(Sanity check: $6 \times 360° = 2160°$ ✓)

**(iv) Total time:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{n_{\text{steps}}}{f} \;=\; \frac{1200}{300} \;=\; 4 \;\text{s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $f = 300$ Hz, $n = 1200$ pulses, $\theta = 2160°$, $T = 4$ s.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

**Q.B (a)** Explain with a timing diagram how the direction of rotation of a 4-phase stepper motor is reversed by changing the excitation sequence. Use the Full-Step (2-phase-on) mode in your explanation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In the Full-Step (2-phase-on) mode the four stator phases are excited in pairs in the cyclic order **AB → BC → CD → DA** for clockwise rotation. The corresponding 4-bit pattern sent by the PC is **0110 → 1100 → 1001 → 1010** (with bit-order D3 D2 D1 D0 representing phases D, C, B, A).

To reverse direction, the same four states are visited but in the *opposite* cyclic order: **AB → DA → CD → BC**, i.e. **0110 → 1010 → 1001 → 1100**. Equivalently, the PC simply walks backward through the same forward-state table.

**Timing Sequence (Forward):**

| Time | D3 D2 D1 D0 | Phases ON |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| t₁ | 0110 | A & B |
| t₂ | 1100 | B & C |
| t₃ | 1001 | C & D |
| t₄ | 1010 | D & A |
| t₅ | 0110 | A & B (cycle repeats) |

**Timing Sequence (Reverse):**

| Time | D3 D2 D1 D0 | Phases ON |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| t₁ | 0110 | A & B |
| t₂ | 1010 | D & A |
| t₃ | 1001 | C & D |
| t₄ | 1100 | B & C |
| t₅ | 0110 | A & B (cycle repeats) |

Because the stator flux rotates in the opposite sense, the rotor is dragged backwards by exactly the same step angle per pulse. There is no need to change the pulse frequency or amplitude — only the *order* of the bitstream is reversed in software.

---

**Q.B (b)** A PC is programmed to send pulses at **750 Hz** to a stepper motor with **200 steps/revolution**.
   (i) Calculate the resulting shaft speed in RPM.
   (ii) If the program requests **15 revolutions**, how many seconds will the motion take?
   (iii) If the inter-pulse delay is doubled (keeping pulse count unchanged), what is the new RPM and total time? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 750$ Hz, $N = 200$, $R = 15$.

**(i) Shaft speed:**

$$
\omega_{\text{RPM}} \;=\; \frac{60 \, f}{N} \;=\; \frac{60 \times 750}{200} \;=\; \frac{45000}{200} \;=\; 225 \;\text{RPM}
$$

**(ii) Total pulses and time:**

$$
n_{\text{steps}} \;=\; R \times N \;=\; 15 \times 200 \;=\; 3000 \;\text{pulses}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{n_{\text{steps}}}{f} \;=\; \frac{3000}{750} \;=\; 4 \;\text{s}
$$

**(iii) Effect of doubling the delay:**

New frequency $f' = f / 2 = 375$ Hz.

$$
\omega_{\text{RPM}}' \;=\; \frac{60 \times 375}{200} \;=\; 112.5 \;\text{RPM}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}}' \;=\; \frac{3000}{375} \;=\; 8 \;\text{s}
$$

**Conclusion:** Doubling the inter-pulse delay halves the speed and doubles the cycle time, while the number of revolutions (and hence total pulses) remains unchanged. Speed control is therefore *linearly independent* of position control.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on This Experiment**
>
> 1. **Conflating *step angle* with *step per rev*.** The step angle is given in degrees; students forget to invert it to find N. Always write $N = 360°/\alpha$ first.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 60** in the speed formula. A common wrong answer is $\omega = f/N$ instead of $\omega = 60f/N$. Units reveal the error immediately — $\omega$ must be in *per minute*, not per second.
> 3. **Treating half-step as full-step.** If the motor is driven in half-step mode, $N$ effectively doubles. Check the excitation mode in the question stem.
> 4. **Ignoring the COM pin of the ULN2003.** Students leave it floating and the IC burns out within seconds. Examiners *will* deduct marks for this in the lab viva.
> 5. **Not stating the open-loop assumption.** Examiners award marks for explicitly mentioning that position is *inferred* from pulse count with no feedback sensor.
> 6. **Pulse-rate exceeding the motor's start-stop capability.** If the calculated $f$ exceeds the motor's maximum slew rate, you must specify an *acceleration ramp* — losing this point is a classic 7-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Punch-List — Memorise Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- ☐ A stepper motor moves in **discrete angular steps** of fixed size α, never continuously.
- ☐ Standard lab motor: **α = 1.8°, N = 200 steps/rev**.
- ☐ **Speed (RPM) = 60 × f / N** — speed is set *only* by pulse frequency.
- ☐ **Direction** is set *only* by the **order** of the 4-bit phase pattern.
- ☐ **Number of revolutions** is set *only* by the **total pulse count n = R × N**.
- ☐ Three excitation modes: **Wave (1-phase), Full-step (2-phase), Half-step (1-2-phase)**.
- ☐ The driver IC (ULN2003 / L293D) must have its **COM / Vs pin tied to the motor supply rail**.
- ☐ Use **opto-isolation** between PC and driver to protect the motherboard from inductive kickback.
- ☐ The motor is **open-loop**: assume every pulse results in one step; never exceed the start-stop pulse rate or steps will be *lost* and position will drift.
- ☐ Half-step mode gives **double resolution (400 steps/rev for α = 1.8°)** but lower torque ripple handling.
- ☐ Always **cross-check** your pulse count result against the equivalent mechanical angle $\theta = n \times \alpha$ — it must equal $R \times 360°$.
- ☐ In KTU lab viva: be ready to (i) draw the 4-state ring diagram, (ii) redraw the software flowchart, (iii) name the driver IC and explain the COM-pin connection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of maximum and minimum position of links. Estimation of accuracy, repeatability and resolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of maximum and minimum position of links. Estimation of accuracy, repeatability and resolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Link Position Limits (Maximum and Minimum Position of Links)

The **maximum and minimum position of links** refers to the extreme physical displacements or angular rotations that each individual link (joint) of a robotic manipulator can achieve, bounded by its mechanical stops, joint limits, and software-enforced travel restrictions. These limits collectively define the **Workspace** — the entire set of points in space that the robot's end-effector (TCP – Tool Center Point) can physically reach.

**Joint-Level Position Limits:**
- **Revolute (R) Joint:** $\theta_i \in [\theta_{i,\min},\ \theta_{i,\max}]$
- **Prismatic (P) Joint:** $d_i \in [d_{i,\min},\ d_{i,\max}]$

**Cartesian (Task Space) Limits:**
- **Maximum Reach Position:** Furthest point the end-effector can reach from the robot base.
- **Minimum Reach Position:** Closest point the end-effector can reach without self-collision (often limited by link geometry).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For a typical 2-DOF planar manipulator, students must experimentally verify the maximum and minimum reach of the end-effector by varying joint angles from their mechanical limits to their extreme opposing limits, and tabulate the corresponding Cartesian coordinates.

### 1.2 Definition of Accuracy, Repeatability and Resolution

| Term | Formal Definition | SI Unit / Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | The ability of a robot to position its end-effector at a *commanded* (target) location in space. | millimeters (mm) |
| **Repeatability** | The ability of the robot to *return repeatedly* to the same pre-taught position under identical loading, speed, and environmental conditions. | millimeters (mm) |
| **Resolution** | The *smallest incremental change* in position or angle that the robot's control system can command, sense, and execute. | mm or degrees (°) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard Reference:** Per **ISO 9283** (Manipulating industrial robots — Performance criteria and related test methods), accuracy and repeatability are measured as positional dispersion over **30 cycles** for a given target point, and reported as statistical measures (mean error and standard deviation).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **human arm reaching for a cup on a table**:
- **Maximum Position:** Fully stretched out — your fingertips are as far as possible.
- **Minimum Position:** Tucked in — your fingertips touch your shoulder.
- **Accuracy:** You try to touch a specific dot on the table — how close do you get? *(Commander asked for point A; you reached point B.)*
- **Repeatability:** You close your eyes, open them, and touch the *same* spot 10 times — how tightly clustered are those 10 touches? *(You always reach the same wrong spot, but consistently.)*
- **Resolution:** The smallest muscle twitch you can make — i.e., the finest granularity of motion your joints can produce.

> [!TIP]
> A common student misconception: *"High accuracy implies high repeatability."* **This is FALSE.** A robot can be highly repeatable (always lands at the same place) but inaccurate (that place is consistently *off* from the target). Repeatability is generally easier to achieve than accuracy, and is usually numerically smaller (better) than accuracy for the same robot.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Link Position Analysis — Kinematic Foundation

The position of any link's end is determined using the **Direct (Forward) Kinematic Equation**:

$$\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$$

where $\mathbf{x}$ is the task-space (Cartesian) position vector and $\mathbf{q}$ is the joint-space vector.

For a **2-DOF planar manipulator** with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, and joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$:

$$x = L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$y = L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

**Why these limits matter:**
- They define the **Dexterous Workspace** (every orientation reachable) vs. the **Reachable Workspace** (at least one orientation reachable).
- They are essential for **offline programming**, **collision avoidance**, and **workcell layout design**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Maximum/Minimum Position Determination

1. **Identify Joint Limits** — Read mechanical stops or controller parameters: $\theta_1 \in [\theta_{1\min}, \theta_{1\max}]$, $\theta_2 \in [\theta_{2\min}, \theta_{2\max}]$.
2. **Compute Reach Radius** — The distance from the base to the end-effector is:
   
   $$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(\theta_2)}$$

3. **Find Maximum Reach** — Occurs when $\cos(\theta_2) = +1$ (link 2 fully extended in line with link 1):
   
   $$r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2$$

4. **Find Minimum Reach** — Occurs when $\cos(\theta_2) = -1$ (link 2 folded back over link 1):
   
   $$r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$$

5. **Verify Joint Limit Feasibility** — Confirm that $\theta_2 = 0°$ and $\theta_2 = 180°$ are within allowed joint range.

### 2.3 Accuracy — Theoretical Framework

Accuracy is fundamentally a **bias error** (systematic), not a random error. It is computed as the **Euclidean distance** between the commanded position and the average (mean) of the attained positions over $N$ trials:

$$A = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2 + (\bar{z} - z_c)^2}$$

where $(\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z})$ is the centroid of achieved positions and $(x_c, y_c, z_c)$ is the commanded target.

**Sources of Inaccuracy:**
- Mechanical deflections under load
- Backlash in gears and transmissions
- Link manufacturing tolerances
- Calibration errors
- Thermal expansion
- Kinematic model errors (neglecting higher-order dynamics)

### 2.4 Repeatability — Statistical Formulation

Repeatability captures the **random (stochastic) scatter** of achieved positions and is universally expressed as a multiple of the standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$R = k \cdot \sigma$$

where $k$ is a coverage factor. For normal (Gaussian) distributions:
- $k = 1 \Rightarrow$ captures ~68.3% of trials (1$\sigma$)
- $k = 2 \Rightarrow$ captures ~95.4% of trials (2$\sigma$)
- $k = 3 \Rightarrow$ captures ~99.7% of trials (3$\sigma$, **industry standard for robotics**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** Robot repeatability is conventionally quoted as $\pm 3\sigma$, meaning 99.7% of attempts lie within the stated band around the mean achieved position.

For a 1D measurement set $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

### 2.5 Resolution — Quantization Limit

Resolution is the **smallest discrete step** the controller can issue. It depends on the **encoder resolution** and any **gear reduction ratio** $n$ between motor and link:

$$\Delta \theta_{\text{link}} = \frac{\Delta \theta_{\text{encoder}}}{n}$$

where $\Delta \theta_{\text{encoder}} = \frac{360°}{N_{\text{pulses per rev}}}$.

For a prismatic axis:

$$\Delta d = \frac{\text{Lead of screw}}{N_{\text{pulses per rev}} \cdot n}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | End-effector position (2R planar) | $x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | m | Forward kinematics |
| 2 | End-effector position (2R planar) | $y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | m | Forward kinematics |
| 3 | Reach distance | $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ | m | Polar form |
| 4 | Maximum reach | $r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2$ | m | Links fully extended |
| 5 | Minimum reach | $r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ | m | Links fully folded |
| 6 | Workspace area (annulus) | $A_w = \pi(r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2)$ | m² | For full-revolution joints |
| 7 | Accuracy | $A = \sqrt{\sum_{i}(x_{c,i} - \bar{x}_i)^2}$ | m | Bias error |
| 8 | Repeatability | $R = \pm 3\sigma$ | m | 99.7% confidence |
| 9 | Standard deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | m | Sample std. dev. |
| 10 | Angular resolution | $\Delta \theta_{\text{link}} = \frac{360°}{N \cdot n}$ | degrees | Encoder + gearbox |
| 11 | Linear resolution | $\Delta d = \frac{\text{Lead}}{N \cdot n}$ | m | Leadscrew + encoder |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Symbol Note:** All absolute-value operations (e.g., $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$) are written with `\vert` to prevent markdown table parsing errors.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly:** Weld gun positioning requires repeatability of $\pm 0.1$ mm or better.
- **PCB Pick-and-Place:** Resolution governs smallest SMD component that can be handled (0201 size ≈ 0.3 mm).
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Repeatability is prioritized over accuracy because procedures are *teach-pendant based* — the surgeon defines the trajectory and the robot must replay it faithfully.
- **Warehouse AMRs:** Mobile robots use SLAM resolution and odometric resolution to estimate position within the warehouse grid.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reach Limits of a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

**Given:** A 2R planar arm with link lengths $L_1 = 0.4$ m and $L_2 = 0.3$ m. Joint limits: $\theta_1 \in [-90°, +90°]$, $\theta_2 \in [-150°, +150°]$.

**Step 1:** Express the squared reach distance using the cosine rule on the triangle formed by base, joint 2, and end-effector.

$$r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(\theta_2)$$

**Step 2:** Identify the extrema by taking the partial derivative with respect to $\theta_2$.

$$\frac{\partial (r^2)}{\partial \theta_2} = -2 L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2)$$

**Step 3:** Set derivative to zero for stationary points.

$$-2 L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \sin(\theta_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \theta_2 = 0° \ \text{or} \ 180°$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate $r^2$ at the critical points.

$$\text{At } \theta_2 = 0°: \quad r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 = (L_1 + L_2)^2$$

$$\text{At } \theta_2 = 180°: \quad r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 - 2 L_1 L_2 = (L_1 - L_2)^2$$

**Step 5:** Compute numerical values.

$$r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2 = 0.4 + 0.3 = 0.7 \text{ m}$$

$$r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 0.4 - 0.3 \vert = 0.1 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Verify feasibility of $\theta_2 = 180°$ against the joint limit $[-150°, +150°]$. Since $180° > 150°$, the true $r_{\min}$ occurs at the boundary $\theta_2 = 150°$:

$$r_{\min,\text{actual}} = \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(150°)}$$

$$r_{\min,\text{actual}} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.09 + 2(0.4)(0.3)(-0.866)}$$

$$r_{\min,\text{actual}} = \sqrt{0.25 - 0.2078} = \sqrt{0.0422} \approx 0.2054 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** When joint limits prevent the theoretical extremes, the boundary values become the actual limits. This is the difference between the *kinematic* workspace and the *reachable* workspace.

### 3.2 Derivation: Accuracy and Repeatability from a Sample Dataset

**Given:** A 2D robot is commanded to reach point $P_c = (100.0 \text{ mm}, 200.0 \text{ mm})$. Over $N = 10$ trials, the attained positions are recorded:

| Trial | $x_i$ (mm) | $y_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100.4 | 199.6 |
| 2 | 99.7 | 200.3 |
| 3 | 100.1 | 200.0 |
| 4 | 99.9 | 200.2 |
| 5 | 100.2 | 199.8 |
| 6 | 100.0 | 200.1 |
| 7 | 100.3 | 199.9 |
| 8 | 99.8 | 200.2 |
| 9 | 100.1 | 200.0 |
| 10 | 100.0 | 199.7 |

**Step 1:** Compute the mean (centroid) of attained positions.

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{100.4 + 99.7 + 100.1 + 99.9 + 100.2 + 100.0 + 100.3 + 99.8 + 100.1 + 100.0}{10}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1000.5}{10} = 100.05 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{199.6 + 200.3 + 200.0 + 200.2 + 199.8 + 200.1 + 199.9 + 200.2 + 200.0 + 199.7}{10}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{1999.8}{10} = 199.98 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the standard deviations $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$.

$$\sigma_x = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

**Step 3:** Tabulate the squared deviations.

| Trial | $(x_i - \bar{x})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | +0.35 | 0.1225 | -0.38 | 0.1444 |
| 2 | -0.35 | 0.1225 | +0.32 | 0.1024 |
| 3 | +0.05 | 0.0025 | +0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 4 | -0.15 | 0.0225 | +0.22 | 0.0484 |
| 5 | +0.15 | 0.0225 | -0.18 | 0.0324 |
| 6 | -0.05 | 0.0025 | +0.12 | 0.0144 |
| 7 | +0.25 | 0.0625 | -0.08 | 0.0064 |
| 8 | -0.25 | 0.0625 | +0.22 | 0.0484 |
| 9 | +0.05 | 0.0025 | +0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 10 | -0.05 | 0.0025 | -0.28 | 0.0784 |
| **Sum** | — | **0.4250** | — | **0.4760** |

**Step 4:** Compute the standard deviations.

$$\sigma_x = \sqrt{\frac{0.4250}{9}} = \sqrt{0.04722} \approx 0.2173 \text{ mm}$$

$$\sigma_y = \sqrt{\frac{0.4760}{9}} = \sqrt{0.05289} \approx 0.2300 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the combined (radial) standard deviation for 2D positional spread.

$$\sigma_r = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2} = \sqrt{0.04722 + 0.05289} = \sqrt{0.10011} \approx 0.3164 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6:** Compute Repeatability as $\pm 3\sigma_r$.

$$R = \pm 3 \times 0.3164 = \pm 0.9493 \text{ mm} \approx \pm 0.95 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7:** Compute Accuracy as the distance between commanded and mean position.

$$A = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2}$$

$$A = \sqrt{(100.05 - 100.0)^2 + (199.98 - 200.0)^2}$$

$$A = \sqrt{(0.05)^2 + (-0.02)^2} = \sqrt{0.0025 + 0.0004} = \sqrt{0.0029}$$

$$A \approx 0.0539 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8:** Compare and conclude.

> **Conclusion:** The robot exhibits a **repeatability of ±0.95 mm** and an **accuracy of ~0.054 mm**. Repeatability is ~17× larger than accuracy, indicating that the systematic bias is well-calibrated but the random scatter dominates the error budget. Such a profile is typical of a well-tuned SCARA or 6-DOF arm.

### 3.3 Derivation: Resolution from Encoder and Gear Train

**Given:** A revolute joint is driven by a motor with an encoder of $N = 2000$ pulses/rev, connected through a harmonic drive gear reducer with ratio $n = 100:1$.

**Step 1:** Compute the encoder's intrinsic angular step.

$$\Delta \theta_{\text{encoder}} = \frac{360°}{N} = \frac{360°}{2000} = 0.18° \text{ per pulse}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the gear reduction. The link moves by a fraction of the motor rotation.

$$\Delta \theta_{\text{link}} = \frac{\Delta \theta_{\text{encoder}}}{n} = \frac{0.18°}{100} = 0.0018° \text{ per pulse}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to radians for analytical use.

$$\Delta \theta_{\text{link}} = 0.0018° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad} \approx 31.4 \ \mu\text{rad}$$

**Step 4:** Determine the linear resolution at the end of a $0.3$ m link.

$$\Delta d = L \cdot \Delta \theta_{\text{link}} = 0.3 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-5} = 9.42 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \approx 9.4 \ \mu\text{m}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Observation | Likely Dominant Cause | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| Large accuracy, small repeatability | Systematic bias (calibration, link length error) | Re-calibrate kinematic model (DH parameters) |
| Small accuracy, large repeatability | Random noise (encoder jitter, backlash, thermal) | Improve mechanical stiffness, reduce backlash |
| Resolution > Repeatability | Controller quantization | Upgrade encoder resolution or gear ratio |
| Accuracy = Repeatability | Random walk (e.g., loose joint) | Mechanical inspection and tightening |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Robot Performance Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Commanded Pose x_c y_c] --> B[Robot Controller Issues Joint Setpoints]
    B --> C[Joint Actuators Drive Links]
    C --> D[End Effector Reaches Actual Position]
    D --> E[External Measurement System e.g. Camera or Laser Tracker]
    E --> F[Record N = 30 Trials]
    F --> G[Statistical Post Processing]
    G --> H[Compute Mean Position x_bar y_bar]
    G --> I[Compute Standard Deviation sigma]
    H --> J[Accuracy = Distance Commanded to Mean]
    I --> K[Repeatability = Plus or Minus 3 sigma]
    L[Encoder Resolution N] --> M[Gear Ratio n]
    M --> N[Link Resolution = 360 divided by N times n]
    N --> O[Resolution Reported in mm or deg]
    J --> P[Final Performance Report]
    K --> P
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Workspace Envelope of a 2R Planar Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Workspace[Reachable Workspace]
        direction TB
        A1[Base Origin] --> A2[Link 1 of length L1]
        A2 --> A3[Joint 2]
        A3 --> A4[Link 2 of length L2]
        A4 --> A5[End Effector]
    end
    A1 --> B1[Inner Boundary r_min = absolute L1 minus L2]
    A1 --> B2[Outer Boundary r_max = L1 plus L2]
    B1 --> C1[Annular Region for theta2 in minus 150 to plus 150 deg]
    B2 --> C1
```

### 4.3 Relationship Between the Three Performance Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Position Error] --> B[Systematic Component Bias]
    A --> C[Random Component Noise]
    B --> D[Accuracy]
    C --> E[Repeatability]
    F[Quantization Limit] --> G[Resolution]
    D --> H[Performance Characterization]
    E --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[ISO 9283 Compliant Report]
```

### 4.4 Experimental Data Acquisition Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Step 1: Define Test Poses in Workspace] --> A2[Step 2: Command Pose to Robot]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Wait for Settling Time t_s]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Record End Effector Position]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Repeat N = 30 times]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Compute Mean and Standard Deviation]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Calculate Accuracy and Repeatability]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8: Tabulate Results in Lab Record]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*Define the terms accuracy, repeatability, and resolution as applied to industrial robots. State the standard test conditions under ISO 9283.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Accuracy (1 mark):** The ability of a robot to position its end-effector at the *commanded* target location. Mathematically, it is the distance between the commanded point and the mean of the attained positions over $N$ trials.
- **Repeatability (1 mark):** The ability of the robot to *return* to the same pre-taught position under identical conditions. It is defined as $\pm 3\sigma$ of the attained positions (99.7% confidence), per **ISO 9283**.
- **Resolution (0.5 mark):** The smallest incremental change in joint position that the control system can command, determined by the encoder and gear ratio.
- **ISO 9283 conditions (0.5 mark):** 30 cycles, maximum payload, rated speed, ambient temperature $20°\pm 2°C$, and pre-warmed robot.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*For a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, derive expressions for the maximum and minimum reach of the end-effector.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Using the cosine rule, the squared reach is $r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1L_2\cos(\theta_2)$ (1 mark).
- Maximum reach occurs when $\cos(\theta_2) = +1$ (i.e., $\theta_2 = 0°$): $r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2$ (1 mark).
- Minimum reach occurs when $\cos(\theta_2) = -1$ (i.e., $\theta_2 = 180°$): $r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ (1 mark).

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** A 2R planar manipulator has $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $L_2 = 0.4$ m, with joint limits $\theta_1 \in [-90°, +90°]$ and $\theta_2 \in [-135°, +135°]$. Determine the maximum and minimum reachable position of the end-effector and plot the workspace envelope. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot is commanded to move to point $P_c = (250.0, 300.0)$ mm. The following 8 measured positions are obtained (in mm). Compute the accuracy and repeatability (use $\pm 3\sigma_r$).

| Trial | $x_i$ | $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 250.6 | 300.4 |
| 2 | 250.2 | 300.1 |
| 3 | 250.4 | 299.7 |
| 4 | 249.8 | 300.3 |
| 5 | 250.1 | 300.0 |
| 6 | 249.9 | 300.2 |
| 7 | 250.3 | 299.8 |
| 8 | 250.0 | 300.1 |

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

- Theoretical maximum reach: $r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2 = 0.5 + 0.4 = 0.9$ m (1 mark).
- Theoretical minimum reach: $r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = 0.1$ m (1 mark).
- Since $\theta_2 = 180°$ is not within $[-135°, +135°]$, the actual minimum occurs at the boundary $\theta_2 = \pm 135°$ (1 mark):

$$r_{\min,\text{actual}} = \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1L_2\cos(135°)}$$

$$r_{\min,\text{actual}} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.16 + 2(0.5)(0.4)(-0.7071)}$$

$$r_{\min,\text{actual}} = \sqrt{0.41 - 0.2828} = \sqrt{0.1272} \approx 0.3567 \text{ m}$$

- So actual reach range: $[0.3567, 0.9]$ m (1 mark).
- Because $\theta_1 \in [-90°, +90°]$, the workspace is a **half-annulus** (semicircle ring) (1 mark).
- The Cartesian envelope: $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$, with the upper half-plane $y \geq 0$ excluded (since $\theta_1 < 0°$ allows below-x-axis; here it spans both halves symmetrically because $\theta_1$ is symmetric). The full annulus is achieved (1 mark) – clarify in your answer depending on $\theta_1$ bounds.
- Sketch the annular region between inner radius $0.357$ m and outer radius $0.9$ m, with the base at origin (1 mark).

**(b) [7 marks]**

- **Step 1:** Compute means (1 mark):

$$\bar{x} = \frac{250.6+250.2+250.4+249.8+250.1+249.9+250.3+250.0}{8} = \frac{2001.3}{8} = 250.1625 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{300.4+300.1+299.7+300.3+300.0+300.2+299.8+300.1}{8} = \frac{2400.6}{8} = 300.0750 \text{ mm}$$

- **Step 2:** Compute deviations and squared deviations (2 marks):

| Trial | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | squared | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | squared |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | +0.4375 | 0.1914 | +0.3250 | 0.1056 |
| 2 | +0.0375 | 0.0014 | +0.0250 | 0.0006 |
| 3 | +0.2375 | 0.0564 | -0.3750 | 0.1406 |
| 4 | -0.3625 | 0.1314 | +0.2250 | 0.0506 |
| 5 | -0.0625 | 0.0039 | -0.0750 | 0.0056 |
| 6 | -0.2625 | 0.0689 | +0.1250 | 0.0156 |
| 7 | +0.1375 | 0.0189 | -0.2750 | 0.0756 |
| 8 | -0.1625 | 0.0264 | +0.0250 | 0.0006 |
| **Sum** | — | **0.4987** | — | **0.3948** |

- **Step 3:** Standard deviations (1 mark):

$$\sigma_x = \sqrt{\frac{0.4987}{7}} = \sqrt{0.07124} = 0.2669 \text{ mm}$$

$$\sigma_y = \sqrt{\frac{0.3948}{7}} = \sqrt{0.05640} = 0.2375 \text{ mm}$$

- **Step 4:** Combined radial sigma (0.5 mark):

$$\sigma_r = \sqrt{0.2669^2 + 0.2375^2} = \sqrt{0.07124 + 0.05641} = \sqrt{0.12765} = 0.3573 \text{ mm}$$

- **Step 5:** Repeatability (0.5 mark):

$$R = \pm 3 \sigma_r = \pm 3(0.3573) = \pm 1.072 \text{ mm}$$

- **Step 6:** Accuracy (1 mark):

$$A = \sqrt{(250.1625 - 250.0)^2 + (300.0750 - 300.0)^2} = \sqrt{0.1625^2 + 0.0750^2}$$

$$A = \sqrt{0.0264 + 0.0056} = \sqrt{0.0320} = 0.179 \text{ mm}$$

- **Final Result:** Accuracy $\approx 0.18$ mm, Repeatability $\approx \pm 1.07$ mm.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the procedure to experimentally determine the **maximum and minimum position of links** of a 2-DOF robotic arm. List the instruments used and the observations recorded. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A revolute joint of an industrial robot is driven by a servo motor with an incremental encoder of 4096 pulses/rev, coupled through a planetary gearhead with ratio 50:1. Calculate (i) the angular resolution at the link, and (ii) the linear resolution at the tip of a 0.25 m link attached to this joint. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

- **Aim (0.5 mark):** To experimentally determine the maximum and minimum Cartesian positions reachable by the end-effector of a 2-DOF robotic arm.
- **Apparatus (1 mark):** 2-DOF robotic arm (servo-driven), teach pendant or control PC, digital protractor or inclinometer, measuring scale/ruler, data sheet.
- **Procedure (3 marks):**
   1. Power on the robot and home all joints.
   2. Using the teach pendant, move joint 1 to its mechanical minimum (e.g., $-90°$) and joint 2 to its minimum (e.g., $-150°$). Record the end-effector (x, y) using the external scale.
   3. Step joint 1 in 10° increments up to its maximum, recording (x, y) at each step.
   4. Repeat for joint 2 sweep while keeping joint 1 fixed.
   5. Identify the (x, y) pair with the maximum and minimum $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ from the dataset.
   6. Tabulate and plot the workspace envelope.
- **Tabulation Format (1 mark):** Table with columns: Joint 1 angle, Joint 2 angle, x, y, r.
- **Result (1 mark):** Maximum reach $r_{\max}$ and minimum reach $r_{\min}$ are reported with $\pm 1$ mm uncertainty.
- **Diagram (0.5 mark):** Sketch of the 2R arm at extreme positions showing the inner and outer reach boundaries.

**(b) [7 marks]**

- **Given:** $N = 4096$ pulses/rev, $n = 50$, link length $L = 0.25$ m.
- **Step (i): Angular resolution (3 marks):**

$$\Delta \theta_{\text{encoder}} = \frac{360°}{4096} = 0.0879° \text{ per pulse}$$

$$\Delta \theta_{\text{link}} = \frac{\Delta \theta_{\text{encoder}}}{n} = \frac{0.0879°}{50} = 0.001758°$$

$$\Delta \theta_{\text{link}} = 0.001758° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 3.069 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad} \approx 30.7 \ \mu\text{rad}$$

- **Step (ii): Linear resolution (4 marks):**

$$\Delta d = L \cdot \Delta \theta_{\text{link}} = 0.25 \times 3.069 \times 10^{-5} = 7.67 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta d \approx 7.67 \ \mu\text{m}$$

- **Conclusion:** The joint can resolve angular increments of $\approx 0.00176°$ ($\approx 30.7 \ \mu$rad) and the link tip can resolve linear increments of $\approx 7.67 \ \mu$m.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (verified from KTU valuation patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing accuracy and repeatability** — Writing the formula for accuracy as $\pm 3\sigma$ (this is repeatability, not accuracy). Accuracy uses the *mean* of the attained positions.
> 2. **Forgetting the $N-1$ denominator** — Using $N$ instead of $N-1$ in the standard deviation formula (Bessel's correction) costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring joint limits in workspace derivation** — Stating $r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ without checking whether $\theta_2 = 180°$ is kinematically allowed.
> 4. **Wrong units in resolution** — Reporting angular resolution in radians when the question asks for degrees, or vice versa.
> 5. **Mixing up resolution and repeatability** — Resolution is a *quantization* property; repeatability is a *statistical scatter* property. They are not the same.
> 6. **Not showing intermediate steps in numerical problems** — KTU examiners explicitly look for mean calculation, deviation table, and sigma computation. Skipping to the final answer loses 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Sign errors in the deviation sum** — One wrong sign in $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ cascades to wrong $\sigma$, wrong $R$, and a fully zero-scored problem.
