### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Link position limits** are governed by joint mechanical stops and controller-enforced software limits. They determine the **Reachable Workspace**.
- **Maximum reach** of a 2R arm: $r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2$ (both links collinear, $\theta_2 = 0°$).
- **Minimum reach** of a 2R arm: $r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ (links folded, $\theta_2 = 180°$), subject to joint feasibility.
- **Workspace area** for a 2R arm with full rotation: $A_w = \pi(r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2)$.
- **Accuracy** = Euclidean distance from *commanded* position to *mean attained* position. It is a *systematic* (bias) error.
- **Repeatability** = $\pm 3\sigma$ of attained positions. It is a *random* (stochastic) error measure. ISO 9283 standard.
- **Resolution** = smallest discrete step the controller can issue, limited by encoder ($N$ pulses/rev) and gear ratio ($n$).
- **Angular resolution:** $\Delta \theta_{\text{link}} = \dfrac{360°}{N \cdot n}$
- **Linear resolution:** $\Delta d = L \cdot \Delta \theta_{\text{link}}$ (for a link of length $L$).
- **Industry rule of thumb:** Repeatability is *almost always numerically better (smaller) than accuracy* in well-calibrated industrial robots.
- **Experiment standard:** $N = 30$ cycles per ISO 9283; pre-warm robot; rated speed and payload; ambient temperature $20° \pm 2°C$.
- **Pitfall:** A robot can be repeatable but inaccurate (consistent bias); less commonly, accurate on average but with large scatter (poor repeatability).
- **Resolution must be smaller than required repeatability**, otherwise the controller cannot even *command* the desired trajectory.
- **In KTU lab record**, always include: aim, apparatus, procedure, observation table, sample calculation, result with units, and a labeled workspace plot.
- **KTU Examiner Tip:** When given numerical data, *always show the deviation table* — it's the single biggest mark-earning element in computational questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification of transformation (position and orientation) with respect to gripper and world coordinate system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Verification of transformation (position and orientation) with respect to gripper and world coordinate system ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of industrial robotics and manipulator kinematics, **Coordinate Transformation** is the mathematical mapping of a point's position and orientation from one reference frame to another. For this experiment, we specifically deal with two critical frames: - **World Coordinate System (Base Frame, $\Sigma_W$):** The fixed, stationary reference frame attached to the base of the robotic manipulator or the workstation floor. All global positions are referenced to this frame. - **Gripper Coordinate System (Tool Frame, $\Sigma_G$):** The moving reference frame attached to the end-effector (gripper) of the robot. Positions of objects in the gripper's vicinity are referenced to this frame. **Homogeneous Transformation Matrix ($^W_GT$):** A $4 \times 4$ matrix that simultaneously encapsulates both the rotational orientation (3×3 rotation sub-matrix $^W_GR$) and translational position (3×1 position vector $^W_GP$) required to map a point from the Gripper frame to the World frame. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The verification experiment demands students demonstrate that the **forward transformation** ($^W_GT$) and **inverse transformation** ($^W_GT^{-1}$) are mathematically consistent and physically realizable on a robotic work-cell or simulation platform (e.g., RoboAnalyzer, MATLAB, or V-REP/CoppeliaSim). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are **driving a car (the gripper)** and you have a **GPS device showing your coordinates (the world system)**. Inside the car, a small toy soldier sitting on the dashboard has a "car-relative position" — say, 1 meter to the right and 2 meters ahead of the steering wheel. The world system reports the car's position as (Latitude, Longitude) on the actual map. Now, if a passenger asks, *"Where is the toy soldier in world coordinates?"*, the answer is **not** just the GPS coordinates of the car. You must: 1. **Rotate** the soldier's car-relative coordinates by the car's heading angle (orientation). 2. **Translate** (add) the car's GPS position to the rotated coordinates. This two-step process (rotate, then translate) is exactly what a **homogeneous transformation** does in robotics. The experiment verifies that this mathematical procedure matches real-world measurements of the gripper. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** We are proving that a single $4 \times 4$ matrix can completely describe *where the gripper is* and *which way it is pointing* relative to the world — and that the math correctly predicts real physical positions of objects around the robot. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard Unit Conventions:** Linear positions in **millimeters (mm)** or **meters (m)**. Angles in **degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)**. - **Gravity Vector:** $\vec{g} = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (relevant for gravity-aligned gripper orientations). - **Rotation Conventions:** **Right-Hand Rule** is the KTU-mandated standard for positive rotations about the X, Y, and Z axes. - **Homogeneous Coordinate Convention:** The fourth row of the transformation matrix is always $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition Box — Homogeneous Transformation:** > A **homogeneous transformation** is a $4 \times 4$ matrix of the form: > $$T = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3 \times 3} & P_{3 \times 1} \\ 0_{1 \times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ > where $R$ is the rotation matrix (orientation) and $P$ is the position vector. This compact form allows a single matrix multiplication to perform rotation AND translation simultaneously. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Transformations Are Needed A robotic manipulator operates in **3D space**. The end-effector (gripper) does not remain stationary — it translates (moves position) and rotates (changes orientation) as the robot executes tasks like pick-and-place, welding, or assembly. To program such tasks, we need a consistent mathematical language that: 1. Describes the gripper's pose (position + orientation) with respect to the world. 2. Allows conversion of object coordinates from one frame to another. 3. Enables inverse calculations (e.g., "Given I want the gripper to be at world position X, what joint angles do I need?"). ### 2.2 The Anatomy of a Homogeneous Transformation Matrix The general form relating Gripper frame ($G$) to World frame ($W$) is denoted $^W_GT$: $$ ^W_GT = \begin{bmatrix} ^W_GR & ^W_GP \\ 0 \; 0 \; 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & p_x \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & p_y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & p_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Structural Breakdown:** - **Top-Left 3×3 Block ($^W_GR$):** Rotation matrix. Its **columns** are the unit vectors of the gripper axes expressed in the world frame. Its **rows** are the world axes expressed in the gripper frame. - **Top-Right 3×1 Block ($^W_GP$):** Position of the gripper origin (with respect to world origin) expressed in the world frame. - **Bottom Row $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$:** Required for homogeneous coordinate math; preserves the scale (always 1 for rigid transformations). ### 2.3 Fundamental Properties of Rotation Matrices For any valid rotation matrix $R$: - $R^T R = I$ (orthogonality) - $\det(R) = +1$ (right-handed coordinate system) - $R^{-1} = R^T$ (inverse equals transpose) For the $4 \times 4$ homogeneous matrix: - $T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T P \\ 0 \; 0 \; 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Concept** | **Formula / Matrix** | **Variables** | **Units** | |-------------|----------------------|---------------|-----------| | Rotation about X-axis by $\theta$ | $R_x(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ 0 & \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | $\theta$ | rad | | Rotation about Y-axis by $\theta$ | $R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | $\theta$ | rad | | Rotation about Z-axis by $\theta$ | $R_z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\theta$ | rad | | Homogeneous Transform (World to Gripper) | $^W_GT = \begin{bmatrix} R & P \\ 0 \; 0 \; 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $R$ (3×3), $P$ (3×1) | unitless, m | | Point Transformation (Gripper to World) | $\begin{bmatrix} P_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = ^W_GT \begin{bmatrix} P_g \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $P_w$, $P_g$ (3×1) | m | | Inverse Transform (World to Gripper) | $^W_GT^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T P \\ 0 \; 0 \; 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | — | — | | Point Transformation (World to Gripper) | $\begin{bmatrix} P_g \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = ^W_GT^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} P_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | — | m | | Compound Rotation (Z-Y-X Euler) | $R = R_z(\alpha) \cdot R_y(\beta) \cdot R_x(\gamma)$ | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ | rad | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** These transformation matrices are the backbone of every industrial robot controller (ABB, KUKA, FANUC). Offline programming software like **RobotStudio** or **RoboDK** uses these exact formulas to simulate and verify tool paths before execution, preventing costly collisions in automotive assembly lines. ### 2.5 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Verification **Why Verify?** Theoretical mathematics can be misapplied. Verification ensures: - The **forward map** ($G \to W$) and **inverse map** ($W \to G$) are consistent (i.e., $T \cdot T^{-1} = I$). - Physical measurement (e.g., reading gripper coordinates from a calibration tool) agrees with computational prediction. - The rotation matrix preserves the orthonormality property (no numerical drift). **How to Verify in the Lab:** 1. **Mathematical Verification:** Compute $T$, $T^{-1}$, and check $T \cdot T^{-1} = I_{4 \times 4}$. 2. **Numerical Round-Trip Test:** Transform a known point $P_g \to P_w \to P_g'$ and confirm $P_g' = P_g$. 3. **Simulation/Software Verification:** Use MATLAB, Python (Robotics Toolbox), or RoboAnalyzer to visualize the gripper frame relative to the world frame. 4. **Hardware Verification (if available):** Use a teach pendant to jog the robot to a known world coordinate and verify the gripper-frame readings match the predicted $T$ matrix. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of a General World-to-Gripper Homogeneous Transform Let the gripper frame origin be located at position $^W_GP = (p_x, p_y, p_z)$ relative to the world frame. Let the gripper frame be rotated relative to the world frame by an orientation described by a rotation matrix $^W_GR$. The relationship between a point $P$ expressed in the gripper frame ($^GP$) and the same point expressed in the world frame ($^WP$) is: **Step 1:** Express the position vector in component form. A point $P$ in the gripper frame has coordinates $^GP = (p_{gx}, p_{gy}, p_{gz})$. Each coordinate represents the projection of the point onto the gripper's local axes. **Step 2:** Re-express each gripper axis in world coordinates. The columns of the rotation matrix $^W_GR$ are the gripper's unit vectors (X, Y, Z) expressed in the world frame: $$ ^W\hat{X}_G = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} \\ r_{21} \\ r_{31} \end{bmatrix}, \quad ^W\hat{Y}_G = \begin{bmatrix} r_{12} \\ r_{22} \\ r_{32} \end{bmatrix}, \quad ^W\hat{Z}_G = \begin{bmatrix} r_{13} \\ r_{23} \\ r_{33} \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the world-frame position as a linear combination. $$ ^WP = p_{gx} \cdot {^W\hat{X}_G} + p_{gy} \cdot {^W\hat{Y}_G} + p_{gz} \cdot {^W\hat{Z}_G} + {^W_GP} $$ **Step 4:** Write this in matrix form. $$ ^WP = {^W_GR} \cdot {^GP} + {^W_GP} $$ **Step 5:** Convert to homogeneous form by appending a "1" to each vector. $$ \begin{bmatrix} {^WP} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} {^W_GR} & {^W_GP} \\ 0 \; 0 \; 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} {^GP} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 6:** Final compact form. $$ \begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} {^WP} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= {^W_GT} \begin{bmatrix} {^GP} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ This derivation confirms that $^W_GT$ is the correct operator for converting gripper-frame points to world-frame points. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Inverse Transformation The inverse $^W_GT^{-1}$ maps a point from the world frame back to the gripper frame. Starting from the relation: $$ \begin{aligned} {^WP} = {^W_GR} \cdot {^GP} + {^W_GP} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1:** Isolate the term with $^GP$. $$ \begin{aligned} {^W_GR} \cdot {^GP} = {^WP} - {^W_GP} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Pre-multiply both sides by $^W_GR^{-1}$. For a rotation matrix, $^W_GR^{-1} = {^W_GR}^T$. $$ \begin{aligned} {^GP} = {^W_GR}^T \left( {^WP} - {^W_GP} \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Distribute the transpose. $$ \begin{aligned} {^GP} = {^W_GR}^T \cdot {^WP} - {^W_GR}^T \cdot {^W_GP} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Write in homogeneous matrix form. $$ \begin{aligned} {^W_GT}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} {^W_GR}^T & -{^W_GR}^T \cdot {^W_GP} \\ 0 \; 0 \; 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Final verification identity. The product of a matrix and its inverse must equal identity: $$ \begin{aligned} {^W_GT} \cdot {^W_GT}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} {^W_GR} & {^W_GP} \\ 0 \; 0 \; 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} {^W_GR}^T & -{^W_GR}^T \cdot {^W_GP} \\ 0 \; 0 \; 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Computing the top-left block: $$ \begin{aligned} {^W_GR} \cdot {^W_GR}^T = I_{3 \times 3} \end{aligned} $$ Computing the top-right block: $$ \begin{aligned} {^W_GR} \cdot \left( -{^W_GR}^T \cdot {^W_GP} \right) + {^W_GP} \cdot 1 = -{^W_GP} + {^W_GP} = 0_{3 \times 1} \end{aligned} $$ Computing the bottom row: clearly $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. Thus: $$ \begin{aligned} {^W_GT} \cdot {^W_GT}^{-1} = I_{4 \times 4} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ The math is self-consistent — the verification of the inverse transform is proven algebraically. --- ### 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Verification Example **Problem Statement:** A robot gripper is positioned at $^W_GP = (300, 200, 100)$ mm relative to the world frame, and is rotated by $\theta = 30°$ about the world Z-axis. A fiducial marker (test point) is located at gripper coordinates $^GP = (50, 0, 0)$ mm. **Task 1: Construct the homogeneous transformation matrix $^W_GT$.** **Step 1:** Compute the rotation matrix elements. With $\theta = 30°$: $$ \begin{aligned} \cos(30°) &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660 \\ \sin(30°) &= \frac{1}{2} = 0.5000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Write the rotation matrix $R_z(30°)$. $$ \begin{aligned} {^W_GR} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 30° & -\sin 30° & 0 \\ \sin 30° & \cos 30° & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8660 & -0.5000 & 0 \\ 0.5000 & 0.8660 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Assemble the full $4 \times 4$ homogeneous matrix. $$ \begin{aligned} {^W_GT} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8660 & -0.5000 & 0 & 300 \\ 0.5000 & 0.8660 & 0 & 200 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 100 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Task 2: Transform the gripper-frame point $^GP = (50, 0, 0)$ to the world frame.** **Step 1:** Express the point in homogeneous form: $^GP = (50, 0, 0, 1)^T$. **Step 2:** Compute the matrix-vector product. $$ \begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} {^WP} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8660 & -0.5000 & 0 & 300 \\ 0.5000 & 0.8660 & 0 & 200 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 100 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 50 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Multiply row by column for each output row. Row 1: $(0.8660 \times 50) + (-0.5000 \times 0) + (0 \times 0) + (300 \times 1) = 43.30 + 0 + 0 + 300 = 343.30$ Row 2: $(0.5000 \times 50) + (0.8660 \times 0) + (0 \times 0) + (200 \times 1) = 25.00 + 0 + 0 + 200 = 225.00$ Row 3: $(0 \times 50) + (0 \times 0) + (1 \times 0) + (100 \times 1) = 0 + 0 + 0 + 100 = 100.00$ Row 4: $0 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 1$ **Step 4:** Final result. $$ \begin{aligned} {^WP} = (343.30, \; 225.00, \; 100.00) \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Task 3: Verify by inverse transformation (round-trip test).** **Step 1:** Compute $^W_GT^{-1}$ using the formula. $$ \begin{aligned} {^W_GT}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} {^W_GR}^T & -{^W_GR}^T \cdot {^W_GP} \\ 0 \; 0 \; 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Compute $^W_GR^T$ (transpose of rotation matrix): $$ \begin{aligned} {^W_GR}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8660 & 0.5000 & 0 \\ -0.5000 & 0.8660 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Compute $-{^W_GR}^T \cdot {^W_GP}$: $$ \begin{aligned} {^W_GR}^T \cdot {^W_GP} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8660 \times 300 + 0.5000 \times 200 + 0 \times 100 \\ -0.5000 \times 300 + 0.8660 \times 200 + 0 \times 100 \\ 0 \times 300 + 0 \times 200 + 1 \times 100 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 259.80 + 100.00 + 0 \\ -150.00 + 173.20 + 0 \\ 0 + 0 + 100 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 359.80 \\ 23.20 \\ 100.00 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, $-{^W_GR}^T \cdot {^W_GP} = (-359.80, \; -23.20, \; -100.00)^T$. **Step 2:** Assemble the inverse matrix. $$ \begin{aligned} {^W_GT}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8660 & 0.5000 & 0 & -359.80 \\ -0.5000 & 0.8660 & 0 & -23.20 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & -100.00 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Apply inverse to world point $^WP = (343.30, 225.00, 100.00, 1)^T$. Row 1: $(0.8660 \times 343.30) + (0.5000 \times 225.00) + (0 \times 100.00) + (-359.80 \times 1) = 297.30 + 112.50 + 0 - 359.80 = 50.00$ Row 2: $(-0.5000 \times 343.30) + (0.8660 \times 225.00) + (0 \times 100.00) + (-23.20 \times 1) = -171.65 + 194.85 + 0 - 23.20 = 0.00$ Row 3: $(0 \times 343.30) + (0 \times 225.00) + (1 \times 100.00) + (-100.00 \times 1) = 0 + 0 + 100 - 100 = 0.00$ Row 4: $0 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 1$ **Step 4:** Final inverse result. $$ \begin{aligned} {^GP'} = (50.00, \; 0.00, \; 0.00) \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification Conclusion:** Since $^GP' = (50, 0, 0)$ matches the original $^GP = (50, 0, 0)$ exactly (within rounding tolerance), the **round-trip test is successful**. The transformation matrix $^W_GT$ and its inverse $^W_GT^{-1}$ are mathematically verified to be consistent. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The round-trip test (forward → inverse → original) is the gold-standard verification method used in KTU lab evaluations. A tolerance of $\pm 0.01$ mm in numerical computation is considered acceptable; any larger deviation indicates either matrix assembly error or numerical instability. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Diagram: Relationship Between World and Gripper Frames The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional relationship and data flow between the World Coordinate System ($\Sigma_W$) and the Gripper Coordinate System ($\Sigma_G$) during the verification experiment. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["WORLD FRAME SIGMA W
Fixed Base Origin
Reference Point 0,0,0"] nodeB["GRIPPER POSITION Px Py Pz
Translation Vector in mm
Relative to World"] nodeC["GRIPPER ORIENTATION R 3x3
Rotation Matrix
Right Hand Rule"] nodeD["HOMOGENEOUS TRANSFORM T 4x4
T = R P 0 0 0 1
Combined Operator"] nodeE["FORWARD MAPPING
Gripper Point to World Point
Pw = T times Pg"] nodeF["INVERSE MAPPING
World Point to Gripper Point
Pg = Tinv times Pw"] nodeG["VERIFICATION TEST 1
Orthogonality Check
R transpose R equals I"] nodeH["VERIFICATION TEST 2
Round Trip Test
Pg to Pw to Pg"] nodeI["VERIFICATION TEST 3
Determinant Check
det R equals plus 1"] nodeJ["VERIFICATION RESULT
Pass or Fail Status
Tolerance plus minus 0.01 mm"] nodeK["LABORATORY OUTPUT
Validated T Matrix
Confirmed Pose of Gripper"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeH nodeC --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeJ nodeH --> nodeJ nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK style nodeA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style nodeK fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px style nodeJ fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Verification Process ```mermaid graph LR stepA["STEP 1: Define Gripper Pose
Input: Position and Orientation
Output: P vector and R matrix"] stepB["STEP 2: Construct T Matrix
T = R P 0 0 0 1
4x4 Homogeneous"] stepC["STEP 3: Compute T Inverse
Tinv = R transpose minus R transpose P
4x4 Matrix"] stepD["STEP 4: Orthogonality Test
Check R transpose R equals I
Check det R equals 1"] stepE["STEP 5: Round Trip Test
Transform Pg to Pw to Pg prime
Compare Pg prime with Pg"] stepF["STEP 6: Tolerance Check
Error less than 0.01 mm
Accept or Reject"] stepG["STEP 7: Document Result
Verified T Matrix
Lab Report Submission"] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG style stepA fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#003d82,stroke-width:2px style stepB fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px style stepD fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px style stepF fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,stroke-width:2px style stepG fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Interpretation Guide | **Node / Step** | **Physical / Mathematical Meaning** | **Lab Activity Mapping** | |-----------------|------------------------------------|--------------------------| | World Frame $\Sigma_W$ | Fixed reference at robot base | Cannot be moved; origin at robot pedestal | | Gripper Position $P$ | Measured by teach pendant or kinematics | Read encoder values or teach coordinates | | Rotation Matrix $R$ | Describes gripper's facing direction | Computed from joint angles via DH parameters | | Homogeneous $T$ | Combined pose descriptor | Single $4 \times 4$ operator | | Forward Mapping | $P_w = T \cdot P_g$ | Predicted world position of a held object | | Inverse Mapping | $P_g = T^{-1} \cdot P_w$ | Required gripper coordinates to reach a world point | | Orthogonality Test | $R^T R = I$ | Sanity check on $R$ matrix | | Round-Trip Test | $P_g \to P_w \to P_g$ | Consistency check; should recover original | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question] > **Define a homogeneous transformation matrix. Explain how it represents both the position and orientation of a gripper with respect to the world coordinate system.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **homogeneous transformation matrix** is a $4 \times 4$ matrix that combines rotation and translation into a single mathematical operator, enabling simultaneous representation of a rigid body's position and orientation. It has the general form: $$ T = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3 \times 3} & P_{3 \times 1} \\ 0 \; 0 \; 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ - The **top-left 3×3 sub-matrix $R$** represents the **orientation** of the gripper. Its three columns are the unit vectors of the gripper's local X, Y, and Z axes expressed in the world frame. - The **top-right 3×1 vector $P$** represents the **position** of the gripper's origin relative to the world origin, expressed in world coordinates. - The **bottom row** $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ is fixed to preserve the homogeneous coordinate scaling convention. This single matrix completely defines the **pose** (position + orientation) of the gripper relative to the world frame and can be applied to transform any point from gripper coordinates to world coordinates via a single matrix multiplication. --- #### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question] > **What is the significance of verifying transformations between the gripper and world coordinate systems in an industrial robot? List any two verification methods.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The verification of transformations is significant because it **ensures the mathematical model of the robot's pose accurately represents real physical positions** in the workspace. Without verification, programming errors (incorrect joint angle conversions, sign errors in rotations) could cause the robot to collide with fixtures, miss target objects, or damage workpieces — all of which are costly in production. **Two Verification Methods:** 1. **Round-Trip Test (Inverse Consistency):** Transform a known point from the gripper frame to the world frame using $T$, then apply the inverse $T^{-1}$ to recover the original gripper-frame point. If the recovered point matches the original (within tolerance), the transformation is verified. 2. **Orthogonality Test on the Rotation Matrix:** Verify that the 3×3 rotation sub-matrix satisfies $R^T R = I$ and $\det(R) = +1$. Any deviation indicates a non-physical or erroneous rotation component. --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted] > **(a)** A robotic gripper is located at position $P = (400, 250, 150)$ mm relative to the world frame, and is rotated by $45°$ about the Z-axis of the world frame. Construct the homogeneous transformation matrix $^W_GT$. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A point $Q$ is located at gripper-frame coordinates $(60, 30, 20)$ mm. Using the matrix from part (a), compute the world-frame coordinates of $Q$ and verify your result using the inverse transformation. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1: Compute trigonometric values.** For $\theta = 45°$: - $\cos(45°) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071$ - $\sin(45°) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071$ **Step 2: Write the Z-axis rotation matrix.** $$ R_z(45°) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7071 & -0.7071 & 0 \\ 0.7071 & 0.7071 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3: Assemble the $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transformation matrix.** $$ {^W_GT} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7071 & -0.7071 & 0 & 400 \\ 0.7071 & 0.7071 & 0 & 250 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 150 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ This is the required transformation matrix mapping gripper points to world points. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1: Express the gripper point in homogeneous form.** $$ {^GQ} = (60, 30, 20, 1)^T $$ **Step 2: Multiply $^W_GT$ by $^GQ$ to get world coordinates.** $$ {^WQ} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7071 & -0.7071 & 0 & 400 \\ 0.7071 & 0.7071 & 0 & 250 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 150 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 60 \\ 30 \\ 20 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Computing each row: - Row 1: $(0.7071 \times 60) + (-0.7071 \times 30) + (0 \times 20) + 400 = 42.43 - 21.21 + 0 + 400 = 421.22$ - Row 2: $(0.7071 \times 60) + (0.7071 \times 30) + (0 \times 20) + 250 = 42.43 + 21.21 + 0 + 250 = 313.64$ - Row 3: $(0 \times 60) + (0 \times 30) + (1 \times 20) + 150 = 0 + 0 + 20 + 150 = 170$ - Row 4: $0 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 1$ **Result:** $^WQ = (421.22, 313.64, 170.00)$ mm. **Step 3: Compute the inverse matrix $^W_GT^{-1}$.** The transpose of $R$ is: $$ R^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7071 & 0.7071 & 0 \\ -0.7071 & 0.7071 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Compute $-R^T \cdot P$: - Row 1: $-(0.7071 \times 400 + 0.7071 \times 250 + 0) = -(282.84 + 176.78) = -459.62$ - Row 2: $-(-0.7071 \times 400 + 0.7071 \times 250 + 0) = -(-282.84 + 176.78) = 106.06$ - Row 3: $-(0 + 0 + 150) = -150$ $$ {^W_GT}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7071 & 0.7071 & 0 & -459.62 \\ -0.7071 & 0.7071 & 0 & 106.06 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & -150 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4: Apply inverse to $^WQ$.** $$ {^GQ}' = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7071 & 0.7071 & 0 & -459.62 \\ -0.7071 & 0.7071 & 0 & 106.06 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & -150 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 421.22 \\ 313.64 \\ 170.00 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Computing each row: - Row 1: $(0.7071 \times 421.22) + (0.7071 \times 313.64) + 0 + (-459.62) = 297.82 + 221.78 - 459.62 = 59.98 \approx 60$ - Row 2: $(-0.7071 \times 421.22) + (0.7071 \times 313.64) + 0 + 106.06 = -297.82 + 221.78 + 106.06 = 30.02 \approx 30$ - Row 3: $0 + 0 + 170 - 150 = 20$ - Row 4: $0 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 1$ **Result:** $^GQ' = (60, 30, 20)$ mm, which **matches the original gripper point** within rounding tolerance. **Verification Successful.** The transformation $^W_GT$ and its inverse are mutually consistent. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted] > **(a)** Derive the inverse of a general $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transformation matrix $T = \begin{bmatrix} R & P \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ where $R$ is a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix and $P$ is a $3 \times 1$ position vector. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Consider a gripper whose pose relative to the world frame is described by a sequence of rotations: first $30°$ about the world X-axis, then $45°$ about the world Y-axis, followed by a translation of $(100, 50, 200)$ mm. Compute the composite homogeneous transformation matrix $^W_GT$ and verify that the resulting rotation matrix is orthogonal. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution Outline:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Follow the derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes, showing the block-matrix inverse, the use of $R^{-1} = R^T$, and the algebraic simplification of the top-right block to yield $-R^T P$. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** - Compute $R_x(30°)$ and $R_y(45°)$ separately. - Form composite rotation $R = R_y(45°) \cdot R_x(30°)$ (right-multiplication applies in the moving frame; left-multiplication in the fixed frame — KTU convention favors **post-multiplication** $R = R_x \cdot R_y$ for intrinsic rotations; explicitly state your convention). - Assemble $^W_GT$ with $R$ and $P = (100, 50, 200)^T$. - Verify orthogonality by computing $R^T R$ and confirming it equals the $3 \times 3$ identity matrix. Compute $\det(R)$ and confirm it equals $+1$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Errors in Rotation Matrices:** The most common mistake is sign errors in the off-diagonal sine terms of $R_x$ and $R_y$. Memorize: positive $\theta$ rotation is counter-clockwise when viewed along the positive axis toward the origin (right-hand rule). > 2. **Order of Matrix Multiplication is Non-Commutative:** $R_x \cdot R_y \neq R_y \cdot R_x$. Students often multiply rotations in the wrong order, leading to a correct-looking but physically wrong matrix. Always confirm by checking $\det(R) = +1$. > 3. **Forgetting the Fourth Row:** The bottom row $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ is essential. A missing or incorrect bottom row will cause the round-trip verification to fail. > 4. **Degrees vs. Radians:** Trigonometric functions in MATLAB and most calculators default to radians. KTU problems often quote angles in degrees — **convert before computing**, or use the `sind()`/`cosd()` functions. > 5. **Confusing Frame Direction:** $^W_GT$ means "from G to W" (Gripper-to-World). Its inverse $^W_GT^{-1}$ is "from W to G." A reversed interpretation is a guaranteed zero in the exam. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - **Homogeneous Transformation Matrix** = $\begin{bmatrix} R & P \\ 0 \; 0 \; 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — a $4 \times 4$ matrix combining rotation and translation. - **World Frame ($\Sigma_W$):** Fixed at the robot base; global reference. - **Gripper Frame ($\Sigma_G$):** Attached to the end-effector; moves with the robot. - **$^W_GT$** maps gripper-frame points TO world-frame points: $P_w = T \cdot P_g$. - **$^W_GT^{-1}$** maps world-frame points TO gripper-frame points: $P_g = T^{-1} \cdot P_w$. - **Inverse formula:** $T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T P \\ 0 \; 0 \; 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. - **Rotation matrix properties:** $R^T R = I$ (orthogonal), $\det(R) = +1$ (right-handed), $R^{-1} = R^T$. - **Right-Hand Rule** is the KTU-mandated convention for positive rotations. - **Standard elementary rotations:** $R_x(\theta)$, $R_y(\theta)$, $R_z(\theta)$ — memorize all three. - **Verification Methods:** (1) Orthogonality check, (2) Determinant check, (3) Round-trip transformation test, (4) Software/simulation cross-verification, (5) Physical measurement using teach pendant. - **Unit Consistency:** Use **mm** for position consistently throughout a single problem — mixing mm and m is a silent error. - **Compound Rotations:** Order matters; intrinsic vs. extrinsic conventions must be explicitly stated. - **Lab Tolerance:** Numerical agreement within $\pm 0.01$ mm is considered verified; deviation beyond this indicates calculation or assembly error. - **Practical Industrial Use:** Transformation matrices are the foundation of every robot controller's coordinate system; they enable offline programming, path planning, collision avoidance, and vision-guided manipulation in modern smart factories (Industry 4.0).

topics.title: Robot programming and simulation for colour identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Robot programming and simulation for colour identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Programming and Simulation for Colour Identification** is a robotic vision experiment in which a simulated manipulator (or mobile robot) is integrated with a **machine vision sensor** to detect, classify, and act upon objects based on their **spectral reflectance characteristics** (colour). The robot employs an **RGB/HSV-based image processing pipeline** combined with **threshold segmentation** and **coordinate transformation** algorithms to localize coloured targets in its workspace and execute tasks such as **pick-and-place**, **sorting**, or **binning**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PCPEL607*, this experiment is a part of the **Robot Vision and Sensor Integration** cluster under Module 2 (Robot Programming and Simulation). It tests the following Course Outcomes (CO):

- **CO3:** *Simulate and program robotic systems for typical industrial applications using industry-standard tools.*
- **CO4:** *Integrate vision and sensor feedback into robotic workflows.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sorting Factory"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a quality-control inspector in a chocolate factory.** A worker visually scans boxes rolling on a conveyor — red boxes go to *Box A*, blue boxes to *Box B*, and green boxes to *Bin C*. Now replace the human eyes with a **camera**, the brain with an **image-processing algorithm**, and the arms with a **robotic gripper**. That is precisely colour identification in robotics.

The robot does *not* "see" colour the way humans do. It receives a **two-dimensional matrix of numerical intensity values** (pixels) from its vision sensor. The software must translate these numerical patterns into discrete colour categories and translate pixel coordinates into **world coordinates** that the robot's controller can act upon.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Meaning | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Vision Sensor** | A 2D (or 3D) camera returning pixel arrays | Provides raw input data |
| **RGB Colour Space** | Red, Green, Blue additive model | Native camera output |
| **HSV Colour Space** | Hue, Saturation, Value | Robust to lighting changes |
| **Thresholding** | Pixel-value filtering for segmentation | Extracts target colour |
| **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** | Joint-angle computation from end-effector pose | Drives robot to target |
| **Pick-and-Place** | Standardized material-handling task | Common industrial benchmark |
| **Workspace Mapping** | Pixel-to-world coordinate transform | Closes the perception-action loop |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Colour Identification Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

The complete experimental flow is divided into **five canonical stages**. Each stage must be implemented and demonstrated in the lab record.

### Stage 1 — Scene Capture
The vision sensor (mounted either *eye-in-hand* on the gripper or *fixed overhead*) acquires a 2D image matrix $I(x,y)$ of size $H \times W \times 3$ (Height × Width × Colour Channels).

### Stage 2 — Colour Space Conversion
Raw RGB is highly sensitive to ambient illumination. The image is transformed into the **HSV colour space**, where the **Hue channel** is largely invariant to brightness changes. This is the single most important step that distinguishes a *robust* implementation from a *fragile* one.

### Stage 3 — Threshold Segmentation (Colour Filtering)
Each pixel is tested against a predefined **Hue range** $[H_{min}, H_{max}]$ that corresponds to the target colour (e.g., Red: $0^\circ - 10^\circ$ or $170^\circ - 180^\circ$; Blue: $200^\circ - 250^\circ$; Green: $90^\circ - 130^\circ$). Pixels within range are marked **white (255)**; the rest are marked **black (0)** to form a **binary mask**.

### Stage 4 — Centroid Localization
The centre of mass of the white pixel cluster is computed. This gives the **(u, v)** pixel coordinates of the detected object's centroid.

### Stage 5 — Coordinate Transformation & Robot Action
Using a pre-calibrated **homography matrix** $H_{3\times3}$, the pixel coordinates are projected onto the **robot's world frame**. The controller then solves the **Inverse Kinematics** problem to drive the end-effector to that pose, grasp the object, and place it in the corresponding colour bin.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Colour Identification Experiment

| # | Equation / Rule | Purpose / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{max} = \max(R,G,B)$ | Largest RGB channel; defines Value (V) |
| 2 | $C_{min} = \min(R,G,B)$ | Smallest RGB channel; defines $\Delta$ |
| 3 | $\Delta = C_{max} - C_{min}$ | Chroma spread; $\Delta = 0 \Rightarrow$ greyscale |
| 4 | $V = C_{max} / 255$ | Normalized brightness; $V \in [0,1]$ |
| 5 | $S = \Delta / C_{max}$ (if $C_{max} \neq 0$) | Saturation; $S = 0$ for greyscale |
| 6 | $H = 60^\circ \cdot \left( \frac{G-B}{\Delta} \bmod 6 \right)$ if $C_{max}=R$ | Hue when red dominates |
| 7 | $H = 60^\circ \cdot \left( \frac{B-R}{\Delta} + 2 \right)$ if $C_{max}=G$ | Hue when green dominates |
| 8 | $H = 60^\circ \cdot \left( \frac{R-G}{\Delta} + 4 \right)$ if $C_{max}=B$ | Hue when blue dominates |
| 9 | $S_{binary}(u,v) = \begin{cases} 255 & \text{if } H_{min} \le H(u,v) \le H_{max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Threshold mask generation |
| 10 | $u_c = \frac{\sum_{(u,v) \in M} u \cdot S_{binary}(u,v)}{\sum_{(u,v) \in M} S_{binary}(u,v)}$ | Centroid X-coordinate |
| 11 | $v_c = \frac{\sum_{(u,v) \in M} v \cdot S_{binary}(u,v)}{\sum_{(u,v) \in M} S_{binary}(u,v)}$ | Centroid Y-coordinate |
| 12 | $\begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = s \cdot \mathbf{K}^{-1} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} u_c \\ v_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Pixel-to-world transform (pin-hole model) |
| 13 | $\mathbf{q} = IK(\mathbf{X}_w)$ | Inverse Kinematics — joint angle vector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This pipeline is the literal backbone of every *smart warehouse* (Amazon Kiva robots), *agricultural sorter* (Tomato grading machines), and *pharmaceutical bin-picker* in the industry. Mastering this experiment directly prepares the student for roles in **Computer Vision, Robotics Process Automation (RPA), and Industry 4.0 systems**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RGB to HSV Conversion (The Math Behind the Mask)

The Hue, Saturation and Value model is derived from the **hexagonal colour cone** geometry. Given normalized RGB values where $R, G, B \in [0,1]$:

**Step 1:** Identify the dominant channel.

$$
C_{max} = \max(R,G,B)
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the minimum channel.

$$
C_{min} = \min(R,G,B)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the chroma (range).

$$
\Delta = C_{max} - C_{min}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the Value (brightness axis).

$$
V = C_{max}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the Saturation. This is the *colourfulness* relative to brightness.

$$
S = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } C_{max} = 0 \\ \dfrac{\Delta}{C_{max}} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the Hue angle. The piecewise formulation handles which colour dominates.

$$
H = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \Delta = 0 \\[6pt] 60^\circ \cdot \left( \dfrac{G-B}{\Delta} \bmod 6 \right) & \text{if } C_{max} = R \\[6pt] 60^\circ \cdot \left( \dfrac{B-R}{\Delta} + 2 \right) & \text{if } C_{max} = G \\[6pt] 60^\circ \cdot \left( \dfrac{R-G}{\Delta} + 4 \right) & \text{if } C_{max} = B \end{cases}
$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**

> *Given* $R = 200, G = 50, B = 50$ (a red pixel), with normalization divide by 255:
> $R' = 0.784, G' = 0.196, B' = 0.196$

**Step 1:** $C_{max} = 0.784$ (Red dominates).

**Step 2:** $C_{min} = 0.196$.

**Step 3:** $\Delta = 0.784 - 0.196 = 0.588$.

**Step 4:** $V = 0.784$ → high brightness.

**Step 5:** $S = 0.588 / 0.784 = 0.75$ → vivid.

**Step 6:** Since $C_{max} = R$, use the first formula:

$$
H = 60 \cdot \left( \frac{0.196 - 0.196}{0.588} \bmod 6 \right) = 60 \cdot 0 = 0^\circ
$$

> **Interpretation:** Hue $0^\circ$ = pure Red. Verified.

## 3.2 Derivation: Centroid Computation for Object Localization

After binary thresholding, a connected white region $M$ represents the detected object. The centroid $(\bar{u}, \bar{v})$ — also called the *first-order image moment* — is computed as:

**Step 1:** Define the binary mask.

$$
M(u,v) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if pixel }(u,v) \text{ is target colour} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the zeroth moment (area).

$$
M_{00} = \sum_{u}\sum_{v} M(u,v)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute first-order moments.

$$
M_{10} = \sum_{u}\sum_{v} u \cdot M(u,v), \quad M_{01} = \sum_{u}\sum_{v} v \cdot M(u,v)
$$

**Step 4:** Compute centroid.

$$
\bar{u} = \frac{M_{10}}{M_{00}}, \quad \bar{v} = \frac{M_{01}}{M_{00}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The centroid is *translation-invariant* but *not rotation-invariant*. If objects are rotated on the conveyor, this still gives a stable pick-point — which is why it is preferred over corner-detection in bin-picking.

## 3.3 Derivation: Pixel-to-World Homography (Simplified Pin-Hole)

For an overhead camera looking vertically at the workspace plane, the transform is a 2D affine mapping (the $Z$-axis is constant):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = s \cdot \mathbf{H} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \bar{u} \\ \bar{v} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where $\mathbf{H}$ is the $3 \times 3$ homography matrix obtained via calibration (e.g., using a checkerboard of known dimensions). $s$ is the scale factor recovered from the third row of the product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the KTU lab viva**, you must be able to derive the **RGB→HSV** equations and the **centroid moment** equations. These are the two highest-weight derivations and are recurrent in university exam questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of the colour identification experiment, from scene capture to robot actuation. It represents a *block-level functional architecture* rather than a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scene Setup: Coloured Objects in Workspace] --> B[Vision Sensor: Image Capture I u v]
    B --> C[Pre-processing: Gaussian Blur and Noise Filter]
    C --> D[Colour Space Conversion: RGB to HSV]
    D --> E[Threshold Segmentation: Hue Range Filter]
    E --> F[Binary Mask Generation]
    F --> G[Connected Component Analysis]
    G --> H[Centroid Computation u-bar v-bar]
    H --> I[Pixel to World Transform H Matrix]
    I --> J[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    J --> K[Robot Trajectory Planning]
    K --> L[Gripper Actuation: Pick]
    L --> M[Colour Bin Selection Logic]
    M --> N[Place Action at Target Bin]
    N --> O[Return to Home Pose]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style G fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
    style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
    style I fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
    style J fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b
    style K fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b
    style L fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b
    style M fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#5e35b1
    style N fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#5e35b1
    style O fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#5e35b1
```

## 4.2 Decision Logic for Colour Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Detected Hue Value H] --> Q1{Is H between 0 and 10 OR between 170 and 180?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| RedClass[Class: RED -> Bin A]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is H between 90 and 130?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| GreenClass[Class: GREEN -> Bin B]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is H between 200 and 250?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| BlueClass[Class: BLUE -> Bin C]
    Q3 -->|No| UnknownClass[Class: UNKNOWN -> Reject]

    style Start fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style RedClass fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#c62828
    style GreenClass fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#2e7d32
    style BlueClass fill:#90caf9,stroke:#1565c0
    style UnknownClass fill:#bdbdbd,stroke:#424242
```

## 4.3 Experimental Workstation Layout (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CamBlock[Vision Subsystem]
        Cam[Vision Sensor / Webcam]
        Lens[Lens and Lighting]
    end

    subgraph ProcBlock[Processing Unit]
        PC[Simulation Software CoppeliaSim or RoboDK]
        Algo[Image Processing Algorithm]
    end

    subgraph RobotBlock[Robot Subsystem]
        Arm[6-DOF Robotic Arm]
        Grip[Parallel Gripper]
    end

    subgraph WorkBlock[Workspace]
        Obj[Coloured Cubes Red Green Blue]
        Bin1[Bin A]
        Bin2[Bin B]
        Bin3[Bin C]
    end

    Lens --> Cam
    Cam --> PC
    PC --> Algo
    Algo --> Arm
    Arm --> Grip
    Grip <--> Obj
    Grip --> Bin1
    Grip --> Bin2
    Grip --> Bin3

    style CamBlock fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style ProcBlock fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style RobotBlock fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style WorkBlock fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Viva:** When asked *"Explain the system architecture"*, trace the diagram from `Cam → PC → Arm → Gripper → Object` and explicitly state that this is a **closed-loop perception-action system**, where the vision sensor provides feedback that re-localizes the object in case of misalignment.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Module 2]`
**Why is the HSV colour space preferred over the RGB colour space for robot colour identification under varying industrial lighting?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In RGB colour space, the three channels are **coupled** — any change in illumination simultaneously alters R, G and B values, making it difficult to set a fixed threshold that works under different lighting conditions. In HSV, the **Hue (H) channel** encodes the pure colour information and is largely **invariant to brightness variations**. The Value (V) channel absorbs the illumination changes separately. Therefore, by thresholding only the Hue channel, the robot can robustly identify red, green, and blue objects even when factory lighting fluctuates. *(Award full 3 marks if the student mentions "Hue is lighting-invariant" and "RGB channels are coupled".)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 2]`
**Define the term "centroid" in the context of robot vision and state its significance in pick-and-place operations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The centroid $(\bar{u}, \bar{v})$ of a segmented object is the **arithmetic mean of all pixel coordinates** belonging to that object, computed using **image moments**:

$$
\bar{u} = \frac{M_{10}}{M_{00}}, \quad \bar{v} = \frac{M_{01}}{M_{00}}
$$

Its significance is that it provides a **single, stable, rotation-invariant 2D reference point** on the object. The robot uses this point as the **gripper target** during pick-and-place, avoiding the need for complex feature extraction. It is also computationally cheap — making it suitable for **real-time** conveyor-tracking applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model QP]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete **robot vision pipeline** for identifying and sorting coloured objects on a conveyor. Explain the function of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **RGB-to-HSV conversion equations** with all boundary conditions. Compute the HSV values for a pixel with $R=100, G=200, B=50$. Identify which colour this pixel represents. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A(a) [7 Marks]

The robot vision pipeline for colour-based sorting consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Image Acquisition:** A vision sensor (mounted overhead or on the robot wrist) captures a 2D image of the workspace containing coloured objects. The output is an $H \times W \times 3$ RGB matrix. *(1 mark)*

2. **Pre-processing:** A Gaussian blur (kernel size $5\times5$ typically) is applied to suppress sensor noise. Optionally, histogram equalization may be applied to normalize contrast. *(1 mark)*

3. **Colour Space Conversion:** RGB is converted to HSV using the standard piecewise formulas. This decouples chromaticity (Hue) from illumination (Value). *(1 mark)*

4. **Threshold Segmentation:** A binary mask is generated by applying a Hue range filter corresponding to the target colour. Pixels within the range are marked white. *(1 mark)*

5. **Morphological Operations:** Erosion and dilation are applied to remove salt-and-pepper noise and fill small holes in the mask. *(1 mark)*

6. **Connected Component Analysis & Centroid:** The largest connected white region is identified. Its centroid $(\bar{u}, \bar{v})$ is computed via image moments. *(1 mark)*

7. **Coordinate Transformation & Robot Action:** The centroid is mapped to world coordinates using a pre-calibrated homography matrix. Inverse kinematics generates joint angles for the robot to move the gripper above the object, descend, grasp, lift, and place it in the appropriate colour-coded bin. *(1 mark)*

---

### Model Answer — Question A(b) [7 Marks]

**Derivation** *(4 marks — equations + boundary conditions)*:

The conversion is governed by the standard formulas:

$$
C_{max} = \max(R,G,B), \quad C_{min} = \min(R,G,B)
$$

$$
\Delta = C_{max} - C_{min}
$$

$$
V = \frac{C_{max}}{255}
$$

$$
S = \frac{\Delta}{C_{max}} \quad (\text{if } C_{max} \neq 0, \text{ else } S=0)
$$

$$
H = \begin{cases} 0 & \Delta = 0 \\ 60 \cdot \frac{G-B}{\Delta} \bmod 6 & C_{max}=R \\ 60 \cdot \left(\frac{B-R}{\Delta} + 2\right) & C_{max}=G \\ 60 \cdot \left(\frac{R-G}{\Delta} + 4\right) & C_{max}=B \end{cases}
$$

**Numerical Computation** *(3 marks)*:

Given $R=100, G=200, B=50$. Normalize: $R'=0.392, G'=0.784, B'=0.196$.

- $C_{max} = 0.784$ (Green dominates)
- $C_{min} = 0.196$
- $\Delta = 0.784 - 0.196 = 0.588$
- $V = 0.784$
- $S = 0.588 / 0.784 = 0.750$
- Since $C_{max}=G$, apply the second formula:

$$
H = 60 \cdot \left( \frac{0.196 - 0.392}{0.588} + 2 \right) = 60 \cdot \left( -0.333 + 2 \right) = 60 \cdot 1.667 = 100^\circ
$$

**Final HSV: $(H, S, V) = (100^\circ, 0.75, 0.78)$** — Hue of **100°** falls in the **Green band (90°–130°)**. Therefore, the pixel represents **GREEN**. *(1 mark for identification)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model QP — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **inverse kinematics problem** in the context of a 6-DOF robot arm performing a colour-sorted pick-and-place. State why IK is necessary after the vision system computes the world coordinates of the object. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe a **calibration procedure** to obtain the homography matrix $\mathbf{H}$ that maps image pixel coordinates $(u,v)$ to the robot's world coordinates $(X_w, Y_w)$. Why is calibration a one-time offline step rather than a per-frame operation? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B(a) [7 Marks]

The vision system outputs the object's centroid in pixel coordinates $(u_c, v_c)$. After applying the calibrated homography, the object is localized in **world coordinates** $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$. However, the robot controller does **not** accept Cartesian world coordinates directly — it commands **joint angles** $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6]^T$ for each of the six motors.

**Inverse Kinematics (IK)** is the mathematical process of computing these joint angles from a desired end-effector pose. Formally:

$$
\mathbf{q} = IK(\mathbf{X}_{desired})
$$

For a 6-DOF arm, this is typically solved using one of three approaches:

1. **Analytical (Closed-form) Solution:** Uses the DH parameters of the arm to derive algebraic expressions. Fast but arm-geometry-specific. *(1 mark)*
2. **Jacobian-based Iterative Solution:** Uses the relation $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ to iteratively refine joint angles until the end-effector converges to the target. *(1 mark)*
3. **Numerical Optimization:** Minimizes the error $\vert\vert \mathbf{X}_{current} - \mathbf{X}_{desired} \vert\vert$ using algorithms like Levenberg–Marquardt. *(1 mark)*

**Why IK is necessary:** *(2 marks)*
- **Mapping:** Vision gives Cartesian pose; motors need angular commands.
- **Reachability:** IK can detect if a target is outside the workspace.
- **Singularity Avoidance:** IK can be augmented with constraints to avoid configurations where the Jacobian loses rank.
- **Smooth Motion:** IK enables trajectory planning in joint space, ensuring smooth motion profiles.

IK is necessary because vision perception and robot actuation operate in **mathematically different coordinate frames** (pixel/task space vs. joint space). *(1 mark)*

---

### Model Answer — Question B(b) [7 Marks]

**Calibration Procedure** *(5 marks)*:

1. **Place a checkerboard pattern of known square size (e.g., 30 mm)** in the workspace plane that the camera overlooks. *(1 mark)*
2. **Capture an image** of the checkerboard from the overhead vision sensor.
3. **Detect the inner corners** of the checkerboard using the Harris corner detector or OpenCV's `findChessboardCorners`. This yields precise sub-pixel coordinates $(u_i, v_i)$ for $i = 1 \ldots N$. *(1 mark)*
4. **Record world coordinates** $(X_i, Y_i)$ of the same corners in the robot's base frame using a teaching pendant or by manually measuring the checkerboard placement. *(1 mark)*
5. **Solve the linear system** for the homography matrix $\mathbf{H}$. Each correspondence gives two equations:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} -u_i & -v_i & -1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & u_i X_i & v_i X_i & X_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & -u_i & -v_i & -1 & u_i Y_i & v_i Y_i & Y_i \end{bmatrix} \cdot \mathbf{h} = 0
$$

Where $\mathbf{h}$ is the 9-element vectorized form of $\mathbf{H}$. With $\ge 4$ correspondences, solve via **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)**. *(1 mark)*
6. **Validate** the homography by computing the **re-projection error** — the mean pixel distance between predicted and observed corners. A good calibration has error $< 1$ pixel. *(1 mark)*

**Why calibration is offline (not per-frame)?** *(2 marks)*

- **Geometric Invariance:** The relative pose between the camera and the robot base does not change once the workstation is set up. The intrinsic camera parameters (focal length, principal point) and extrinsic parameters (camera position relative to the robot base) are **constants of the physical setup**.
- **Computational Cost:** SVD on a large matrix is expensive; performing it on every video frame (typically 30 fps) would consume all available CPU budget, leaving no time for segmentation, IK, and motion control.
- **Accuracy:** Multi-image calibration (averaging over many checkerboard poses) suppresses measurement noise, yielding a more accurate $\mathbf{H}$ than any single online estimate could provide.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **HSV Hue Range Mistakes:** Many students forget that **Red wraps around the 0°/360° boundary**, so the red range is **either** $[0°,10°]$ **or** $[170°,180°]$. Missing this dual range is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **RGB→HSV Normalization:** If a student forgets to divide R, G, B by 255 before computing Hue, the resulting Hue will be a large meaningless number. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for un-normalized input.
> 3. **Confusing Centroid with Bounding-Box Centre:** The centroid is the *pixel-mass-weighted* centre, not the geometric centre of the bounding box. They coincide only for perfectly convex, symmetric objects. Use the correct moment formula.
> 4. **Homography vs. Perspective Projection:** Students often confuse the simplified 2D affine homography with the full 3D pin-hole projection. For an **overhead, vertical-view** camera, the 2D homography is valid. For tilted cameras, you must use the full projection matrix. Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Viva Trap — "Why not just use RGB directly?":** The correct answer is that Hue is **lighting-invariant**, not that RGB is "wrong". Examiners look for this specific phrasing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)**

- **Definition:** Colour identification in robotics = vision sensor + image processing + IK + actuation.
- **Pipeline Order:** Capture → Blur → RGB→HSV → Threshold → Morphology → Centroid → Homography → IK → Pick → Place.
- **HSV over RGB:** Hue is invariant to illumination; this is the core robustness argument.
- **Hue Bands (Memorize):** Red = $[0°,10°] \cup [170°,180°]$; Yellow = $[20°,40°]$; Green = $[90°,130°]$; Blue = $[200°,250°]$.
- **Conversion Equations:** Three piecewise formulas for $H$ depending on whether $R$, $G$ or $B$ is $C_{max}$. $\Delta=0 \Rightarrow$ greyscale.
- **Centroid Formula:** $\bar{u} = M_{10}/M_{00}$, $\bar{v} = M_{01}/M_{00}$ — first-order image moments.
- **Homography:** A $3\times3$ matrix mapping pixel → world. Calibrated offline using checkerboard + SVD.
- **IK Necessity:** Vision outputs Cartesian pose; motors need joint angles. Three methods: Analytical, Jacobian-based, Numerical Optimization.
- **Software Tools (KTU Lab):** CoppeliaSim (V-REP), RoboDK, MATLAB Image Processing Toolbox, OpenCV-Python.
- **Typical Hardware:** 6-DOF robotic arm (e.g., ABB IRB, KUKA KR, or simulated DOBOT), parallel-jaw gripper, RGB webcam (5 MP minimum), diffuse LED lighting.
- **Common Viva Questions:** "Why HSV over RGB?", "Why centroid and not corners?", "Why offline calibration?", "What if two objects of the same colour overlap?" — *Answer: Apply connected-component analysis and pick the largest cluster, or use depth sensing (RGB-D camera) to disambiguate.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open and closed loop control of AC and DC drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Open and closed loop control of AC and DC drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module Experiment 2: Open and Closed Loop Control of AC and DC Drives

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open Loop Control of Drives:** A control architecture in which the controller (e.g., a rectifier firing circuit or VFD reference input) commands the power converter based purely on a *reference setpoint* without measuring the actual motor output (speed/torque). There is **no feedback path** from the process variable to the controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed Loop Control of Drives:** A control architecture in which the actual motor speed (or torque) is continuously measured by a **tachogenerator / encoder**, compared against the reference value, and the resulting **error signal** is fed to a controller (typically **PI / PID**). The controller dynamically adjusts the converter output so that the motor follows the reference despite load disturbances or parameter variations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Cruise Control Story**
> - **Open loop (no feedback):** You are driving a car and you *blindly press the accelerator to a fixed position* expecting a constant speed. When you start climbing a hill, the car *slows down* — and you do nothing about it. The throttle angle is fixed regardless of what the car actually does.
> - **Closed loop (with feedback):** You have **cruise control ON**. The car *measures* its actual speed via the wheel sensors, *compares* it with the setpoint (say 80 km/h), and *automatically opens/closes the throttle* to maintain 80 km/h even when the road inclines. The speed sensor is the **feedback element**, the ECU is the **PI controller**, and the throttle is the **actuator**.

### 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in the Lab

| Aspect | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | None | Tachogenerator / Encoder |
| Accuracy | Poor under load | High, regulated |
| Disturbance rejection | None | Excellent |
| Hardware complexity | Simple (potentiometer + SCR firing) | Higher (sensor + PI op-amp card) |
| Typical use | Fan, blower, pump (constant load) | Lathe, conveyor, mill, robotic joint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A very common 3-mark question is *"Differentiate between open loop and closed loop control of DC drive."* Memorise the table above; it is the *exact valuation key* style.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DC Drive — Mathematical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Armature Voltage Control (Most Common Method)

The separately excited DC motor is governed by the **armature voltage equation** and the **mechanical (torque) equation**:

**Electrical side (armature circuit):**
$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt}$$

**Back-EMF (Faraday's law for rotating machine):**
$$E_b = K \phi \omega$$

**Mechanical side (Newton's 2nd law for rotation):**
$$T_e - T_L = J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega$$

**Electromagnetic torque developed:**
$$T_e = K \phi I_a$$

### 2.1.2 The Two Control Loops in a DC Drive

1. **Inner current loop (torque loop):** Faster (≈ 1–5 ms). Regulates $I_a$ by controlling $V_a$ via the SCR bridge firing angle $\alpha$.
2. **Outer speed loop:** Slower (≈ 10–50 ms). Regulates $\omega$ by generating the $I_a^*$ reference from a PI controller.

### 2.2 The AC Drive — V/f Control (Scalar Control)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volts-per-Hertz (V/f) Law:** To keep the air-gap flux $\phi$ constant at its rated value across the entire speed range, the **stator voltage must be varied in direct proportion to the supply frequency**.

$$\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant} = \frac{V_{\text{rated}}}{f_{\text{rated}}}$$

If $V$ is held constant and $f$ is increased, flux $\phi \propto V/f$ **decreases**, leading to:
- Reduced torque capability $T \propto \phi I$
- Poor speed regulation under load
- Risk of **field-weakening** instability above base speed

### 2.3 The PI Controller (Workhorse of Industrial Drives)

The **Proportional-Integral** controller transfer function:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = K_p \left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s}\right)$$

Where:
- $K_p$ = Proportional gain (instantaneous corrective action)
- $K_i$ = Integral gain (eliminates steady-state error)
- $T_i = K_p / K_i$ = Integral time constant

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PI and not just P?** A pure P-controller always leaves a *steady-state error* (offset). The **integrator** accumulates the error over time and drives it to **zero** — critical for accurate speed holding in industrial drives.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it covers ~80% of the numerical part of any KTU exam question on this experiment.**

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Back-EMF | $E_b = K \phi \omega$ | Volts; $K$ in V·s/rad |
| 2 | Motor torque | $T_e = K \phi I_a$ | N·m |
| 3 | Armature voltage | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Steady-state, $L_a$ ignored |
| 4 | Speed (DC motor) | $\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \phi}$ | rad/s |
| 5 | V/f ratio | $V / f = \text{const}$ | V/Hz, must be held constant |
| 6 | PI controller | $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$ | Error-driven output |
| 7 | Closed-loop speed | $\omega = \omega_{\text{ref}} \left[ 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right]$ | First-order response |
| 8 | Slip (induction motor) | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Per-unit, 0 to 1 |
| 9 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | RPM; $P$ = number of poles |
| 10 | Steady-state error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s)$ | Zero with PI for step input |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC drives (closed loop):** Used in **paper mills, rolling mills, elevators, traction systems, machine tool spindles** where precise speed and torque control are critical.
- **AC V/f drives (closed loop):** Used in **HVAC blowers, conveyor belts, pumps, cranes** where the robust squirrel-cage induction motor is preferred and moderate accuracy is acceptable.
- **Vector-controlled AC drives (advanced):** Used in **servo applications, robotics (CNC, pick-place)** where dynamic response rivals DC drives.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Open-Loop Speed Equation of a Separately Excited DC Motor

**Step 1 — Start from the fundamental armature KVL** (ignoring armature inductance $L_a$ for steady state):

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the back-EMF expression** $E_b = K \phi \omega$:

$$V_a = K \phi \omega + I_a R_a$$

**Step 3 — Solve for angular speed $\omega$** by rearranging:

$$K \phi \omega = V_a - I_a R_a$$

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \phi}}$$

> **Logic behind the step:** The applied voltage $V_a$ must overcome *two* opposing quantities — (a) the back-EMF (which represents the motor acting as a generator opposing the source), and (b) the resistive drop $I_a R_a$. The "useful remainder" is what produces mechanical rotation.

**Step 4 — Convert to RPM** (since lab instruments usually display RPM):

$$N = \frac{60 \, \omega}{2 \pi} = \frac{60}{2 \pi K \phi}\left(V_a - I_a R_a\right)$$

**Step 5 — Observe the sensitivity.** Taking partial derivative of $N$ with respect to $I_a$:

$$\frac{\partial N}{\partial I_a} = -\frac{60 \, R_a}{2 \pi K \phi} < 0$$

> **Key conclusion:** As load current $I_a$ rises, speed $N$ **drops linearly** — this is called **load regulation** or **droop**. In **open loop**, nothing compensates for this drop → hence poor speed regulation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Loop Speed Regulation with PI Controller

**Step 1 — Define the error signal** $e(t)$ between reference speed and actual speed:

$$e(t) = \omega_{\text{ref}} - \omega_{\text{actual}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the PI control law:**

$$V_a^*(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Laplace transform** (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$V_a^*(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) E(s) = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s} E(s)$$

**Step 4 — The closed-loop transfer function** (with motor plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}$ and unity feedback):

$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{G_c(s) \, G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) \, G_p(s)}$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state error for a step reference** $\omega_{\text{ref}} = \Omega_0 / s$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{\Omega_0 / s}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} = \frac{\Omega_0}{1 + G_c(0) G_p(0)}$$

> **Since the PI controller has an integrator, $G_c(0) = K_i / 0 \to \infty$**, therefore $\boxed{e_{ss} = 0}$. This is the **fundamental justification** for using PI control in drives.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Tuning the PI Controller for a DC Drive

> [!NOTE]
> **Ziegler-Nichols Open-Loop Method** (commonly expected in KTU viva):

| Step | Action | Calculation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Disconnect PI; apply a small step $V_a$ | Record $\omega(t)$ |
| 2 | Measure process gain $K$ | $K = \Delta \omega_{\text{ss}} / \Delta V_a$ |
| 3 | Draw tangent at inflection point of $\omega(t)$ | Find $L$ (dead time) and $T$ (time constant) |
| 4 | Compute PI gains | $K_p = 0.9 \, T / (K L)$; $T_i = 3 L$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Open Loop DC Drive (Armature Control)

> [!NOTE]
> The student should be able to sketch this from memory in the exam. Use **block-level functional architecture** (Mermaid-safe).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> B[Step Down Transformer]
    B --> C[Full Wave SCR Bridge Rectifier]
    D[Firing Circuit with Potentiometer Reference] -->|Firing Pulses| C
    C -->|Variable DC Va| E[DC Motor Armature]
    F[DC Supply for Field Winding] -->|Constant Vf| G[Field Winding]
    E -->|Mechanical Shaft| H[Load]
    style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

> **Key point to write in the record:** In the open-loop case, the potentiometer sets the firing angle $\alpha$ *manually*. The speed is not sensed.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed Loop DC Drive (with PI Speed Controller)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Speed Setpoint] -->|+| B[Summing Junction]
    C[Tachogenerator Feedback] -->|-| B
    B -->|Error e| D[PI Controller]
    D -->|Ia ref| E[Current Controller Inner Loop]
    E -->|Firing Angle alpha| F[SCR Converter]
    F -->|Variable DC Va| G[DC Motor]
    G -->|Shaft| H[Load]
    G -->|Speed| I[Tachogenerator Sensor]
    I --> C
    style D fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

> **Two nested loops** are clearly visible: an *inner current loop* (fast) and an *outer speed loop* (slower). The **tachogenerator** is the feedback transducer.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Open Loop AC Drive (V/f Control)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Frequency Reference Knob] --> B[Reference Oscillator VCO]
    B -->|Control Signal| C[PWM Inverter IGBT Bridge]
    D[DC Bus from Rectifier] --> C
    C -->|Variable V Variable f| E[3 Phase Induction Motor]
    E --> F[Mechanical Load]
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

> **Lab note:** The PWM inverter generates a *PWM-modulated sine wave*. By holding the **V/f ratio constant**, the flux remains nominal and torque capability is preserved.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Closed Loop AC Drive (V/f with Speed Feedback)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Speed Setpoint] -->|+| B[Summing Junction]
    C[Encoder Pulse Feedback] -->|-| B
    B -->|Error| D[PI Speed Controller]
    D -->|f ref and V ref V proportional to f| E[SPWM Generator]
    E -->|Gate Pulses| F[IGBT Inverter]
    F -->|3 Phase PWM AC| G[Induction Motor]
    G --> H[Load]
    G -->|Shaft Speed| I[Optical Encoder]
    I --> C
    style D fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

## 4.5 Typical Lab Setup (Functional Architecture View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LabBench[LABORATORY BENCH SETUP]
        P1[Variac / Autotransformer] --> P2[Power Converter SCR or IGBT]
        P2 --> P3[DC or AC Motor]
        P3 --> P4[Mechanical Loading Arrangement]
        P3 --> P5[Tachogenerator / Encoder]
        P5 --> P6[Signal Conditioning Unit]
        P6 --> P7[PI Controller Op Amp Card]
        P7 --> P2
        M1[Digital Multimeter] -.-> P2
        M2[CRO or DSO] -.-> P2
        M3[RPM Tachometer] -.-> P3
    end
    style P7 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333
    style P5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style P3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between open loop and closed loop control of a DC drive. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback element | Absent | Tachogenerator / Encoder present |
| Speed accuracy | Poor, varies with load | High, regulated within ±1% |
| Disturbance rejection | None | Excellent via PI action |
| Steady-state error | Non-zero, depends on load | Zero (with integral action) |
| Complexity & cost | Low | Higher |

*(1 mark per key difference. Examiner allocates 1 mark for accuracy of any one row, 1 mark for second row, 1 mark for the third — write at least 3 valid differences.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is V/f ratio maintained constant in AC drive control? What happens if it is not maintained? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The air-gap flux $\phi$ in an induction motor is proportional to $V/f$ (derived from $E/f = 4.44 \, f N \phi$). To retain **rated flux** and hence **rated torque capability** at every operating point, the ratio $V/f$ is held constant throughout the speed range below base speed.

If $V/f$ is **not maintained constant:**
- If $V/f$ *decreases* (over-frequencing) → flux weakens → torque reduces → motor may stall.
- If $V/f$ *increases* (over-volting at low frequency) → flux saturates → excessive magnetising current → overheating.

*(1 mark for the basic idea, 1 mark for the flux equation, 1 mark for consequence. Use the boxed equation to score full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Module 2 Choice Question]`

**a)** Draw the block diagram of a **closed-loop speed control of a separately excited DC motor** using a PI controller. Explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the **transfer function of the closed-loop system** and show that the steady-state error for a step change in speed reference is **zero** when a PI controller is used. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

Refer to **Section 4.2** (block diagram with PI controller). The student must reproduce a *labelled* block diagram showing:

1. **Speed reference** — input setpoint (potentiometer or DAC output). *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Summing junction** — subtraction of feedback. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Error amplifier / PI controller** — generates control signal $V_a^* = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt$. *(1 mark)*
4. **SCR / IGBT converter** — produces variable armature voltage $V_a$. *(1 mark)*
5. **DC motor (plant)** — converts electrical to mechanical, described by $G_p(s) = K_m / (\tau_m s + 1)$. *(1 mark)*
6. **Tachogenerator (feedback element)** — produces voltage proportional to speed, $V_t = K_t \omega$. *(1 mark)*
7. **Load** — disturbance input applied at motor shaft. *(1 mark — if discussed)*

*Total: 7 marks*

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Define plant and controller transfer functions.**

Let the motor (armature-controlled) be modelled as a first-order system:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}$$

Let the PI controller be:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

Let the feedback path be unity: $H(s) = 1$.

**Step 2 — Closed-loop transfer function.**

$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{G_c(s) G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) H(s)}$$

$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{\dfrac{(K_p s + K_i)}{s} \cdot \dfrac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}}{1 + \dfrac{(K_p s + K_i)}{s} \cdot \dfrac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(\tau_m s + 1)$:**

$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{K_m (K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau_m s + 1) + K_m (K_p s + K_i)}$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state error to a unit step input** $\Omega_0(s) = 1/s$:

$$E(s) = \frac{\Omega_0(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} = \frac{1/s}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

Apply final value theorem:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $G_c(s) G_p(s)$ at $s = 0$:**

$$G_c(0) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s} = \frac{K_i}{0} \to \infty$$

Therefore:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

*Mark split: 2 marks for writing the transfer function, 2 marks for the closed-loop expression, 2 marks for the final value theorem setup, 1 mark for the conclusion.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Module 2 Choice Question]`

**a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **working of a closed-loop V/f control of a three-phase induction motor drive**. Discuss the role of the encoder and PI controller. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A separately excited DC motor has $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $K \phi = 1.2 \, \text{V·s/rad}$. It is fed from a fully-controlled bridge rectifier with $V_s = 230 \, \text{V (rms)}$. For a firing angle $\alpha = 30^\circ$, calculate: **(i)** average armature voltage $V_a$, **(ii)** no-load speed in RPM, **(iii)** speed when the motor draws $I_a = 20 \, \text{A}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

Refer to **Section 4.4** (closed-loop V/f block diagram). The student must describe:

1. **Reference speed input** — frequency command. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Summing junction** — error generation. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **PI controller** — produces $f^*$ such that $V^* = (V/f)_{\text{rated}} \cdot f^*$. *(1.5 marks — explain the V/f law)*
4. **SPWM generator / sine-triangle modulator** — produces switching pulses with modulation index $m = V^* / V_{\text{tri}}$. *(1 mark)*
5. **IGBT inverter** — converts DC bus to 3-phase PWM AC. *(1 mark)*
6. **Induction motor** — mechanical output. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Encoder (feedback)** — produces $N$ pulses/revolution; frequency is proportional to speed. Feeds back to summing junction. *(2 marks — explain the role: 1 mark for signal generation, 1 mark for closing the loop)*

*Total: 7 marks*

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $K \phi = 1.2 \, \text{V·s/rad}$, $V_s = 230 \, \text{V (rms)}$, $\alpha = 30^\circ$.

**(i) Average armature voltage** $V_a$ (full converter output):

$$V_a = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha = \frac{2 \times \sqrt{2} \times 230}{\pi} \cos 30^\circ$$

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27 \, \text{V}$$

$$V_a = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \times 0.8660 = \frac{650.54 \times 0.8660}{\pi} = \frac{563.4}{\pi}$$

$$\boxed{V_a = 179.3 \, \text{V}}$$

**(ii) No-load speed** ($I_a \approx 0$):

$$\omega_{nl} = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \phi} = \frac{179.3 - 0}{1.2} = 149.4 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$N_{nl} = \frac{60 \times 149.4}{2 \pi} = \frac{60 \times 149.4}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{N_{nl} \approx 1427 \, \text{RPM}}$$

**(iii) Speed at $I_a = 20 \, \text{A}$:**

$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \phi} = \frac{179.3 - (20)(0.5)}{1.2} = \frac{179.3 - 10}{1.2} = \frac{169.3}{1.2}$$

$$\omega = 141.08 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$N = \frac{60 \times 141.08}{2 \pi}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 1347 \, \text{RPM}}$$

*Mark split: 1 mark each for (i) formula + substitution, (ii) no-load equation + answer, (iii) loaded equation + answer, plus 1 mark for unit conversions and final numerical accuracy (4 sub-parts, 7 marks).*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
