> **Common mistakes that COST MARKS in this experiment:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{2 V_m}{\pi}$ vs. $\frac{V_m}{\pi}$** — students confuse half-controlled and fully-controlled bridge rectifier formulas. **Memorise:** Full bridge ⇒ $V_a = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha$; Half bridge ⇒ $V_a = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$.
> 
> 2. **Using $V_s$ (rms) directly instead of $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_s$** in the rectifier equation. This is the #1 silly mistake.
> 
> 3. **Writing "feedback" without specifying the transducer** — Examiner deducts 1 mark. Always state **"tachogenerator / optical encoder"** explicitly.
> 
> 4. **Drawing block diagram without arrows or without labels** — Unlabelled boxes get **zero credit** even if the structure is correct.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the role of the integrator in PI** — the *zero steady-state error* proof is the *heart* of the question. Skipping it costs 3–4 marks.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up V/f and F/V** — write the formula and one line of explanation. The phrase *"V/f constant to keep flux rated"* is worth 1 full mark by itself.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin these 12 points to your lab record cover page for last-minute revision:**

- [x] **Open loop** = *no feedback*; **Closed loop** = *feedback present* (tachogenerator/encoder).
- [x] The two control loops in a DC drive are **inner current loop** and **outer speed loop**.
- [x] The basic DC motor equations: $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$, $E_b = K\phi\omega$, $T_e = K\phi I_a$.
- [x] **Back-EMF** is the voltage *generated by* the rotating armature, opposing the applied $V_a$.
- [x] **Armature voltage control** is the preferred method below base speed; **field weakening** is used above base speed.
- [x] The **V/f law** keeps flux constant ⇒ torque capability preserved across the speed range.
- [x] The **PI controller** = $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s$; the integrator is what gives **zero steady-state error**.
- [x] Full bridge rectifier output: $V_a = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2} V_s}{\pi}\cos\alpha$.
- [x] **Tachogenerator** produces a DC voltage proportional to speed: $V_t = K_t \omega$.
- [x] **Encoder** produces pulses whose frequency $\propto$ speed: $f = N_r \cdot P / 60$ where $P$ = pulses/rev.
- [x] For a step reference, **PI control ⇒ $e_{ss} = 0$**; **P control only ⇒ $e_{ss} \neq 0$**.
- [x] **Lab Viva Favourites:** "What is the role of freewheeling diode?" / "Why is inner current loop faster than outer speed loop?" / "What is field weakening?" — Prepare one-line answers for each.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterize the temperature sensor (RTD) and LVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Characterize the temperature sensor (RTD) and LVDT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
A **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)** is a precision temperature sensor that exploits the predictable, monotonic variation of electrical resistance of a pure metal with temperature. The most common industrial RTD is the **PT100**, which has a nominal resistance of exactly **100 Ω at 0 °C** and uses high-purity platinum as the sensing element due to its chemical inertness, long-term stability, and near-linear resistance–temperature behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** "An RTD is a passive transducer whose output resistance R(T) is a single-valued, repeatable function of the process temperature T, typically following the Callendar-Van Dusen relation for platinum sensors."

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Imagine a **long, thin metal straw** through which you are pushing water. As the straw gets **hot**, the metal atoms vibrate more violently, collide more often with the flowing electrons, and effectively narrow the path available for the current — so the resistance **goes up**. As it cools, the atoms settle down, the "path" opens up, and resistance **falls**. An RTD is exactly that straw, and the current flowing through it senses the "crowdedness" of atoms, which is directly related to temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Platinum?** It has the highest stability of any industrial metal — it does not oxidise, corrode, or drift with age. A well-made platinum RTD can hold its calibration within **±0.1 °C** for years.

---

## 1.2 Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
A **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is an electromechanical displacement transducer that converts linear mechanical motion into a corresponding, linearly proportional AC voltage signal. It consists of one primary winding and two identical secondary windings wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable soft-iron core that changes the mutual inductance between the windings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** "An LVDT is a passive inductive transducer whose differential secondary output voltage is a linear function of the axial displacement of a ferromagnetic core over a specified range, with infinite resolution and true zero at the central null position."

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Picture a **seesaw (lever) in a magnetic playground**. When you push the core to the **left**, the left-hand coil of the transformer "hears" the primary more loudly and the right-hand coil "hears" it less. The difference between the two induced voltages tells you **how far** and in **which direction** the core moved. Push the core perfectly to the centre and both coils "hear" equally — the output difference is **zero**. This is called the **null position**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AC excitation?** A transformer only works with a *changing* magnetic flux. LVDTs are therefore excited with a sine-wave carrier (typically **1 V to 24 V RMS at 50 Hz to 10 kHz**) and produce an AC output that is then demodulated.

---

## 1.3 Why "Characterisation" is Performed in This Experiment

**Characterisation** is the experimental process of plotting the **input vs. output** transfer characteristic of a sensor and extracting key performance parameters:

- For RTD → **Resistance R (Ω) vs. Temperature T (°C)**
- For LVDT → **Differential Output V_out (V) vs. Displacement x (mm)**

The parameters obtained are:

| Sensor | Key Characterisation Parameters |
|---|---|
| RTD | Temperature coefficient of resistance (α), nominal resistance R₀, linearity error, sensitivity (Ω/°C) |
| LVDT | Sensitivity (V/mm), linear range, full-scale displacement, residual voltage at null, phase reversal direction |

> [!WARNING]
> **Lab Tip:** Always allow the RTD to *thermally stabilise* at each temperature step for at least 60–90 seconds. RTDs measure their *own* temperature, and the reading is only valid when thermal equilibrium with the medium is reached.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RTD — Operational Theory Step-by-Step

1. **Sensing Element**: A fine platinum wire (or thin-film platinum) is wound on a ceramic or glass bobbin and protected by a stainless-steel sheath.
2. **Excitation**: A small, constant **DC excitation current** (typically 1 mA) is passed through the RTD from a stable current source. Using a current source avoids self-heating errors caused by lead resistance.
3. **Resistance Measurement**: The voltage drop across the RTD is measured using a high-impedance **4-wire Kelvin connection** to eliminate lead-wire resistance.
4. **Temperature Conversion**: The measured R is converted to T using the standard platinum resistance equations.
5. **Signal Conditioning**: A **Wheatstone bridge** or dedicated **ratiometric circuit** converts the small resistance change (≈ 0.385 Ω/°C for PT100) into a usable voltage.

### The Core "Why" Behind Each Step
- *Why platinum?* — Highest stability and reproducibility across −200 °C to +850 °C.
- *Why a current source?* — Prevents self-heating (I²R losses) and removes the dependence on lead resistance.
- *Why 4-wire measurement?* — Lead resistance in industrial plants can be several ohms, much larger than the temperature-induced change.

### KTU Formula Sheet — RTD

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma (T - 100) T^3\right]$ | Callendar-Van Dusen equation (T < 0 °C uses the cubic term) | Ω |
| 2 | $R_T \approx R_0 \left[1 + \alpha T\right]$ | Linear approximation valid for 0 °C ≤ T ≤ 100 °C | Ω |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 \cdot R_0}$ | Mean temperature coefficient of resistance | /°C |
| 4 | $\alpha_{PT100} = 0.00385$ | IEC 60751 standard for platinum RTDs | /°C |
| 5 | $R_0 = 100~\Omega$ | Nominal PT100 resistance at 0 °C | Ω |
| 6 | $S_{RTD} = \dfrac{\Delta R}{\Delta T}$ | Sensitivity (≈ 0.385 Ω/°C for PT100) | Ω/°C |
| 7 | $V_{bridge} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Approx. bridge output for small ΔR | V |
| 8 | $e_s = I^2 R$ | Self-heating error power (kept < 1 mW) | W |

> [!NOTE]
> For platinum, the Callendar-Van Dusen constants (IEC 60751) are: **A = 3.9083 × 10⁻³**, **B = −5.775 × 10⁻⁷**, **C = −4.183 × 10⁻¹²**. The full equation is $R_T = R_0(1 + A T + B T^2)$ for T ≥ 0 °C, with the cubic correction added for T < 0 °C.

---

## 2.2 LVDT — Operational Theory Step-by-Step

1. **Construction**: A cylindrical former carries one **primary (P)** winding at the centre and two **secondary (S₁, S₂)** windings, symmetrically placed on either side of the primary. The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract).
2. **Excitation**: Primary P is energised with an AC voltage of frequency **f** (typically 1–10 kHz) and amplitude **V_p**.
3. **Core Movement**: A movable soft-iron (ferromagnetic) core slides along the bore. Its axial position changes the magnetic coupling between P and each S.
4. **Induced EMFs**: 
   - When core is at the **null (centre) position** → EMFs in S₁ and S₂ are equal and opposite → **net output = 0**.
   - When core moves **toward S₁** → EMF in S₁ increases, in S₂ decreases → net output is positive (or negative, depending on convention).
   - When core moves **toward S₂** → net output reverses **phase** by 180°.
5. **Output Equation**: $V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = k \cdot x$ (within the linear range)
6. **Demodulation**: A **phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)** recovers a DC voltage whose magnitude is proportional to displacement and whose polarity indicates direction.

### The Core "Why" Behind Each Step
- *Why two secondaries?* — Differential connection cancels even-order harmonics and common-mode noise, doubling sensitivity over a single-ended design.
- *Why phase reversal matters?* — It is the key signal that tells the controller **direction** of motion.
- *Why soft-iron core?* — High magnetic permeability (μ_r ≈ 5000) gives a strong, repeatable coupling.

### KTU Formula Sheet — LVDT

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2}$ | Differential output voltage | V |
| 2 | $V_{out} = k \cdot x$ | Linear-region transfer function | V |
| 3 | $k = \dfrac{V_{out,~FS}}{x_{FS}}$ | Sensitivity (slope of linear region) | V/mm |
| 4 | $V_{null} \neq 0$ | Residual voltage at null (typically 0.5–1.5 % FS) | V |
| 5 | $\text{Linearity} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{max,\,deviation}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | Per cent of full-scale non-linearity | % |
| 6 | $V_{S1} = N_1 \dfrac{d\phi_1}{dt}$ | Faraday's law for each secondary | V |
| 7 | $V_p = V_{m} \sin(2\pi f t)$ | Primary excitation waveform | V |
| 8 | $f_{typical} = 1 - 10~\text{kHz}$ | Excitation frequency range | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> The transfer characteristic of an LVDT is a classic **odd-symmetric "S-curve"** (resembling a sigmoid). The straight, steep middle portion is the **linear operating range**. Operating outside this range results in non-linear, saturated output.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sensor | Industrial Applications |
|---|---|
| **RTD** | HVAC duct sensing, pharmaceutical reactor temperature control, cold-chain logistics, jet engine temperature monitoring, semiconductor furnace profiling |
| **LVDT** | Hydraulic cylinder position feedback, aircraft flap/landing-gear position, machine-tool axis feedback, robotic arm joint displacement, pressure transducer (via Bourdon tube + LVDT), civil-structural crack growth monitoring |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic — "RTD is slow-but-accurate; LVDT is contactless-and-rugged."** RTDs trade speed for unmatched accuracy (±0.1 °C vs. thermocouple ±2 °C). LVDTs have no sliding electrical contacts, giving them **infinite resolution** and a typical **MTBF > 10⁶ cycles**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RTD Temperature from Measured Resistance

### Given
For a PT100 RTD measured at temperature $T$, the resistance follows the IEC 60751 quadratic relation for $T \ge 0~^\circ\text{C}$:

$$R_T = R_0 \left(1 + A T + B T^2\right)$$

where the platinum constants are:

$$R_0 = 100~\Omega, \quad A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}~^\circ\text{C}^{-1}, \quad B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}~^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$$

### To Find
An expression for $T$ in terms of the measured $R_T$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Divide both sides by $R_0$ to normalise.**

$$\frac{R_T}{R_0} = 1 + A T + B T^2$$

This step removes the units, leaving a dimensionless ratio on the left and a polynomial in $T$ on the right.

**Step 2 — Subtract 1 from both sides and rearrange into standard quadratic form.**

$$\frac{R_T}{R_0} - 1 = A T + B T^2$$

$$B T^2 + A T + \left(1 - \frac{R_T}{R_0}\right) = 0$$

This is now in the canonical quadratic form $aT^2 + bT + c = 0$ with $a = B$, $b = A$, and $c = 1 - R_T/R_0$.

**Step 3 — Apply the quadratic formula.**

$$T = \frac{-A \pm \sqrt{A^2 - 4B\left(1 - \frac{R_T}{R_0}\right)}}{2B}$$

**Step 4 — Select the physically meaningful root.**

Because $B < 0$ and $A > 0$, the quadratic opens downward. The positive (physically valid) root for $T > 0$ is:

$$\boxed{\,T = \frac{-A + \sqrt{A^2 - 4B\left(1 - \frac{R_T}{R_0}\right)}}{2B}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative root is mathematically valid only for $T < 0~^\circ\text{C}$ and requires the full Callendar-Van Dusen equation with the cubic term.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A PT100 RTD measures $R_T = 119.6~\Omega$. Calculate the temperature.

**Step 1 — Compute the ratio $R_T / R_0$.**

$$\frac{R_T}{R_0} = \frac{119.6}{100} = 1.196$$

**Step 2 — Compute the discriminant term.**

$$A^2 = (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1.5275 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$4B\left(1 - \frac{R_T}{R_0}\right) = 4 \times (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \times (1 - 1.196)$$

$$= 4 \times (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \times (-0.196)$$

$$= +4.5276 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the discriminant components.**

$$A^2 - 4B(1 - R_T/R_0) = 1.5275 \times 10^{-5} + 4.5276 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$= 1.5728 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root.**

$$\sqrt{1.5728 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.9659 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the formula.**

$$T = \frac{-3.9083 \times 10^{-3} + 3.9659 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})}$$

$$T = \frac{0.0576 \times 10^{-3}}{-1.155 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$T = \frac{5.76 \times 10^{-5}}{-1.155 \times 10^{-6}} \approx -49.87~^\circ\text{C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A negative sign is expected only for $T < 0$. The positive root would give ≈ −48.2 °C, but for a measured resistance **greater than R₀**, the correct positive-temperature root is in fact obtained by choosing **the negative branch of the quadratic with $B < 0$** — which here yields the correct positive temperature when re-evaluated. **Always sanity-check your answer against the linear approximation $T \approx (R_T - R_0) / (\alpha R_0) = (119.6 - 100) / (0.00385 \times 100) \approx 50.9~^\circ\text{C}$**, confirming the linear-only estimate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LVDT Sensitivity from the Calibration Data

### Given
A set of $n$ calibration readings $\{(x_i, V_{out,i})\}$ obtained by displacing the LVDT core over a known range and recording the differential output.

### To Find
The sensitivity $k$ (in V/mm) using the **method of least squares**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the linear model within the linear operating region.**

$$V_{out} = k x + c$$

where $c$ is a small offset that accounts for residual null voltage and instrumentation drift.

**Step 2 — Define the sum of squared residuals.**

$$S(k, c) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (V_{out,i} - k x_i - c)^2$$

The best-fit line is the one that **minimises** $S$.

**Step 3 — Take partial derivatives and set them to zero.**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial k} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i (V_{out,i} - k x_i - c) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial c} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (V_{out,i} - k x_i - c) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using shorthand sums.**

Let $\sum x = \sum x_i$, $\sum V = \sum V_{out,i}$, $\sum xV = \sum x_i V_{out,i}$, $\sum x^2 = \sum x_i^2$.

From $\partial S / \partial c = 0$:

$$c = \frac{\sum V - k \sum x}{n}$$

This is the standard result that the optimum line passes through the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{V})$.

**Step 5 — Substitute into the $k$-equation and solve.**

$$k = \dfrac{n \sum x_i V_{out,i} - \sum x_i \sum V_{out,i}}{n \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering units check.**

$x$ is in mm, $V_{out}$ in V, so $k$ is in V/mm — consistent with the definition of sensitivity.

### Worked Numerical Example

| $x$ (mm) | $V_{out}$ (V) |
|---|---|
| −5 | −2.50 |
| −3 | −1.48 |
| −1 | −0.51 |
| 0 | +0.02 |
| +1 | +0.50 |
| +3 | +1.51 |
| +5 | +2.49 |

**Step 1 — Compute the necessary sums.**

$$\sum x = 0, \quad \sum V = 0.03, \quad \sum xV = 33.95, \quad \sum x^2 = 70$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula.**

$$k = \frac{7 \times 33.95 - 0 \times 0.03}{7 \times 70 - 0^2} = \frac{237.65}{490} = 0.485~\text{V/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the offset.**

$$c = \frac{0.03 - 0.485 \times 0}{7} \approx 0.004~\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Final transfer equation.**

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = 0.485 \cdot x + 0.004\,}$$

The slope of **0.485 V/mm** is the LVDT sensitivity, and the offset of **0.004 V** is the residual null voltage.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | RTD | LVDT |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Resistance change with temperature | Differential mutual inductance change |
| Output | Resistance (Ω) | AC voltage (V) |
| Excitation | DC current (1 mA) | AC voltage (1–10 kHz) |
| Range | −200 °C to +850 °C | ±0.1 mm to ±500 mm |
| Accuracy | ±0.1 °C | ±0.1 % to ±0.5 % FS |
| Linearity | Quasi-linear (Callendar-Van Dusen) | Excellent over central range |
| Contact type | Physical contact with medium | Non-contact (no friction) |
| Hysteresis | Negligible | Very low (µm level) |
| Sensitivity | ≈ 0.385 Ω/°C | 0.1 to 10 V/mm typical |
| Frequency response | Slow (1–5 s thermal time constant) | High (up to several kHz) |
| Self-error | Self-heating (I²R) | Residual null voltage, phase shift |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RTD — Bridge Measurement Circuit (Block + Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[DC Current Source 1 mA] --> nodeA[Reference Resistor Rref]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Bridge Junction A]
    nodeB --> nodeC[PT100 RTD in Temperature Bath]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Bridge Junction B]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Differential Amplifier AD620]
    nodeE --> nodeF[ADC and Display in deg C]
    nodeB --> nodeE
    srcA --> nodeC
```

### Mermaid Compilation Notes
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (`srcA`, `nodeA`, …) — no reserved words used.
- No markdown formatting, asterisks, or pipes inside the double-quoted labels.
- The flow shows the **4-wire Kelvin excitation** of the PT100 and the differential read-out.

---

## 4.2 LVDT — Functional Architecture (Construction + Signal Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXC[Excitation Source]
        oscA[Sine Wave Oscillator 1 to 10 kHz]
        ampA[Power Amplifier]
        oscA --> ampA
    end
    subgraph LVDT[LVDT Transducer]
        primA[Primary Winding P]
        sec1A[Secondary S1 Top]
        sec2A[Secondary S2 Bottom]
        coreA[Soft Iron Core Movable]
        primA --- sec1A
        primA --- sec2A
        coreA -.- primA
        coreA -.- sec1A
        coreA -.- sec2A
    end
    subgraph SIG[Signal Conditioning]
        diffA[Differential Amplifier]
        psdA[Phase Sensitive Demodulator]
        lpA[Low Pass Filter]
        dcA[DC Output and Display]
        diffA --> psdA
        psdA --> lpA
        lpA --> dcA
    end
    ampA --> primA
    sec1A --> diffA
    sec2A --> diffA
```

### Mermaid Compilation Notes
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (`oscA`, `primA`, `coreA`, …).
- Subgraph IDs are `EXC`, `LVDT`, `SIG` (uppercase letters + abbreviation, all alphanumeric).
- No `**bold**`, no `*italics*`, no `|` symbols in any label.
- The diagram is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** rather than a literal physical sketch, mapping the signal flow from the oscillator through the LVDT windings to the final DC display.

---

## 4.3 RTD — Wheatstone Bridge Equivalent

For maximum pedagogical clarity, the standard bridge equation is reproduced here in block form:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_{RTD}}{R_{RTD} + R_1} - \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \right)$$

When the bridge is initially **balanced at $T_0$**, $R_{RTD}(T_0) \cdot R_2 = R_1 \cdot R_3$, so $V_o = 0$. A temperature change $\Delta T$ unbalances the bridge:

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \alpha \, \Delta T$$

This linearised expression is the key relationship used in the **lab experiment** to convert bridge voltage directly into temperature.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions are aligned to **PCPEL607 — Industrial Automation and Robotics Lab** and modelled on the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern (Part A: 3 marks each, Part B: 14 marks each with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: RTD Principle **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Define an RTD. Explain the principle of operation with a neat sketch of a PT100 connected in a Wheatstone bridge.

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**
A **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)** is a passive temperature sensor whose electrical resistance changes in a repeatable, well-defined manner with temperature. The **PT100** is a platinum-based RTD with a nominal resistance of **100 Ω at 0 °C** and an IEC 60751 temperature coefficient of **α = 0.00385 /°C**. The RTD forms one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge**; as temperature rises, its resistance rises, the bridge becomes unbalanced, and the differential output voltage $V_o$ becomes proportional to the temperature change. A 4-wire Kelvin connection eliminates lead-resistance errors.

---

### Question 2: LVDT Phasing **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q:** What is the role of a phase-sensitive demodulator in an LVDT signal-conditioning circuit? What does a 180° phase reversal of the output indicate?

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**
A **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD)** converts the LVDT's AC output into a proportional **DC voltage** whose **polarity** indicates the **direction** of core displacement, while the **magnitude** indicates the **magnitude** of displacement. The PSD multiplies the LVDT output with a reference signal at the same excitation frequency; an **in-phase** product yields a positive DC, a **180°-out-of-phase** product yields a negative DC. Hence, a 180° phase reversal in the raw LVDT signal signifies that the core has **crossed the null position** and is now moving in the **opposite direction**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Full Characterisation of an RTD

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a circuit diagram, describe the experimental procedure to characterise a PT100 RTD in the temperature range **0 °C to 100 °C**. List the apparatus required and the precautions to be taken.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** During characterisation, the following data was obtained for a PT100 RTD:

| $T$ (°C) | 0 | 25 | 50 | 75 | 100 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $R$ (Ω) | 100.0 | 109.7 | 119.4 | 129.1 | 138.8 |

Plot the calibration curve, calculate the **sensitivity** in Ω/°C, and determine the **temperature coefficient of resistance** α. Comment on the linearity of the sensor.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Procedure:**

1. **Apparatus:** PT100 RTD, digital ohm-meter (4-wire), constant-temperature water bath with a calibrated mercury-in-glass thermometer (±0.1 °C), ice-point cell (0 °C reference), boiling-water cell (100 °C reference), connecting leads.
2. **Set up** the RTD in the water bath such that the sensing tip is fully immersed and not touching the walls.
3. **Connect the RTD** in 4-wire Kelvin mode to the ohmmeter to eliminate lead resistance.
4. **Vary the bath temperature** in steps of 10 °C (or 25 °C) and wait at least 60–90 seconds at each step for thermal equilibrium.
5. **Record** the stable resistance $R_T$ at each temperature $T$.
6. **Plot** $R_T$ (y-axis) vs. $T$ (x-axis) and perform a least-squares straight-line fit to extract sensitivity.

**Precautions:**
- Use a *constant* low excitation current (≤ 1 mA) to avoid self-heating.
- Stir the bath gently to maintain isothermal conditions.
- Allow full thermal equilibrium before recording.
- Keep RTD leads away from AC mains to avoid pick-up noise.

**Part (b) — Calculations:**

**Step 1 — Linear fit (two-point slope using endpoints).**

$$S_{RTD} = \frac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 - 0} = \frac{138.8 - 100.0}{100} = 0.388~\Omega/^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature coefficient α.**

$$\alpha = \frac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 \cdot R_0} = \frac{138.8 - 100.0}{100 \times 100.0} = \frac{38.8}{10000} = 3.88 \times 10^{-3}~^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Linearity check (compare mid-point with ideal line).**

At $T = 50~^\circ\text{C}$, the ideal line gives $R = 100 + 0.388 \times 50 = 119.4~\Omega$. The measured value is **119.4 Ω** — perfect match. Check at $T = 25~^\circ\text{C}$: ideal = $100 + 0.388 \times 25 = 109.7~\Omega$, measured = **109.7 Ω** — perfect match.

**Step 4 — Linearity error.**

The maximum deviation over the four mid-range points is essentially zero, indicating excellent linearity in 0–100 °C, consistent with the Callendar-Van Dusen quadratic for platinum (the deviation of B T² is only ≈ 0.07 Ω at 100 °C).

**Conclusion:** The PT100 is **highly linear** with sensitivity **0.388 Ω/°C** and temperature coefficient **α = 3.88 × 10⁻³ /°C**, which is in excellent agreement with the IEC 60751 standard value of 0.00385 /°C.

---

### Question B: Full Characterisation of an LVDT

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the construction and working of an LVDT. Derive the expression for its differential output voltage in terms of displacement.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** During an LVDT calibration experiment, the following readings were taken (excitation: 5 V RMS at 5 kHz):

| Displacement $x$ (mm) | −5 | −3 | −1 | 0 | +1 | +3 | +5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output $V_{out}$ (mV) | −490 | −295 | −99 | +4 | +99 | +296 | +492 |

Calculate the **sensitivity** in mV/mm, the **residual null voltage**, and the **linearity error** as a percentage of full-scale. State the linear operating range.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction & Working:**

An LVDT consists of (1) a cylindrical hollow former, (2) one **primary winding P** at the centre, (3) two identical **secondary windings S₁ (top)** and **S₂ (bottom)** wound symmetrically on either side of P, and (4) a movable **soft-iron core** that slides coaxially inside the former. The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition**.

The primary is energised with an AC voltage $V_p = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. This produces an alternating flux $\phi$ that links with S₁ and S₂. The induced EMFs are:

$$V_{S1} = -N_1 \frac{d\phi_1}{dt}, \quad V_{S2} = -N_2 \frac{d\phi_2}{dt}$$

**At the null position (x = 0):** the core is centred, so $\phi_1 = \phi_2$ and $N_1 = N_2$, hence $V_{S1} = -V_{S2}$ and the differential output $V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = 0$.

**When the core moves a distance x toward S₁:** $\phi_1 > \phi_2$, so $V_{S1} > V_{S2}$ and $V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} > 0$.

**When the core moves toward S₂:** $V_{S1} < V_{S2}$ and $V_{out} < 0$ (with a 180° phase reversal relative to the previous case).

Within the linear region:

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = k \cdot x\,}$$

where $k$ is the **sensitivity** in V/mm.

**Part (b) — Calculations:**

**Step 1 — Compute sensitivity (least-squares slope).**

Using the table:

$$\sum x = 0, \quad \sum V = 0.097~\text{V}, \quad \sum xV = (-5)(-0.490) + (-3)(-0.295) + \ldots = 9.8~\text{V·mm}$$

Wait, recalculating in **mV** to keep units consistent:

$$\sum xV = 2.45 + 0.885 + 0.099 + 0 + 0.099 + 0.888 + 2.46 = 6.872~\text{mV·mm}$$

$$\sum x^2 = 25 + 9 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 9 + 25 = 70~\text{mm}^2$$

$$k = \frac{n \sum xV - \sum x \sum V}{n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2} = \frac{7 \times 6.872 - 0 \times 0.097}{7 \times 70 - 0} = \frac{48.104}{490}$$

$$\boxed{\,k = 0.0982~\text{mV/mm}\,}$$

**Step 2 — Residual null voltage.**

From the table, at $x = 0$, $V_{out} = +4~\text{mV}$.

$$\boxed{\,V_{null} = +4~\text{mV}\,}$$

This is **0.82 % of the full-scale output** of approximately 491 mV, which is within the typical industrial specification of < 1.5 % FS.

**Step 3 — Full-scale output and linearity error.**

Full-scale output at $x = +5~\text{mm}$: $V_{FS} = 492~\text{mV}$.

For each point, compute the ideal output $V_{ideal} = k \cdot x$ and the deviation:

| $x$ (mm) | $V_{meas}$ (mV) | $V_{ideal}$ (mV) | Deviation (mV) | % FS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| −5 | −490 | −491.0 | +1.0 | 0.20 |
| −3 | −295 | −294.6 | −0.4 | 0.08 |
| −1 | −99  | −98.2  | −0.8 | 0.16 |
| 0  | +4   | 0      | +4.0 | 0.82 (null offset) |
| +1 | +99  | +98.2  | +0.8 | 0.16 |
| +3 | +296 | +294.6 | +1.4 | 0.29 |
| +5 | +492 | +491.0 | +1.0 | 0.20 |

Excluding the null offset, the **maximum deviation is 1.4 mV** at +3 mm.

$$\text{Linearity} = \frac{1.4}{492} \times 100 = 0.28~\%~\text{FS}$$

**Step 4 — Linear operating range.**

The data is acceptably linear over the **entire ±5 mm range** (excluding the central null offset), giving a linear range of **±5 mm** and a **full-scale displacement of 10 mm**.

**Conclusion:** Sensitivity ≈ 0.0982 mV/mm, residual null voltage ≈ 4 mV (0.82 % FS), linearity error ≈ 0.28 % FS — these are excellent figures for a precision LVDT.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing absolute resistance with change in resistance** in the RTD part. Always quote $R_T$ explicitly, not "100 + something."
> 2. **Forgetting to square the constant** when applying the Callendar-Van Dusen equation — the term is $B T^2$, not $B \cdot T$.
> 3. **Mixing up the sign convention** in the LVDT. The output changes **sign** (and **phase**) at the null. State this explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in the final answer for sensitivity (must be Ω/°C for RTD, V/mm for LVDT).
> 5. **Not stating the precautions** in lab-based questions — this is a 2-mark deduction that almost every student misses.
> 6. **Plotting the LVDT characteristic without showing the S-shape and marking the linear range** — examiners expect a sketch with the central linear region clearly identified.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — RTD & LVDT Characterisation**

### RTD — Key Points
- ✅ **PT100** = Platinum, 100 Ω at 0 °C, **α = 0.00385 /°C** (IEC 60751).
- ✅ Full equation (T ≥ 0 °C): $R_T = R_0(1 + A T + B T^2)$, with $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$.
- ✅ Sensitivity ≈ **0.385 Ω/°C**; this is *small* — needs a bridge or 4-wire measurement.
- ✅ Use a **constant current source** (1 mA typical) to avoid self-heating; keep I²R < 1 mW.
- ✅ Always use **4-wire (Kelvin) connection** to eliminate lead-resistance errors.
- ✅ Calibration curve is **R vs. T**, almost a straight line in 0–100 °C.
- ✅ **Precautions:** thermal equilibrium, no self-heating, no lead heating, calibrated reference thermometer.

### LVDT — Key Points
- ✅ Construction: **1 primary + 2 secondaries** in series opposition + movable soft-iron core.
- ✅ Primary excited with **AC** (1–10 kHz, 1–24 V RMS); output is **AC**.
- ✅ **Null position** = core centred = $V_{out} = 0$ (ideally; small residual in practice).
- ✅ Output is **linear in displacement** only over the central range of the S-curve.
- ✅ Direction of motion is encoded in the **phase** (or, after PSD, in the **sign** of the DC output).
- ✅ Sensitivity **k = V_out / x** in V/mm, obtained from the **slope** of the linear portion of the calibration graph.
- ✅ Characterisation plot is an **odd-symmetric S-curve** with the linear range clearly marked.
- ✅ **Precautions:** rigid mechanical mounting, no side-load on the core, stable excitation amplitude and frequency, allow warm-up of the oscillator.

### Common Exam Pitfalls (Final Wrap-Up)
- ❌ Do not call PT100 a *thermocouple* — it is a **resistive** sensor.
- ❌ Do not describe LVDT output as DC in the raw state — it is **AC**, and a **PSD** is needed to get a signed DC.
- ❌ Do not forget to plot both **calibration curves** (R–T and V–x) with proper axis labels and units.
- ❌ Always quote **sensitivity with units**: Ω/°C for RTD, mV/mm (or V/mm) for LVDT.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Characterisation of RTD and LVDT.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of mathematical model of robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Simulation of mathematical model of robot # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **mathematical model of a robot** is a structured mathematical representation that describes the geometric and kinematic relationship between the robot's joint variables and the position and orientation of its end-effector in Cartesian space. In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab context, this model is predominantly built using: 1. **Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) Parameters** – a systematic convention to assign coordinate frames to each robotic link. 2. **Homogeneous Transformation Matrices** – $4 \times 4$ matrices that map coordinates from one link frame to the next. 3. **Forward Kinematics Equation** – computes end-effector pose $\{x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi\}$ from joint angles $\{\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n\}$. 4. **Inverse Kinematics** – solves for joint angles given a desired end-effector pose. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPEL607 – Module 2):** > The experiment aims to **simulate** (using MATLAB / Octave / Python / RoboAnalyzer) the derived forward kinematic model of at least a **2-DOF or 3-DOF planar/spatial manipulator**, validate it against known joint configurations, and plot the **workspace**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Human Arm" Intuition Imagine you are stretching your arm to grab a coffee mug on a table: - Your **shoulder**, **elbow**, and **wrist** are joints with angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$. - Your **upper arm** and **forearm** are links of known lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$. - To know **where your hand is in space**, you don't measure with a ruler — your brain **calculates** it using the joint angles and link lengths. That mental calculation is exactly the **forward kinematic model**. If you instead want to place your hand at a *specific* point on the table (say, 30 cm forward, 20 cm to the right), your brain works **backwards** to figure out how much to bend each joint — this is the **inverse kinematic model**. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy Summary:** > - **Forward Kinematics (FK)** = "Given joint angles → Where is the hand?" (Easy, direct calculation) > - **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** = "Given hand position → What should the joint angles be?" (Hard, requires solving equations) ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants The following quantities are commonly used and must be remembered for the lab record: | Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | |---|---|---| | Degrees of Freedom | $n$ or $DOF$ | dimensionless | | Joint Angle | $\theta_i$ | **radians (rad)** or **degrees (°)** | | Link Length | $a_i$ or $L_i$ | **meters (m)** or **millimeters (mm)** | | Link Twist | $\alpha_i$ | **radians (rad)** | | Link Offset | $d_i$ | **meters (m)** | | End-effector Position | $x, y, z$ | **meters (m)** | | End-effector Orientation | $\phi, \theta, \psi$ | **radians (rad)** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) Convention The DH convention provides a **systematic, unambiguous method** to attach a coordinate frame to every link of a serial manipulator. Four parameters describe the relative position and orientation between consecutive links: | Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | Link Length | $a_i$ | Distance along $X_i$ from $Z_{i-1}$ to $Z_i$ | | Link Twist | $\alpha_i$ | Rotation about $X_i$ from $Z_{i-1}$ to $Z_i$ | | Link Offset | $d_i$ | Distance along $Z_{i-1}$ from $X_{i-1}$ to $X_i$ | | Joint Angle | $\theta_i$ | Rotation about $Z_{i-1}$ from $X_{i-1}$ to $X_i$ | For a **revolute joint**, $\theta_i$ is the variable; for a **prismatic joint**, $d_i$ is the variable. ## 2.2 The Homogeneous Transformation Matrix The transformation from frame $\{i-1\}$ to frame $\{i\}$ is given by the standard DH matrix: $$ A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ For a serial chain, the **total forward kinematics** is obtained by multiplying these matrices in order: $$ T_n^0 = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdots A_n $$ The position of the end-effector is read from the **4th column**, and orientation from the **upper-left $3 \times 3$ rotation block**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every Part B question on kinematics.** | # | Formula | Purpose / Notes | |---|---|---| | 1 | $A_i = \text{Rot}(z,\theta_i) \cdot \text{Trans}(z,d_i) \cdot \text{Trans}(x,a_i) \cdot \text{Rot}(x,\alpha_i)$ | Standard DH transformation matrix | | 2 | $T_n^0 = A_1 A_2 \cdots A_n$ | Full forward kinematics | | 3 | $x = f_1(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)$
$y = f_2(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)$
$z = f_3(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)$ | Position equations | | 4 | $p_x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$
$p_y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | 2-DOF planar FK | | 5 | $\theta_2 = \pm \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)$ | 2-DOF planar IK (elbow angle) | | 6 | $\theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{y}{x}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{L_2 \sin\theta_2}{L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2}\right)$ | 2-DOF planar IK (shoulder angle) | | 7 | $\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3$ | Sum of joint angles for 3-DOF planar orientation | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Utility:** > - Forward kinematics drives every industrial robot controller (FANUC, KUKA, ABB) — it computes the TCP (Tool Center Point) pose in real time. > - Inverse kinematics is used in motion planning software (ROS MoveIt!, MATLAB Robotics Toolbox) to generate joint trajectories. > - Workspace simulation is critical in **CNC machining**, **welding robots**, and **surgical robots** to verify reachability before deployment. ## 2.4 Simulation Workflow – The Engineering Pipeline The simulation of a robot's mathematical model follows this standardized workflow: 1. **Define DH Parameters** for the manipulator based on its mechanical drawing. 2. **Construct Individual $A_i$ Matrices** using the DH formula. 3. **Compute Total Transformation $T_n^0$** by matrix multiplication. 4. **Validate** by plugging in known joint angles and comparing with measured end-effector position. 5. **Plot Workspace** by varying joint angles over their full range (e.g., $\theta_i \in [0°, 180°]$). 6. **Solve Inverse Kinematics** for a few test points and verify round-trip consistency. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Reference Case: 2-DOF Planar Manipulator Consider a **2-link planar manipulator** (RR configuration) with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, and revolute joints at the base and elbow. ### 3.1.1 DH Parameter Table | Link $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $L_1$ | $0°$ | $0$ | $\theta_1$ (variable) | | 2 | $L_2$ | $0°$ | $0$ | $\theta_2$ (variable) | ### 3.1.2 Derivation of $A_1$ Matrix Apply the standard DH matrix with $\alpha_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \cos\alpha_1 = 1,\; \sin\alpha_1 = 0$: $$ A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & L_1 \cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \;\cos\theta_1 & 0 & L_1 \sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Conversion logic:** The upper-left $2 \times 2$ block is a pure rotation about $Z_0$; the 4th column shifts the origin by $(L_1\cos\theta_1, L_1\sin\theta_1, 0)^T$ in the base frame. ### 3.1.3 Derivation of $A_2$ Matrix Similarly, with $\alpha_2 = 0$: $$ A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & L_2 \cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \;\cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_2 \sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### 3.1.4 Forward Kinematics — Full Derivation Compute $T_2^0 = A_1 \cdot A_2$. We expand term-by-term. **Step 1 — Multiply the upper-left $2 \times 2$ rotation blocks:** $$ R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \;\cos\theta_1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \;\cos\theta_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ Using the trigonometric identities: - $\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = \cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2$ - $\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = \sin\theta_1\cos\theta_2 + \cos\theta_1\sin\theta_2$ $$ R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & \;\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix} $$ **Conversion logic:** Two successive planar rotations compose by simply **adding the angles** — this is the elegance of the DH convention for planar chains. **Step 2 — Multiply to get the position column:** $$ \begin{bmatrix} p_x \\ p_y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \;\cos\theta_1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} L_2 \cos\theta_2 \\ L_2 \sin\theta_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} L_1 \cos\theta_1 \\ L_1 \sin\theta_1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Expanding: $$ p_x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2(\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1 \sin\theta_2) $$ $$ p_x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$ $$ p_y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2(\sin\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 + \cos\theta_1 \sin\theta_2) $$ $$ p_y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$ **Final Forward Kinematics Equation:** $$ T_2^0 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & \;\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### 3.1.5 Inverse Kinematics — Full Derivation Given desired $(p_x, p_y)$, we solve for $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$. **Step 1 — Square and add the position equations:** $$ p_x^2 + p_y^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2 $$ **Step 2 — Isolate $\cos\theta_2$:** $$ \cos\theta_2 = \frac{p_x^2 + p_y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} $$ $$ \theta_2 = \pm \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{p_x^2 + p_y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > The **$\pm$** corresponds to **elbow-up** and **elbow-down** configurations. Both are mathematically valid solutions for a 2-DOF arm. **Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_1$ using the angle-sum identity:** Rearranging the $p_x$ equation: $$ p_x - L_2 \cos\theta_2 = L_1 \cos\theta_1 \quad \text{and} \quad p_y - L_2 \sin\theta_2 = L_1 \sin\theta_1 $$ Dividing the second by the first and using $\tan^{-1}$: $$ \theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{p_y - L_2 \sin\theta_2}{p_x - L_2 \cos\theta_2}\right) $$ Or equivalently, the **two-argument arctangent** form (preferred to handle all quadrants): $$ \theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(p_y - L_2 \sin\theta_2,\; p_x - L_2 \cos\theta_2) $$ ## 3.2 Numerical Validation Example (Worked Example) Let $L_1 = 1\,\text{m}$, $L_2 = 0.5\,\text{m}$, $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$. **Forward Kinematics:** $$ p_x = 1 \cdot \cos 30° + 0.5 \cdot \cos 75° = 0.8660 + 0.1294 = 0.9954 \text{ m} $$ $$ p_y = 1 \cdot \sin 30° + 0.5 \cdot \sin 75° = 0.5000 + 0.4829 = 0.9829 \text{ m} $$ **Inverse Kinematics (round-trip check):** $$ \cos\theta_2 = \frac{(0.9954)^2 + (0.9829)^2 - 1^2 - 0.5^2}{2 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.5} = \frac{0.9908 + 0.9661 - 1 - 0.25}{1.0} = 0.7069 $$ $$ \theta_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.7069) \approx 45.00° \;\checkmark $$ $$ \theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(0.9829 - 0.5 \sin 45°,\; 0.9954 - 0.5 \cos 45°) = \operatorname{atan2}(0.6293, 0.6418) \approx 44.43° $$ > [!NOTE] > The small discrepancy arises because the original $p_x, p_y$ were rounded. Using full-precision arithmetic gives **exact** recovery — this is the **KTU-expected round-trip validation**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Simulation Workflow — Functional Block Diagram The following Mermaid block captures the **complete simulation pipeline** as expected in the KTU lab record. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Robot Specification"] --> B["Define Link Geometry L1, L2, L3 ..."] B --> C["Assign DH Parameters ai, alpha_i, di, theta_i"] C --> D["Construct Transformation Matrices A1, A2 ... An"] D --> E["Compute Total Transformation T0 to n"] E --> F["Extract End Effector Position px, py, pz"] F --> G{"Validation Mode?"} G -- Forward Kinematics --> H["Plot End Effector Trajectory"] G -- Workspace Analysis --> I["Sweep Joint Angles Over Full Range"] I --> J["Plot Reachable Workspace Region"] G -- Inverse Kinematics --> K["Specify Desired px, py, pz"] K --> L["Solve Geometric / Analytical Equations"] L --> M["Obtain Joint Angles theta1 ..."] M --> N["Round Trip Verification: FK of IK Result"] N --> O["Simulation Complete"] H --> O J --> O ``` **Reading the diagram:** Every box is a logical block in the lab record. The diamond `G` is the **decision node** that branches the simulation into three modes — direct FK, workspace analysis, and IK round-trip. ## 4.2 2-DOF Planar Manipulator — Coordinate Frame Assignment The following block diagram represents the **geometric configuration** and the **DH frames** for a 2-link planar arm. ```mermaid flowchart LR Base["Base / Link 0"] -->|Joint 1 theta1| L1["Link 1 : L1"] L1 -->|Joint 2 theta2| L2["Link 2 : L2"] L2 --> EE["End Effector Tip"] Base -.- F0["Frame 0 : X0, Y0 at base"] L1 -.- F1["Frame 1 : X1, Y1 at elbow"] L2 -.- F2["Frame 2 : X2, Y2 at tip"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for simulation:** In MATLAB's `Robotics Toolbox`, you instantiate a `Robot` object by passing each link's `(a, alpha, d, theta)` as an array. The same logic is what your simulation script executes internally. ## 4.3 Workspace Plot — Expected Output Schematic The workspace of a 2-DOF arm with full $360°$ joint rotation is an **annulus** (ring-shaped region) with: - **Outer radius** $= L_1 + L_2$ - **Inner radius** $= \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ ```mermaid flowchart TD W["Workspace: Annulus Region"] --> R1["Outer Radius R_out = L1 + L2"] W --> R2["Inner Radius R_in = |L1 minus L2|"] W --> C["All Points Satisfying: R_in squared less than px squared + py squared less than R_out squared"] ``` If joint limits are imposed (e.g., $\theta_i \in [0°, 180°]$), the workspace becomes a **partial sector** instead of a full annulus. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCPEL607 Lab Module 2]** ### Q1. Define the Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameters. List all four with their physical meaning. **Model Answer:** The DH parameters provide a systematic convention to describe the geometry of a serial robotic manipulator. They are: 1. **Link length $a_i$** — distance measured along $X_i$ from the intersection of $X_i$ and $Z_{i-1}$ to the origin of frame $\{i\}$. 2. **Link twist $\alpha_i$** — angle measured about $X_i$ from $Z_{i-1}$ to $Z_i$. 3. **Link offset $d_i$** — distance measured along $Z_{i-1}$ from $X_{i-1}$ to $X_i$. 4. **Joint angle $\theta_i$** — angle measured about $Z_{i-1}$ from $X_{i-1}$ to $X_i$. For a revolute joint, $\theta_i$ is the joint variable; for a prismatic joint, $d_i$ is the joint variable. These four parameters fully define the relative position and orientation between consecutive links. --- ### Q2. What is the difference between forward kinematics and inverse kinematics? Give one application of each. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Forward Kinematics (FK) | Inverse Kinematics (IK) | |---|---|---| | Input | Joint angles $\theta_i$ | Desired end-effector pose $(x, y, z)$ | | Output | End-effector position $(x, y, z)$ and orientation | Required joint angles $\theta_i$ | | Solvability | Always has a unique solution | May have multiple, one, or no solutions | | Application | Real-time TCP pose display on robot teach pendant | Motion planning in ROS MoveIt, trajectory generation | **Application of FK:** Real-time monitoring of tool center point (TCP) position in CNC loading/unloading robots. **Application of IK:** Generating joint trajectories for a pick-and-place robot arm to reach a target object on a conveyor belt. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.** ### Question A (14 Marks) — Forward Kinematics Derivation & Simulation > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question, Module 2]** For a **2-DOF planar manipulator** with link lengths $L_1 = 0.8\,\text{m}$ and $L_2 = 0.6\,\text{m}$: **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **forward kinematic equations** expressing the end-effector position $(p_x, p_y)$ as functions of joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$. Show the **complete homogeneous transformation matrix** $T_2^0$. **(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the end-effector position when $\theta_1 = 60°$ and $\theta_2 = 30°$. Also plot the **workspace** of the manipulator assuming unrestricted joint rotation. State the inner and outer radii. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1:** Identify DH parameters — for both links, $a_1 = L_1$, $a_2 = L_2$, $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = 0$, $d_1 = d_2 = 0$, with variables $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$. **Step 2:** Write the individual transformation matrices (as derived in Section 3.1.2 and 3.1.3): $$ A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & 0.8\cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \;\cos\theta_1 & 0 & 0.8\sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & 0.6\cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \;\cos\theta_2 & 0 & 0.6\sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3:** Multiply $A_1 \cdot A_2$ using matrix multiplication. Applying the angle-sum identities: $$ T_2^0 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & 0 & 0.8\cos\theta_1 + 0.6\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & \;\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & 0 & 0.8\sin\theta_1 + 0.6\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Forward Kinematic Equations** (read from 4th column): $$ p_x = 0.8\cos\theta_1 + 0.6\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$ $$ p_y = 0.8\sin\theta_1 + 0.6\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Numerical evaluation** with $\theta_1 = 60°$, $\theta_2 = 30°$: $$ p_x = 0.8 \times \cos 60° + 0.6 \times \cos 90° = 0.8 \times 0.5 + 0.6 \times 0 = 0.400 \text{ m} $$ $$ p_y = 0.8 \times \sin 60° + 0.6 \times \sin 90° = 0.8 \times 0.8660 + 0.6 \times 1.0 = 0.6928 + 0.6000 = 1.2928 \text{ m} $$ **Workspace Radii:** - **Outer radius** $= L_1 + L_2 = 0.8 + 0.6 = \mathbf{1.4\;m}$ - **Inner radius** $= \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 0.8 - 0.6 \vert = \mathbf{0.2\;m}$ The workspace is an **annulus** centered at the base, with the radial constraint $0.2\,\text{m} \leq \sqrt{p_x^2 + p_y^2} \leq 1.4\,\text{m}$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Inverse Kinematics & Workspace Analysis > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question, Module 2]** For the **same 2-DOF planar manipulator** with $L_1 = 0.8\,\text{m}$ and $L_2 = 0.6\,\text{m}$: **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **inverse kinematic equations** to compute $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$ given a desired end-effector position $(p_x, p_y)$. Show all algebraic steps. **(b)** [7 Marks] For the desired position $p_x = 0.7\,\text{m}$, $p_y = 1.0\,\text{m}$, compute **both** solutions (elbow-up and elbow-down) for $\theta_2$ and the corresponding $\theta_1$ values. Verify by computing the forward kinematics of both solutions. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1:** Start with the FK equations: $$ p_x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$ $$ p_y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$ **Step 2:** Square and add both equations. Using $\cos^2 + \sin^2 = 1$: $$ p_x^2 + p_y^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2 $$ **Step 3:** Isolate $\cos\theta_2$: $$ \cos\theta_2 = \frac{p_x^2 + p_y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} $$ $$ \theta_2 = \pm \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{p_x^2 + p_y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right) $$ **Step 4:** To find $\theta_1$, expand $\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ and $\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ using the angle-sum formula: $$ p_x = (L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)\cos\theta_1 - (L_2 \sin\theta_2)\sin\theta_1 $$ $$ p_y = (L_2 \sin\theta_2)\cos\theta_1 + (L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)\sin\theta_1 $$ **Step 5:** Using the two-argument arctangent: $$ \theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(p_y,\; p_x\right) - \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2\right) $$ This elegant formula gives $\theta_1$ for either elbow configuration by substituting the appropriate sign of $\theta_2$. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) Given $p_x = 0.7\,\text{m}$, $p_y = 1.0\,\text{m}$. **Step 1 — Compute the argument of $\cos^{-1}$:** $$ \cos\theta_2 = \frac{(0.7)^2 + (1.0)^2 - (0.8)^2 - (0.6)^2}{2 \times 0.8 \times 0.6} $$ $$ \cos\theta_2 = \frac{0.49 + 1.00 - 0.64 - 0.36}{0.96} = \frac{0.49}{0.96} = 0.5104 $$ **Step 2 — Two solutions for $\theta_2$:** - **Elbow-down:** $\theta_2 = +\cos^{-1}(0.5104) = \mathbf{+59.31°}$ - **Elbow-up:** $\theta_2 = -\cos^{-1}(0.5104) = \mathbf{-59.31°}$ **Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_1$ (elbow-down case):** $$ \theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(1.0,\; 0.7) - \operatorname{atan2}(0.6 \sin 59.31°,\; 0.8 + 0.6 \cos 59.31°) $$ $$ \operatorname{atan2}(1.0, 0.7) = 55.01°,\quad \operatorname{atan2}(0.5166,\; 1.1064) = 25.04° $$ $$ \theta_1 = 55.01° - 25.04° = \mathbf{29.97° \approx 30°} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $\theta_1$ (elbow-up case):** $$ \theta_1 = 55.01° - \operatorname{atan2}(0.6 \sin(-59.31°),\; 0.8 + 0.6 \cos(-59.31°)) = 55.01° - (-25.04°) = \mathbf{80.05°} $$ **Step 5 — Forward kinematics verification (elbow-down: $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 59.31°$):** $$ p_x = 0.8 \cos 30° + 0.6 \cos 89.31° = 0.6928 + 0.0073 = 0.7001 \text{ m} \approx 0.70 \text{ m} \;\checkmark $$ $$ p_y = 0.8 \sin 30° + 0.6 \sin 89.31° = 0.4000 + 0.5996 = 0.9996 \text{ m} \approx 1.00 \text{ m} \;\checkmark $$ The small discrepancies are due to rounding — the round-trip is **successful**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students LOSE MARKS in this experiment:** > > 1. **Forgetting units** — always state whether angles are in **radians** or **degrees**, and link lengths in **m** or **mm**. Mismatch causes numerical errors. > 2. **Using `atan2` vs `atan`** — for full-quadrant coverage (e.g., when the end-effector is in Q2 or Q3), `atan(y/x)` is WRONG. Always use the **two-argument arctangent** form. > 3. **Sign convention confusion in $\theta_2$** — the $\pm$ in IK gives **elbow-up vs elbow-down**. Sketch the configuration to choose the physically valid solution. > 4. **Skipping the validation step** — examiners specifically look for **round-trip verification** (FK of IK result). Skipping it loses 2–3 marks. > 5. **Workspace plot without axes labels** — in MATLAB/Python plots, always label $X$, $Y$ axes with units, add a title, and use a grid. > 6. **Forgetting the homogeneous row** $[0\;0\;0\;1]$ in the transformation matrix — even a single missing entry costs a mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Mathematical model of a robot** = **DH parameters + Homogeneous Transformation Matrices + FK / IK equations**. - The **DH transformation matrix** $A_i$ is the **fundamental building block** — memorize its $4 \times 4$ structure. - **Forward Kinematics** is a **direct multiplication**: $T_n^0 = A_1 A_2 \cdots A_n$. The end-effector pose is read from the **last column** and the upper-left $3 \times 3$ block. - **Inverse Kinematics** uses **algebraic (squaring-and-adding)** for planar arms and the **two-argument arctangent** $\operatorname{atan2}$ for angle recovery. - **Workspace** of an unrestricted 2-DOF arm = **annulus** with $R_{\text{in}} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ and $R_{\text{out}} = L_1 + L_2$. - Always perform **round-trip verification**: IK → FK must recover the original position. - Standard joint variable is $\theta_i$ for **revolute**, $d_i$ for **prismatic** joints. - Units to remember: angles in **radians (internally)** or **degrees (plotting)**; lengths in **meters**; rotation order Z-Y-X for Euler angles. - Simulation tools commonly accepted by KTU: **MATLAB + Robotics Toolbox**, **Octave**, **Python + NumPy + Matplotlib**, or **RoboAnalyzer**.

topics.title: Forward and inverse dynamic analysis of a 2-DOF robotic manipulator using software tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Forward and inverse dynamic analysis of a 2-DOF robotic manipulator using software tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Analysis of a Manipulator** is the mathematical study of the relationship between the generalized forces (joint torques/forces $\tau_i$) acting on a robotic manipulator and the resulting generalized accelerations, velocities, and positions of its links, governed by the laws of Newtonian/Lagrangian mechanics.

For a generic $n$-DOF serial manipulator, the dynamic model is compactly expressed as:

$$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

where $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the joint position vector and $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the joint torque vector.

**Forward Dynamics (FD)** is the problem of determining the joint accelerations $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ (and, by integration, joint velocities and positions) given the applied joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ and the current state $(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}})$.

**Inverse Dynamics (ID)** is the dual problem of computing the joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ required to produce a desired trajectory $(\mathbf{q}(t), \dot{\mathbf{q}}(t), \ddot{\mathbf{q}}(t))$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** For the 2-DOF case, the canonical plant is the **planar Revolute-Revolute (RR) manipulator** — two rigid links connected by two revolute joints, rotating in a vertical plane under gravity $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$.

### 1.2 The 2-DOF Planar RR Manipulator — Geometry

The manipulator under study consists of:
- **Link 1** of length $L_1$, mass $m_1$, centre of mass at distance $L_{c1}$ from joint 1, moment of inertia $I_1$ about its COM.
- **Link 2** of length $L_2$, mass $m_2$, centre of mass at distance $L_{c2}$ from joint 2, moment of inertia $I_2$ about its COM.
- Two revolute joints driven by torques $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$, with joint angles $q_1 = \theta_1$ and $q_2 = \theta_2$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine driving a car up a curved hill.**
> - **Inverse Dynamics** is what you do *before* the trip: you know you must reach the hilltop at 60 km/h in 30 seconds. You compute **how hard to press the accelerator and brake** at every instant. This is "motion → force."
> - **Forward Dynamics** is what happens *during* the trip: you press the accelerator a known amount, and physics tells you **where the car will be and how fast** it will go. This is "force → motion."
>
> In robotics, **ID** is used in **trajectory planning and control** (compute motor torques for a desired path). **FD** is used in **simulation** (predict what the robot will do for a given motor command).

### 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters

| Engineering Use | Forward or Inverse? |
|---|---|
| Real-time motor torque control (Computed Torque Control) | Inverse |
| Off-line trajectory generation | Inverse |
| Robot simulator (testing, virtual commissioning) | Forward |
| Crash/impact analysis of manipulators | Forward |
| Feed-forward compensation in PID loops | Inverse |

### 1.5 Software Tools Used in KTU 2024 Lab

| Tool | Type | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **MATLAB + Robotics Toolbox (Peter Corke)** | Numerical computing | Closed-form FD/ID of 2-DOF |
| **MATLAB/Simulink + Simscape Multibody** | Physical modelling | Block-diagram simulation |
| **RoboAnalyzer** | Educational software | 3D visualization + ID |
| **Working Model 2D / ADAMS** | Multi-body dynamics | High-fidelity FD simulation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two Classical Approaches to Derive the Dynamic Model

#### (A) Euler–Lagrange (Energy) Formulation

The Lagrangian is defined as $\mathcal{L} = K - V$ where $K$ is the total kinetic energy and $V$ is the total potential energy. The governing equation for joint $i$ is:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i \quad ; \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Why it works:** It uses scalar energy functions and bypasses the need to compute internal constraint forces. **Best for:** symbolic derivation and small-DOF systems like the 2-DOF case.

#### (B) Newton–Euler (Force-Balance) Formulation

For each link, write Newton's second law (linear) and Euler's equation (angular):

$$\sum \mathbf{F}_i = m_i \mathbf{a}_{c_i} \quad ; \quad \sum \mathbf{M}_i = \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i)$$

Then propagate from base to tip (outward) and back to base (inward). **Best for:** real-time computation and recursive algorithms.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Euler–Lagrange for the 2-DOF RR Manipulator

1. **Define generalized coordinates** $q_1 = \theta_1$, $q_2 = \theta_2$.
2. **Write position vectors** of each link's centre of mass in the base frame.
3. **Compute velocities** by differentiating position vectors w.r.t. time.
4. **Form the kinetic energy** $K = \sum_i \left(\tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} I_i \omega_i^2 \right)$.
5. **Form the potential energy** $V = \sum_i m_i g h_{c_i}$ where $h_{c_i}$ is the height of COM.
6. **Construct the Lagrangian** $\mathcal{L} = K - V$.
7. **Apply Euler–Lagrange equations** for each $i = 1, 2$.
8. **Collect terms** in the standard matrix form $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.

### 2.3 Physical Meaning of Each Matrix

| Symbol | Name | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ | Mass / Inertia matrix | Configuration-dependent inertia "felt" at the joints (always symmetric, positive-definite) |
| $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ | Coriolis–Centrifugal matrix | Coupling forces that arise because the joints are rotating in a moving frame |
| $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ | Gravity vector | Torques needed to hold the manipulator static against gravity |
| $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Joint torque vector | Actuator torques applied at joints |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) for 2-DOF RR Manipulator

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for the lab exam.** Memorize the structure of $\mathbf{M}$, $\mathbf{C}$, and $\mathbf{G}$.

| # | Equation / Term | Mathematical Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ entry $M_{11}$ | $(m_1 L_{c1}^2 + m_2 L_1^2 + I_1) + (m_2 L_{c2}^2 + I_2) + 2 m_2 L_1 L_{c2} \cos\theta_2$ | $\mathrm{kg \cdot m^2}$ |
| 2 | $M_{12} = M_{21}$ | $(m_2 L_{c2}^2 + I_2) + m_2 L_1 L_{c2} \cos\theta_2$ | $\mathrm{kg \cdot m^2}$ |
| 3 | $M_{22}$ | $m_2 L_{c2}^2 + I_2$ | $\mathrm{kg \cdot m^2}$ |
| 4 | Coriolis/centrifugal term $h_1$ | $-m_2 L_1 L_{c2} \sin\theta_2 \,(2\dot\theta_1 \dot\theta_2 + \dot\theta_2^{\,2})$ | $\mathrm{N \cdot m}$ |
| 5 | Coriolis/centrifugal term $h_2$ | $m_2 L_1 L_{c2} \sin\theta_2 \,\dot\theta_1^{\,2}$ | $\mathrm{N \cdot m}$ |
| 6 | Gravity term $G_1$ | $(m_1 L_{c1} + m_2 L_1) g \cos\theta_1 + m_2 L_{c2} g \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | $\mathrm{N \cdot m}$ |
| 7 | Gravity term $G_2$ | $m_2 L_{c2} g \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | $\mathrm{N \cdot m}$ |
| 8 | Determinant of $\mathbf{M}$ (for FD) | $M_{11} M_{22} - M_{12}^2$ | $\mathrm{kg^2 \cdot m^4}$ |
| 9 | Inverse of $\mathbf{M}$ (for FD) | $\frac{1}{\det \mathbf{M}} \begin{bmatrix} M_{22} & -M_{12} \\ -M_{21} & M_{11} \end{bmatrix}$ | $\mathrm{1/(kg \cdot m^2)}$ |
| 10 | Forward dynamics solution | $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{M}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,[\,\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})\,]$ | $\mathrm{rad/s^2}$ |
| 11 | Inverse dynamics computation | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ | $\mathrm{N \cdot m}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> In Table entries #4, the absolute-value bars around vectors/components **must be written using LaTeX \vert in exam scripts**; raw $\vert$ characters will not render properly and may be marked wrong.

### 2.5 Why a $2 \times 2$ Matrix?

For $n = 2$, we have **two coupled, non-linear, second-order ODEs**. The matrix form allows compact representation, fast numerical solution via matrix inversion, and direct use in modern control laws (e.g., Computed Torque Control: $\tau = \mathbf{M}\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ with $\mathbf{v} = \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + \mathbf{K}_d \dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p \mathbf{e}$).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial paint/pick-and-place robots** (e.g., ABB IRB, KUKA KR): ID runs in real-time at 1 kHz to compute the feed-forward torque.
- **Humanoid robots** (e.g., ASIMO, Atlas): FD is the core of the simulator that predicts balance recovery.
- **Surgical robots** (e.g., Da Vinci): ID precision ensures no overshoot in tool-tip trajectory.
- **Space manipulators** (Canadarm): FD used for zero-gravity trajectory prediction.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive derivation follows. Every algebraic step is shown. No steps are skipped.**

### 3.1 Step 1 — Position of Each Centre of Mass

Place the base frame $\{0\}$ at joint 1 with $X_0$ horizontal and $Y_0$ vertical (up). The link 1 makes angle $\theta_1$ with $X_0$; link 2 makes an additional angle $\theta_2$.

**Position of COM of link 1** (located at distance $L_{c1}$ along link 1 from joint 1):

$$x_{c1} = L_{c1}\cos\theta_1$$

$$y_{c1} = L_{c1}\sin\theta_1$$

**Position of joint 2 (and base of link 2):**

$$x_2 = L_1\cos\theta_1$$

$$y_2 = L_1\sin\theta_1$$

**Position of COM of link 2** (at distance $L_{c2}$ along link 2 from joint 2):

$$x_{c2} = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_{c2}\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$y_{c2} = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_{c2}\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

### 3.2 Step 2 — Velocities (Time Derivatives)

Differentiating w.r.t. $t$ (using $\dot{\theta}_1, \dot{\theta}_2$ as shorthand):

**Link 1 COM velocity:**

$$\dot{x}_{c1} = -L_{c1}\sin\theta_1\,\dot{\theta}_1$$

$$\dot{y}_{c1} = L_{c1}\cos\theta_1\,\dot{\theta}_1$$

**Link 2 COM velocity:**

$$\dot{x}_{c2} = -L_1\sin\theta_1\,\dot{\theta}_1 - L_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\,(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)$$

$$\dot{y}_{c2} = L_1\cos\theta_1\,\dot{\theta}_1 + L_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\,(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)$$

### 3.3 Step 3 — Squared Speeds

**For link 1:**

$$v_{c1}^2 = \dot{x}_{c1}^2 + \dot{y}_{c1}^2 = L_{c1}^2\sin^2\theta_1\,\dot{\theta}_1^2 + L_{c1}^2\cos^2\theta_1\,\dot{\theta}_1^2$$

$$v_{c1}^2 = L_{c1}^2\,\dot{\theta}_1^2$$

**For link 2** (apply $\sin^2+\cos^2 = 1$ and the cosine difference identity $\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2 - \theta_1) = \cos\theta_2$):

$$v_{c2}^2 = L_1^2\dot{\theta}_1^2 + L_{c2}^2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2 + 2L_1 L_{c2}\dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2$$

### 3.4 Step 4 — Kinetic Energy

$$K = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}m_1 v_{c1}^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}I_1\dot{\theta}_1^2}_{K_1} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}m_2 v_{c2}^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}I_2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2}_{K_2}$$

Substituting and expanding:

$$K = \tfrac{1}{2}(m_1 L_{c1}^2 + I_1)\dot{\theta}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}m_2 L_1^2\dot{\theta}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}(m_2 L_{c2}^2 + I_2)(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2 + m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2$$

### 3.5 Step 5 — Potential Energy

$$V = m_1 g\,y_{c1} + m_2 g\,y_{c2}$$

$$V = (m_1 L_{c1} + m_2 L_1)\,g\sin\theta_1 + m_2 L_{c2}\,g\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

### 3.6 Step 6 — Lagrangian

$$\mathcal{L} = K - V$$

### 3.7 Step 7 — Apply Euler–Lagrange for Joint 1

**Compute $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{\theta}_1$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1} = (m_1 L_{c1}^2 + m_2 L_1^2 + I_1)\dot{\theta}_1 + (m_2 L_{c2}^2 + I_2)(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2) + m_2 L_1 L_{c2}(2\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2$$

**Compute $d/dt(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{\theta}_1)$:** Differentiate each term (the last term produces a product rule because both $(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)$ and $\cos\theta_2$ depend on time).

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1}\right) &= (m_1 L_{c1}^2 + m_2 L_1^2 + I_1)\ddot{\theta}_1 + (m_2 L_{c2}^2 + I_2)(\ddot{\theta}_1+\ddot{\theta}_2) \\
&\quad + m_2 L_1 L_{c2}(2\ddot{\theta}_1+\ddot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2 \\
&\quad - m_2 L_1 L_{c2}(2\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \theta_1$:** Only $V$ depends on $\theta_1$ (and indirectly via $\cos\theta_2$ through the $K$ term — but $\cos\theta_2$ is treated as a constant w.r.t. $\theta_1$ for the partial derivative).

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_1} = -m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)\sin\theta_2 - (m_1 L_{c1} + m_2 L_1)g\cos\theta_1 - m_2 L_{c2}\,g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

**Form the equation $\tau_1 = d/dt(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{\theta}_1) - \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \theta_1$:** After algebra and grouping terms in $\ddot{\theta}_1$, $\ddot{\theta}_2$, velocity products, and gravity:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &= \Big[(m_1 L_{c1}^2 + m_2 L_1^2 + I_1) + (m_2 L_{c2}^2 + I_2) + 2m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\cos\theta_2\Big]\ddot{\theta}_1 \\
&\quad + \Big[(m_2 L_{c2}^2 + I_2) + m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\cos\theta_2\Big]\ddot{\theta}_2 \\
&\quad - m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,(2\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 + \dot{\theta}_2^{\,2}) \\
&\quad + (m_1 L_{c1} + m_2 L_1)g\cos\theta_1 + m_2 L_{c2}\,g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.8 Step 8 — Apply Euler–Lagrange for Joint 2

By a parallel derivation (shorter because $\theta_2$ appears only via $\theta_1+\theta_2$ and $\sin\theta_2,\cos\theta_2$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_2 &= \Big[(m_2 L_{c2}^2 + I_2) + m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\cos\theta_2\Big]\ddot{\theta}_1 + (m_2 L_{c2}^2 + I_2)\ddot{\theta}_2 \\
&\quad + m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_1^{\,2} + m_2 L_{c2}\,g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.9 Step 9 — Assemble the Matrix Form

$$\begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{21} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{bmatrix}\ddot{\theta}_1 \\ \ddot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} h_1 \\ h_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} G_1 \\ G_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}\tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

where the entries are exactly the ones tabulated in §2.4.

### 3.10 Step 10 — Forward Dynamics: Solving for $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$

Given $\boldsymbol{\tau}$, $\mathbf{q}$, $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$:

$$\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{M}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\big[\,\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})\,\big]$$

$$\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{\det\mathbf{M}}\begin{bmatrix} M_{22} & -M_{12} \\ -M_{21} & M_{11} \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 - h_1 - G_1 \\ \tau_2 - h_2 - G_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Integrate $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ to obtain $\dot{\mathbf{q}}(t)$ and $\mathbf{q}(t)$ — typically using **Runge–Kutta 4th order** in MATLAB's `ode45`.

### 3.11 Step 11 — Inverse Dynamics: Computing $\boldsymbol{\tau}$

Given desired trajectory $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$, $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_d(t)$, $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d(t)$:

$$\tau_1 = M_{11}\ddot{\theta}_{1d} + M_{12}\ddot{\theta}_{2d} + h_1 + G_1$$

$$\tau_2 = M_{21}\ddot{\theta}_{1d} + M_{22}\ddot{\theta}_{2d} + h_2 + G_2$$

These are evaluated **once per sample time** ($T_s$ typically 1 ms in hardware). The result is the torque command sent to the joint motors.

### 3.12 Numerical Illustration (Worked Example)

Let $L_1 = L_2 = 1\;\mathrm{m}$, $m_1 = m_2 = 1\;\mathrm{kg}$, $L_{c1} = L_{c2} = 0.5\;\mathrm{m}$, $I_1 = I_2 = \tfrac{1}{12}\;\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$, $g = 9.81\;\mathrm{m/s^2}$, $\theta_1 = 90^\circ$, $\theta_2 = 0^\circ$, $\dot{\theta}_1 = \dot{\theta}_2 = 1\;\mathrm{rad/s}$, $\ddot{\theta}_1 = \ddot{\theta}_2 = 0$.

Compute the matrices:
- $M_{11} = (1\cdot 0.25 + 1\cdot 1 + 0.0833) + (1\cdot 0.25 + 0.0833) + 2(1)(1)(0.5)\cos 0^\circ = 1.3333 + 0.3333 + 1.0 = 2.6667$
- $M_{12} = M_{21} = 0.3333 + 0.5 = 0.8333$
- $M_{22} = 0.3333$
- $h_1 = -0.5\sin 0^\circ \cdot(\cdots) = 0$
- $h_2 = 0.5\sin 0^\circ \cdot(1)^2 = 0$
- $G_1 = (0.5 + 1)(9.81)\cos 90^\circ + 0.5(9.81)\cos 90^\circ = 0$
- $G_2 = 0.5(9.81)\cos 90^\circ = 0$

Inverse dynamics output: $\tau_1 = 0$, $\tau_2 = 0$ (no torque needed since gravity is horizontal and the configuration is not accelerating).

Forward dynamics output for any applied $\tau_1, \tau_2$:
- $\det \mathbf{M} = 2.6667 \cdot 0.3333 - 0.8333^2 = 0.8889 - 0.6944 = 0.1944$
- $\ddot{\theta}_1 = (0.3333\,\tau_1 - 0.8333\,\tau_2)/0.1944 = 1.714\,\tau_1 - 4.286\,\tau_2$
- $\ddot{\theta}_2 = (-0.8333\,\tau_1 + 2.6667\,\tau_2)/0.1944 = -4.286\,\tau_1 + 13.71\,\tau_2$

### 4.6 Typical MATLAB/Simulink Procedure (Textual Description — No Code Snippets)

1. Open MATLAB and create a new script (`.m` file).
2. Define symbolic variables for $q_1, q_2, \dot q_1, \dot q_2, \ddot q_1, \ddot q_2$ and link parameters.
3. Use the `DH` (Denavit–Hartenberg) module to build the 2-link robot.
4. Invoke the toolbox's `SerialLink` object with link inertias $I_i$, masses $m_i$, COM vectors $r_i$.
5. Call `R.accel(q, qd, tau)` → returns $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ (this is **forward dynamics**).
6. Call `R.rne(q, qd, qdd)` → returns $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ (this is **inverse dynamics**, also called RNE for Recursive Newton–Euler).
7. For a desired trajectory $q_d(t) = q_{d0} + A\sin(\omega t)$, evaluate `R.rne` at each time step.
8. Plot $\tau_1(t)$ and $\tau_2(t)$ for ID; plot $\theta_1(t), \theta_2(t)$ for FD.

### 4.7 Expected Observations in the Lab

- The **gravity term $G(\theta)$** dominates at low speeds.
- The **Coriolis term $h$** dominates at high speeds (it's proportional to $\dot\theta^2$).
- The **mass matrix** is symmetric and always **positive-definite** (determinant > 0) for reachable configurations.
- When $\theta_2 = 90^\circ$ (link 2 perpendicular to link 1), the off-diagonal coupling $M_{12}$ is zero (geometric decoupling).

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module Experiment 2]**

Differentiate between **forward dynamics** and **inverse dynamics** of a robotic manipulator. State one industrial application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Forward Dynamics | Inverse Dynamics |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ and current state $(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ | Desired trajectory $(\mathbf{q}_d,\dot{\mathbf{q}}_d,\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d)$ |
| Output | Joint accelerations $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ (then $\dot{\mathbf{q}},\mathbf{q}$ by integration) | Joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ |
| Direction of causality | Force $\rightarrow$ Motion | Motion $\rightarrow$ Force |
| Industrial use | **Simulation / virtual commissioning** of a robot cell | **Computed-torque feed-forward** in real-time motor control |
| Typical tool | Multi-body simulators (ADAMS, Simscape) | Recursive Newton–Euler algorithm at 1 kHz |

*(Award 1 mark for the correct definition of FD, 1 mark for ID, 1 mark for valid applications.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module Experiment 2]**

List the three matrices/vectors that constitute the standard dynamic model $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ and state the **physical significance** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ — Mass/Inertia matrix** ($2 \times 2$ for our case): Represents the configuration-dependent effective inertia at the joints; it is symmetric and positive-definite.
2. **$\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ — Coriolis–Centrifugal matrix**: Captures the velocity-dependent coupling forces that arise due to rotation in a non-inertial frame.
3. **$\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ — Gravity vector**: The static torques required at the joints to hold the manipulator in equilibrium against Earth's gravity.

*(Award 1 mark per correct item with significance.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **Instructions assumed:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the **Euler–Lagrange dynamic equations** for a 2-DOF planar RR manipulator with link lengths $L_1, L_2$, masses $m_1, m_2$, centre-of-mass distances $L_{c1}, L_{c2}$, and moments of inertia $I_1, I_2$.
