Express your final answer in the standard matrix form $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$. **State clearly all intermediate steps** (positions, velocities, kinetic and potential energies). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the parameter values $L_1 = 0.5\;\mathrm{m}$, $L_2 = 0.4\;\mathrm{m}$, $m_1 = 2\;\mathrm{kg}$, $m_2 = 1.5\;\mathrm{kg}$, $L_{c1} = 0.25\;\mathrm{m}$, $L_{c2} = 0.2\;\mathrm{m}$, $I_1 = 0.05\;\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$, $I_2 = 0.02\;\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$, and the instantaneous state $\theta_1 = 60^\circ$, $\theta_2 = 30^\circ$, $\dot{\theta}_1 = 0.5\;\mathrm{rad/s}$, $\dot{\theta}_2 = 0.2\;\mathrm{rad/s}$, $\ddot{\theta}_1 = 1.0\;\mathrm{rad/s^2}$, $\ddot{\theta}_2 = 0.8\;\mathrm{rad/s^2}$, $g = 9.81\;\mathrm{m/s^2}$:

- (i) Compute the **mass matrix** $\mathbf{M}$ and its **determinant**.
- (ii) Using the **inverse dynamics formula**, compute the joint torques $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Full derivation:** Refer to §3.1 to §3.9 of these notes. The student must show:
1. Positions of COM (3.1) — 1 mark
2. Velocities and squared speeds (3.2, 3.3) — 1 mark
3. Kinetic and potential energy expressions (3.4, 3.5) — 1 mark
4. Lagrangian and Euler–Lagrange equations for both joints (3.6, 3.7, 3.8) — 2 marks
5. Final matrix form with clearly identified $\mathbf{M}$, $\mathbf{h}$, $\mathbf{G}$ (3.9) — 2 marks

**(b) Numerical evaluation:**

**(i) Compute $M_{ij}$:**

- $M_{22} = m_2 L_{c2}^2 + I_2 = 1.5 \times 0.04 + 0.02 = 0.06 + 0.02 = 0.08\;\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$
- $M_{12} = M_{21} = M_{22} + m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\cos\theta_2 = 0.08 + 1.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.2 \times \cos 30^\circ$
- $m_2 L_1 L_{c2} = 1.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.2 = 0.15$
- $0.15 \times 0.8660 = 0.1299$
- $M_{12} = 0.08 + 0.1299 = 0.2099\;\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$
- $M_{11} = m_1 L_{c1}^2 + m_2 L_1^2 + I_1 + M_{22} + 2 m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\cos\theta_2$
- $m_1 L_{c1}^2 = 2 \times 0.0625 = 0.125$
- $m_2 L_1^2 = 1.5 \times 0.25 = 0.375$
- $I_1 = 0.05$
- $2 m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\cos\theta_2 = 2 \times 0.1299 = 0.2598$
- $M_{11} = 0.125 + 0.375 + 0.05 + 0.08 + 0.2598 = 0.8898\;\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$

- $\det \mathbf{M} = M_{11} M_{22} - M_{12}^2 = 0.8898 \times 0.08 - 0.2099^2 = 0.07118 - 0.04406 = 0.02712\;\mathrm{kg^2\cdot m^4}$

**(ii) Inverse dynamics:**

- $h_1 = -m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,(2\dot\theta_1\dot\theta_2 + \dot\theta_2^{\,2})$
  - $\sin 30^\circ = 0.5$; $2\dot\theta_1\dot\theta_2 = 2 \times 0.5 \times 0.2 = 0.2$; $\dot\theta_2^{\,2} = 0.04$; sum = $0.24$
  - $h_1 = -0.15 \times 0.5 \times 0.24 = -0.018\;\mathrm{N\cdot m}$
- $h_2 = m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot\theta_1^{\,2}$
  - $\dot\theta_1^{\,2} = 0.25$
  - $h_2 = 0.15 \times 0.5 \times 0.25 = 0.01875\;\mathrm{N\cdot m}$
- $G_1 = (m_1 L_{c1} + m_2 L_1)g\cos\theta_1 + m_2 L_{c2}\,g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$
  - $m_1 L_{c1} + m_2 L_1 = 2 \times 0.25 + 1.5 \times 0.5 = 0.5 + 0.75 = 1.25$
  - $\cos 60^\circ = 0.5$; $\cos 90^\circ = 0$
  - $G_1 = 1.25 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 + 0 = 6.131\;\mathrm{N\cdot m}$
- $G_2 = m_2 L_{c2}\,g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) = 1.5 \times 0.2 \times 9.81 \times 0 = 0$

**Compute $\tau_1$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &= M_{11}\ddot{\theta}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{\theta}_2 + h_1 + G_1 \\
&= 0.8898 \times 1.0 + 0.2099 \times 0.8 + (-0.018) + 6.131 \\
&= 0.8898 + 0.1679 - 0.018 + 6.131 \\
&= 7.171\;\mathrm{N\cdot m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $\tau_2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_2 &= M_{21}\ddot{\theta}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{\theta}_2 + h_2 + G_2 \\
&= 0.2099 \times 1.0 + 0.08 \times 0.8 + 0.01875 + 0 \\
&= 0.2099 + 0.064 + 0.01875 \\
&= 0.293\;\mathrm{N\cdot m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\tau_1 \approx 7.17\;\mathrm{N\cdot m}, \quad \tau_2 \approx 0.29\;\mathrm{N\cdot m}}$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model, Alternative]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat flowchart**, describe the **forward dynamics** algorithm for a 2-DOF robotic manipulator. State the equation used and explain the role of the inverse of the mass matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-DOF RR manipulator has the same parameters as in Q-A(b). At the instant $\theta_1 = 30^\circ$, $\theta_2 = 45^\circ$, $\dot{\theta}_1 = \dot{\theta}_2 = 0$ (static), torques $\tau_1 = 5\;\mathrm{N\cdot m}$ and $\tau_2 = 2\;\mathrm{N\cdot m}$ are applied.

- (i) Compute the **mass matrix** and its inverse.
- (ii) Compute the **resulting joint accelerations** $\ddot{\theta}_1$ and $\ddot{\theta}_2$ using the **forward dynamics equation**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Forward dynamics algorithm (7 marks):**

The student must:
1. State the FD equation $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{M}^{-1}[\boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{h} - \mathbf{G}]$ — 2 marks
2. Provide a step-wise procedure (inputs, computation of M, h, G, invert M, multiply) — 2 marks
3. Draw a **flowchart** (use the structure from §4.4 of these notes) — 2 marks
4. State that the inverse of $\mathbf{M}$ exists because $\mathbf{M}$ is positive-definite for any reachable configuration (so $\det \mathbf{M} > 0$) — 1 mark

**(b) Numerical evaluation:**

**(i) Compute $\mathbf{M}$:**

- $M_{22} = 0.08\;\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$
- $m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\cos\theta_2 = 0.15 \times \cos 45^\circ = 0.15 \times 0.7071 = 0.1061$
- $M_{12} = M_{21} = 0.08 + 0.1061 = 0.1861\;\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$
- $2 m_2 L_1 L_{c2}\cos\theta_2 = 0.2122$
- $M_{11} = 0.125 + 0.375 + 0.05 + 0.08 + 0.2122 = 0.8422\;\mathrm{kg\cdot m^2}$

- $\det \mathbf{M} = 0.8422 \times 0.08 - 0.1861^2 = 0.06738 - 0.03463 = 0.03275\;\mathrm{kg^2\cdot m^4}$

- $\mathbf{M}^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.03275}\begin{bmatrix} 0.08 & -0.1861 \\ -0.1861 & 0.8422 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.443 & -5.683 \\ -5.683 & 25.72 \end{bmatrix}\;\mathrm{1/(kg\cdot m^2)}$

**(ii) At static state, $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}$:**

- $G_1 = 1.25 \times 9.81 \times \cos 30^\circ + 1.5 \times 0.2 \times 9.81 \times \cos 75^\circ$
  - $\cos 30^\circ = 0.8660$; $\cos 75^\circ = 0.2588$
  - $G_1 = 1.25 \times 9.81 \times 0.8660 + 0.3 \times 9.81 \times 0.2588$
  - $G_1 = 10.617 + 0.762 = 11.379\;\mathrm{N\cdot m}$
- $G_2 = 0.3 \times 9.81 \times 0.2588 = 0.762\;\mathrm{N\cdot m}$

- Net torque vector: $\mathbf{F}_N = \boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 - 11.379 \\ 2 - 0.762 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -6.379 \\ 1.238 \end{bmatrix}\;\mathrm{N\cdot m}$

- $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{M}^{-1} \mathbf{F}_N$
- $\ddot{\theta}_1 = 2.443 \times (-6.379) + (-5.683) \times 1.238 = -15.586 - 7.034 = -22.62\;\mathrm{rad/s^2}$
- $\ddot{\theta}_2 = -5.683 \times (-6.379) + 25.72 \times 1.238 = 36.246 + 31.84 = 68.09\;\mathrm{rad/s^2}$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\ddot{\theta}_1 \approx -22.62\;\mathrm{rad/s^2}, \quad \ddot{\theta}_2 \approx 68.09\;\mathrm{rad/s^2}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $h_1$**: Students often forget the factor of $2$ in the term $2\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2$ — costing **2 marks** in the ID computation.
> 2. **Confusing $\sin\theta_2$ with $\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$**: The Coriolis term uses $\sin\theta_2$ (the *relative* angle), but the gravity term on link 2 uses $\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ (the *absolute* angle). Mixing these loses **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the moment of inertia $I_i$**: A very common mistake is to write the kinetic energy using $K = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ only, omitting the rotational $\tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ term. This leads to wrong $\mathbf{M}$ matrix. **Cost: up to 3 marks.**
> 4. **Unit mismatch**: Failing to keep all lengths in metres and masses in kg produces numerically wrong answers. Always carry units symbolically and only substitute at the end.
> 5. **In forward dynamics, students often write $\mathbf{M}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\tau}$ only** — forgetting to subtract $\mathbf{h}$ and $\mathbf{G}$. This is the most common conceptual error. **Cost: 4 marks.**
> 6. **Determinant sign**: If a student computes $\det \mathbf{M} < 0$, the configuration is unreachable and the answer is unphysical — the student should re-check the kinematic feasibility of the chosen $\theta$ values.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Lab Exam:**

- [ ] **Two problems, two directions**: FD is force→motion; ID is motion→force.
- [ ] **General equation** (memorize verbatim): $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.
- [ ] **Dimensions for 2-DOF**: $\mathbf{M}$ is $2\times 2$, symmetric, positive-definite, depends only on $\mathbf{q}$ (not $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$).
- [ ] **$M_{22}$ is the simplest entry** — it is *always* $m_2 L_{c2}^2 + I_2$, independent of $\theta$.
- [ ] **$M_{12}$ contains $\cos\theta_2$** (relative joint angle), not $\cos\theta_1$.
- [ ] **Coriolis/centrifugal terms contain $\sin\theta_2$** (the *only* source of velocity coupling in the planar 2-DOF case).
- [ ] **Gravity terms** use absolute angle $\theta_1$ or $\theta_1+\theta_2$ — be careful with the trig function choice.
- [ ] **Forward dynamics** requires inverting a $2\times 2$ matrix — use the formula $\mathbf{M}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det\mathbf{M}}\begin{bmatrix} M_{22} & -M_{12} \\ -M_{21} & M_{11} \end{bmatrix}$.
- [ ] **Inverse dynamics** is a direct matrix-vector multiply — no inversion needed.
- [ ] **Software workflow** (MATLAB Robotics Toolbox): `SerialLink.accel()` for FD, `SerialLink.rne()` for ID.
- [ ] **Physical sanity checks**:
  - At $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 0$ (links horizontal, gravity is pure shear), $G$ is **maximum**.
  - At $\theta_1 = 90^\circ$ (links vertical), $G = 0$.
  - At zero velocity, only $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ is active.
  - At zero acceleration and zero gravity, $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$.
- [ ] **Industrial relevance**: ID runs at 1 kHz on every modern robot controller; FD is the basis of all robot simulators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building and programming a simple Arduino-based robot for basic movement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Building and programming a simple Arduino-based robot for basic movement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Arduino-based mobile robot for basic movement** is a programmable, wheeled autonomous (or semi-autonomous) electro-mechanical platform that uses an Arduino microcontroller as its central processing and control unit. The system integrates **DC geared motors** (typically 2 or 4), a **motor driver IC** (commonly the L298N H-Bridge), a **chassis**, a **power source** (battery pack), and supporting electronics (wheels, casters, jumper wires, sensors) to achieve controlled locomotion across the three primary degrees of freedom of planar motion:

1. **Forward Translation** (along the +X axis)
2. **Reverse Translation** (along the -X axis)
3. **In-place Yaw Rotation** (left/right turning about the Z axis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment directly satisfies the course outcome **CO2** – *"Design and implement basic robotic systems using microcontrollers for industrial automation applications."* It is a **mandatory practical** in Module 2 of the PCPEL607 lab manual and is evaluated for **15 marks** in the continuous internal assessment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine you are **teaching a pet dog to pull a small cart**. You don't tell the dog the physics equations of friction, traction, or torque. Instead, you give it simple verbal commands:

- "Walk forward" → both legs move forward together
- "Back up" → both legs move backward
- "Turn left" → right legs go forward, left legs stay (or reverse)
- "Stop" → both legs freeze

The Arduino robot works **exactly the same way**. The Arduino board is the "brain" (your voice), the motor driver is the "translator" that converts your small voice into strong leg muscle commands, and the DC motors are the "legs" that actually move the cart. The wheels are the dog's paws gripping the floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A simple Arduino robot is essentially a **remote-controlled toy car** whose "remote control" is a small program written in the Arduino IDE (C/C++ based), uploaded to the microcontroller via USB, and executed autonomously once powered.

### 1.3 Key Standard Parameters & Hardware Specifications

| Component | Standard Specification Used in KTU Labs |
|---|---|
| Microcontroller | **Arduino Uno R3** (ATmega328P, 16 MHz, 14 digital I/O pins) |
| Motor Driver | **L298N Dual H-Bridge** (logic voltage: 5 V, drive voltage: 5–35 V, max current: 2 A per channel) |
| Motors | **BO Motor / Johnson Motor** (Geared DC, 3–12 V, 100–300 RPM, 2 motors) |
| Wheels | 65 mm rubber tires + 1 caster / ball wheel |
| Power Source | **7.4 V Li-ion / Li-Po 2S pack** OR **4× AA 1.5 V battery holder (6 V)** |
| Chassis | Acrylic / Mild Steel 4-Wheel chassis (approx. 200×150 mm) |
| Communication | USB Type-B (programming), optional HC-05 Bluetooth (wireless) |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Tip:** In industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), the same architecture is scaled up — replacing the Arduino with a **PLC** or **Raspberry Pi + ROS**, and the BO motors with **BLDC servo motors with optical encoders**. The fundamental H-bridge control logic remains identical.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Operational Principles

The basic-movement robot operates on **three fundamental pillars** of automation engineering:

#### Pillar 1 — The H-Bridge Motor Driver Concept
A DC motor spins in one direction when current flows from Terminal A to B, and reverses when the current is reversed. An **H-Bridge** is an electronic circuit built using 4 switches (transistors/MOSFETs) arranged in an "H" shape around the motor. By selectively closing diagonal pairs of switches, you can drive current in either direction through the motor.

**Four Switching States of the L298N:**

| IN1 | IN2 | IN3 | IN4 | Left Motor | Right Motor | Robot Action |
|-----|-----|-----|-----|------------|-------------|--------------|
| HIGH | LOW | HIGH | LOW | Forward | Forward | **Move Forward** |
| LOW | HIGH | LOW | HIGH | Reverse | Reverse | **Move Backward** |
| HIGH | LOW | LOW | HIGH | Forward | Reverse | **Turn Right** |
| LOW | HIGH | HIGH | LOW | Reverse | Forward | **Turn Left** |
| LOW | LOW | LOW | LOW | Stop | Stop | **Brake/Stop** |
| HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | Stop | Stop | **Brake/Stop** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "H" Shape Explained:** If you draw the circuit, the motor sits in the middle of the "H" and the 4 switches form the 4 vertical legs. Closing the top-left + bottom-right switch pair drives current one way; closing the top-right + bottom-left pair drives it the other way. This is the **single most-tested concept** in KTU viva voce for this experiment.

#### Pillar 2 — Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) for Speed Control
The Arduino cannot output a true analog voltage on its digital pins. Instead, it uses **PWM** — a technique where the pin is switched HIGH and LOW so rapidly that the *average* voltage delivered to the motor is reduced. This average voltage controls the motor speed.

- PWM Frequency on Arduino Uno: **~490 Hz** on most pins, **~980 Hz** on pins 5 and 6
- PWM Resolution: **8-bit** → values from **0 (0% duty cycle) to 255 (100% duty cycle)**

#### Pillar 3 — Differential Steering (Kinematic Model)
A two-wheeled robot with a passive caster achieves turning by **differential wheel speed**. Unlike a car which uses Ackermann steering, the robot simply spins the left and right wheels at different rates (or in opposite directions) to rotate about its center.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{avg} = V_s \times D$ | Average voltage to motor from PWM | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{total}}$ | Duty Cycle (0 to 1) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $D_{\%} = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | Duty Cycle percentage | Percent (%) |
| 4 | $PWM_{Arduino} = 255 \times D$ | Arduino `analogWrite()` value | Integer (0–255) |
| 5 | $V = I \times R$ | Ohm's Law — battery/power check | V, A, Ω |
| 6 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from rated RPM $N$ | rad/s |
| 7 | $v = \omega \times r$ | Linear wheel velocity | m/s |
| 8 | $I_{total} = 2 \times I_{motor} + I_{logic}$ | Total battery current draw | Amperes (A) |
| 9 | $t_{battery} = \dfrac{C_{battery}}{I_{total}}$ | Battery life (capacity in Ah ÷ current) | Hours (h) |
| 10 | $P = V \times I$ | Electrical power consumed | Watts (W) |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These equations are the bedrock of **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) design** in warehouse robotics (Amazon Kiva robots), **AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot)** fleet management in hospitals and factories, and **AGRICULTURE drones** for crop monitoring. Every wheeled industrial robot in a Toyota or Tesla plant uses differential steering + PWM motor control.

### 2.3 Pin Configuration Reference (Standard Wiring)

| Arduino Uno Pin | L298N Terminal | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| D5 (PWM) | ENA | Left motor speed control (PWM) |
| D6 (PWM) | ENB | Right motor speed control (PWM) |
| D7 | IN1 | Left motor direction bit 1 |
| D8 | IN2 | Left motor direction bit 2 |
| D9 | IN3 | Right motor direction bit 1 |
| D10 | IN4 | Right motor direction bit 2 |
| VIN / 5V | +5V (logic) | Logic supply to L298N |
| VIN (via switch) | +12V / V_MOT | Motor supply from battery |
| GND | GND | **Common ground (CRITICAL — must be shared)** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How `analogWrite(255)` Maps to 100% Motor Speed

The Arduino Uno's ATmega328P timer modules generate PWM using **fast PWM mode**. The timer counter increments from 0 to 255 (the TOP value for 8-bit mode), then resets to 0.

$$
\text{Threshold value} = \text{value written in OCRx register}
$$

When the timer counter value is **less than or equal to** the OCRx register value, the output pin is held **HIGH**. Otherwise, it is **LOW**.

**Step-by-step logical derivation:**

Let $T_{total}$ be the total PWM period. For Arduino Uno, the PWM frequency is approximately **490 Hz**, so:

$$
T_{total} = \dfrac{1}{f_{PWM}} = \dfrac{1}{490} \approx 2.04 \text{ ms}
$$

For any chosen `analogWrite(pin, value)` where $value \in [0, 255]$:

$$
D = \dfrac{value}{255}
$$

The HIGH time within one period is:

$$
T_{ON} = D \times T_{total} = \dfrac{value}{255} \times 2.04 \text{ ms}
$$

**Numerical Example — verifying half speed:**

Suppose the user writes `analogWrite(5, 128)`. The value 128 is approximately **half of 255**.

$$
D = \dfrac{128}{255} \approx 0.502
$$

$$
T_{ON} = 0.502 \times 2.04 \text{ ms} \approx 1.024 \text{ ms}
$$

The motor receives ~1.024 ms of HIGH and ~1.016 ms of LOW in every 2.04 ms cycle. The average voltage is therefore:

$$
V_{avg} = 5\text{ V} \times 0.502 \approx 2.51 \text{ V}
$$

The motor rotates at roughly **half of its maximum speed**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Robot Linear Velocity from Motor RPM

A standard BO motor with 65 mm rubber wheel and gear ratio of 1:48 has a free-run RPM of approximately **200 RPM** at 6 V.

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to angular velocity:**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \times \pi \times 200}{60} \approx 20.94 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert angular velocity to linear wheel velocity** using the wheel radius $r = 0.0325$ m (65 mm diameter):

$$
v_{wheel} = \omega \times r = 20.94 \times 0.0325 \approx 0.681 \text{ m/s}
$$

This is the maximum theoretical forward speed. With PWM at 50% duty, the actual speed is halved:

$$
v_{robot, 50\%} = 0.5 \times 0.681 \approx 0.340 \text{ m/s}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: PWM-to-`analogWrite` Value Conversion

Given a desired speed as a percentage, the Arduino `analogWrite` value is:

$$
\text{PWM}_{Arduino} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{Speed_{\%}}{100} \times 255 + 0.5 \right\rfloor
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:** A student wants the robot to move forward at 70% of maximum speed.

$$
\text{PWM}_{Arduino} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{70}{100} \times 255 + 0.5 \right\rfloor = \lfloor 178.5 + 0.5 \rfloor = \lfloor 179.0 \rfloor = 179
$$

So the code becomes: `analogWrite(5, 179);` and `analogWrite(6, 179);`

### 3.4 Complete Working Arduino Sketch (Annotated)

```cpp
/* ============================================================
 * KTU PCPEL607 - Module 2 Experiment 2
 * Basic Arduino Robot - Forward / Backward / Left / Right / Stop
 * Controller: Arduino Uno R3
 * Driver    : L298N Dual H-Bridge
 * ============================================================ */

// ---------- PIN DEFINITIONS ----------
// Motor A (Left Motor)
const int ENA = 5;     // PWM speed pin for Left Motor
const int IN1 = 7;     // Direction pin 1 for Left Motor
const int IN2 = 8;     // Direction pin 2 for Left Motor

// Motor B (Right Motor)
const int ENB = 6;     // PWM speed pin for Right Motor
const int IN3 = 9;     // Direction pin 1 for Right Motor
const int IN4 = 10;    // Direction pin 2 for Right Motor

// ---------- SPEED SETTINGS ----------
const int SPEED_FAST = 200;   // ~78% of max
const int SPEED_TURN = 150;   // ~59% of max (used for in-place turns)

// ---------- SETUP ----------
void setup() {
  // Configure all motor control pins as OUTPUT
  pinMode(ENA, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(ENB, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(IN1, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(IN2, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(IN3, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(IN4, OUTPUT);

  // Initialize Serial for debugging (9600 baud)
  Serial.begin(9600);
  Serial.println("KTU Basic Movement Robot - Initialized");
}

// ---------- MAIN LOOP ----------
void loop() {

  // 1. MOVE FORWARD for 2 seconds
  moveForward();
  delay(2000);

  // 2. STOP for 1 second
  stopRobot();
  delay(1000);

  // 3. TURN RIGHT for 1 second
  turnRight();
  delay(1000);

  // 4. STOP for 1 second
  stopRobot();
  delay(1000);

  // 5. MOVE BACKWARD for 2 seconds
  moveBackward();
  delay(2000);

  // 6. STOP for 1 second
  stopRobot();
  delay(1000);

  // 7. TURN LEFT for 1 second
  turnLeft();
  delay(1000);

  // 8. STOP for 1 second
  stopRobot();
  delay(1000);
}

// ---------- MOVEMENT FUNCTIONS ----------

void moveForward() {
  // Left motor forward
  digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH);
  digitalWrite(IN2, LOW);
  // Right motor forward
  digitalWrite(IN3, HIGH);
  digitalWrite(IN4, LOW);
  // Apply speed
  analogWrite(ENA, SPEED_FAST);
  analogWrite(ENB, SPEED_FAST);
}

void moveBackward() {
  // Left motor reverse
  digitalWrite(IN1, LOW);
  digitalWrite(IN2, HIGH);
  // Right motor reverse
  digitalWrite(IN3, LOW);
  digitalWrite(IN4, HIGH);
  analogWrite(ENA, SPEED_FAST);
  analogWrite(ENB, SPEED_FAST);
}

void turnRight() {
  // Left motor forward, Right motor reverse -> rotates clockwise
  digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH);
  digitalWrite(IN2, LOW);
  digitalWrite(IN3, LOW);
  digitalWrite(IN4, HIGH);
  analogWrite(ENA, SPEED_TURN);
  analogWrite(ENB, SPEED_TURN);
}

void turnLeft() {
  // Left motor reverse, Right motor forward -> rotates counter-clockwise
  digitalWrite(IN1, LOW);
  digitalWrite(IN2, HIGH);
  digitalWrite(IN3, HIGH);
  digitalWrite(IN4, LOW);
  analogWrite(ENA, SPEED_TURN);
  analogWrite(ENB, SPEED_TURN);
}

void stopRobot() {
  // Apply brake (both inputs HIGH) to each motor
  digitalWrite(IN1, LOW);
  digitalWrite(IN2, LOW);
  digitalWrite(IN3, LOW);
  digitalWrite(IN4, LOW);
  analogWrite(ENA, 0);
  analogWrite(ENB, 0);
}
```

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Movement Truth Table

| Robot Action | IN1 | IN2 | IN3 | IN4 | ENA (PWM) | ENB (PWM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 200 | 200 |
| Backward | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 200 | 200 |
| Turn Right | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 150 | 150 |
| Turn Left | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 150 | 150 |
| Stop (Coast) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Stop (Brake) | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 255 | 255 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Brake vs. Coast — an examiner favourite:** Setting both INx pins HIGH with ENA/ENB HIGH = "active brake" (motor terminals shorted through the low-side FETs, generating back-EMF that stops the rotor quickly). Setting both LOW with ENA/ENB = 0 is "coast" (motor spins down naturally over ~1 second).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USB Programming Port] -->|UART TX/RX| B[Arduino Uno R3 - ATmega328P]
    Bat[7.4V Li-ion Battery] -->|VIN via Switch| B
    Bat -->|Direct 7.4V| C[L298N Motor Driver - Motor Supply]
    B -->|5V Logic| C
    B -->|D5 PWM ENA| C
    B -->|D6 PWM ENB| C
    B -->|D7 IN1| C
    B -->|D8 IN2| C
    B -->|D9 IN3| C
    B -->|D10 IN4| C
    C -->|OUT1 OUT2| ML[Left DC Geared Motor]
    C -->|OUT3 OUT4| MR[Right DC Geared Motor]
    ML --> WL[Left Wheel 65mm]
    MR --> WR[Right Wheel 65mm]
    Chassis[Acrylic Chassis with Caster Wheel] --- ML
    Chassis --- MR
```

### 4.2 Program Execution Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Power ON / Reset]) --> P[pinMode configuration of all 6 pins]
    P --> L[loop function begins]
    L --> F1[Call moveForward - IN1=HIGH, IN2=LOW, IN3=HIGH, IN4=LOW]
    F1 --> D1[delay 2000 ms]
    D1 --> ST1[Call stopRobot - all INx=LOW, ENA=0, ENB=0]
    ST1 --> D2[delay 1000 ms]
    D2 --> T1[Call turnRight - IN1=H, IN2=L, IN3=L, IN4=H]
    T1 --> D3[delay 1000 ms]
    D3 --> ST2[Call stopRobot]
    ST2 --> D4[delay 1000 ms]
    D4 --> B1[Call moveBackward - IN1=L, IN2=H, IN3=L, IN4=H]
    B1 --> D5[delay 2000 ms]
    D5 --> ST3[Call stopRobot]
    ST3 --> D6[delay 1000 ms]
    D6 --> T2[Call turnLeft - IN1=L, IN2=H, IN3=H, IN4=L]
    T2 --> D7[delay 1000 ms]
    D7 --> ST4[Call stopRobot]
    ST4 --> D8[delay 1000 ms]
    D8 --> L
```

### 4.3 H-Bridge Internal Switching Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stop
    Stop --> Forward: IN1=HIGH, IN2=LOW, IN3=HIGH, IN4=LOW
    Forward --> Stop: All INx=LOW
    Stop --> Backward: IN1=LOW, IN2=HIGH, IN3=LOW, IN4=HIGH
    Backward --> Stop: All INx=LOW
    Stop --> TurnRight: IN1=HIGH, IN2=LOW, IN3=LOW, IN4=HIGH
    TurnRight --> Stop: All INx=LOW
    Stop --> TurnLeft: IN1=LOW, IN2=HIGH, IN3=HIGH, IN4=LOW
    TurnLeft --> Stop: All INx=LOW
    Forward --> TurnRight: Change IN3 IN4 polarity
    Backward --> TurnLeft: Change IN1 IN2 polarity
```

### 4.4 Hardware Power Distribution Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Power_Management
        BAT[Battery 7.4V 2200mAh Li-ion] --> SW[SPST Toggle Switch]
        SW --> ARD_VIN[Arduino VIN pin]
        SW --> L298_12V[L298N +12V terminal - Motor Power]
    end

    subgraph Arduino_Logic
        ARD_VIN --> REG[Onboard 5V Regulator]
        REG --> MC[ATmega328P Microcontroller]
        MC --> PINS[Digital I/O Pins D5 to D10]
    end

    subgraph Motor_Driver_Block
        L298_12V --> HBRIDGE[Internal H-Bridge Transistors]
        PINS --> HBRIDGE
        HBRIDGE --> OUT1[OUT1 - Motor A Terminal 1]
        HBRIDGE --> OUT2[OUT2 - Motor A Terminal 2]
        HBRIDGE --> OUT3[OUT3 - Motor B Terminal 1]
        HBRIDGE --> OUT4[OUT4 - Motor B Terminal 2]
    end

    GND[Common Ground Rail] --- ARD_VIN
    GND --- L298_12V
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Ground Rule (Frequently Asked in Viva):** The Arduino's **GND** pin and the L298N's **GND** terminal **MUST be connected together** with a jumper wire. Without this, the logic-level signals (HIGH/LOW) from the Arduino have no reference, and the L298N will not respond. This single wire is the cause of ~60% of "my robot is not working" complaints in KTU labs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q1.** What is an H-Bridge? Why is it required to drive a DC motor from an Arduino?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An H-Bridge is an electronic circuit consisting of **4 switching elements** (transistors or MOSFETs) arranged in an "H" topology around a DC motor. It is required because:
- (1 mark) Arduino digital pins can only output **±40 mA** maximum, which is insufficient to drive a motor drawing **200–500 mA**.
- (1 mark) The H-Bridge acts as a **current amplifier**, allowing a small logic signal from the Arduino to control a high-current motor.
- (1 mark) It also enables **bidirectional control** — by reversing current flow through the motor, the direction of rotation can be changed without any mechanical relay.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2.** Explain the concept of PWM. How does the Arduino Uno generate PWM, and what is the resolution?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a technique to simulate an analog output using a digital pin by varying the ratio of HIGH time to total cycle time (duty cycle).
- (1 mark) The Arduino Uno generates PWM using **hardware timers** inside the ATmega328P. The default PWM frequency is **~490 Hz** on pins 3, 9, 10, 11 and **~980 Hz** on pins 5, 6.
- (1 mark) The PWM **resolution is 8-bit**, meaning the duty cycle can be set in **256 discrete steps** from 0 (always OFF) to 255 (always ON) using the `analogWrite()` function.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Internal Lab Exam Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an Arduino-based differential-drive mobile robot. List all major hardware components and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Arduino program to make the robot perform the following sequence: move forward for 2 seconds, stop for 1 second, turn right for 1 second, stop for 1 second, move backward for 2 seconds, and then stop. Use pins 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 for ENA, ENB, IN1, IN2, IN3, IN4 respectively. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture & Components — 7 marks:**

The robot consists of **three functional layers**:

- **(1.5 marks) Power Layer:** 7.4 V Li-ion battery pack supplies power to the motor driver and Arduino (via onboard regulator). A SPST switch is used as a master kill switch.
- **(2 marks) Control Layer:** The Arduino Uno board houses the ATmega328P microcontroller. It receives user code via USB, executes instructions, and outputs **6 control signals** (2 PWM speed signals to ENA/ENB + 4 direction signals to IN1–IN4).
- **(1.5 marks) Actuation Layer:** The L298N motor driver receives 5 V logic signals from Arduino and switches high-current paths to the two DC geared motors, which in turn drive the rubber wheels mounted on the chassis. A passive caster wheel provides the third point of ground contact.
- **(2 marks) Component table** with motor (100–300 RPM), wheels (65 mm), chassis material (acrylic/MS), battery (2200 mAh), and connecting jumper wires must be drawn.

**(b) Arduino Program — 7 marks:**

```cpp
int ENA = 5, ENB = 6, IN1 = 7, IN2 = 8, IN3 = 9, IN4 = 10;

void setup() {
  pinMode(ENA, OUTPUT); pinMode(ENB, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(IN1, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN2, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(IN3, OUTPUT); pinMode(IN4, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  // Forward
  digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW);
  digitalWrite(IN3, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN4, LOW);
  analogWrite(ENA, 200); analogWrite(ENB, 200);
  delay(2000);
  // Stop
  analogWrite(ENA, 0); analogWrite(ENB, 0);
  delay(1000);
  // Turn Right
  digitalWrite(IN1, HIGH); digitalWrite(IN2, LOW);
  digitalWrite(IN3, LOW);  digitalWrite(IN4, HIGH);
  analogWrite(ENA, 150); analogWrite(ENB, 150);
  delay(1000);
  // Stop
  analogWrite(ENA, 0); analogWrite(ENB, 0);
  delay(1000);
  // Backward
  digitalWrite(IN1, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2, HIGH);
  digitalWrite(IN3, LOW); digitalWrite(IN4, HIGH);
  analogWrite(ENA, 200); analogWrite(ENB, 200);
  delay(2000);
  // Stop
  analogWrite(ENA, 0); analogWrite(ENB, 0);
  delay(1000);
}
```

**Mark split:** Correct pin declarations (1), `setup()` configuration (1), Forward logic (1), Right turn logic (1), Backward logic (1), Stop logic and timing (1), Code compiles and is well-commented (1).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the working of an L298N motor driver. With the help of a truth table, show how the four input pins (IN1, IN2, IN3, IN4) control the direction of two DC motors connected to OUT1–OUT4. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot uses two BO motors rated at **6 V, 200 RPM, 150 mA each**, powered by a 7.4 V 2200 mAh battery. Calculate: (i) the maximum linear speed of the robot if the wheel diameter is 65 mm, (ii) the theoretical battery runtime, and (iii) the PWM value needed to run the motors at 60% of maximum speed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) L298N Working & Truth Table — 7 marks:**
The L298N is a **dual full-bridge driver** IC housed in a 15-pin Multiwatt package. It contains two independent H-bridges, each capable of driving one DC motor. (2 marks for description). It accepts **TTL-compatible logic inputs** (0–5 V) on pins IN1–IN4, and uses an internal transistor array to switch the higher motor current. ENA and ENB are **enable pins** that also accept PWM signals for speed control. (2 marks).

**Truth Table — 3 marks:**

| ENA | IN1 | IN2 | Motor A Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | X | X | Stop (high impedance) |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | Brake (low-side slow decay) |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Forward |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Reverse |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Brake (high-side slow decay) |

(Same for Motor B with ENB/IN3/IN4)

**(b) Numerical Calculations — 7 marks:**

**(i) Maximum linear speed:** (2 marks)

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 200}{60} = 20.94 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{65 \text{ mm}}{2} = 0.0325 \text{ m}
$$

$$
v_{max} = \omega \times r = 20.94 \times 0.0325 \approx 0.681 \text{ m/s} \approx 68.1 \text{ cm/s}
$$

**(ii) Battery runtime:** (2 marks)

$$
I_{total} = 2 \times 0.150 \text{ A} = 0.300 \text{ A}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{C_{battery}}{I_{total}} = \dfrac{2.2 \text{ Ah}}{0.3 \text{ A}} \approx 7.33 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) PWM value for 60% speed:** (3 marks)

$$
PWM = \left\lfloor \dfrac{60}{100} \times 255 + 0.5 \right\rfloor = \lfloor 153.5 \rfloor = 153
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgot the common GND wire** between Arduino and L298N → −2 marks
> 2. **Confusing ENA/ENB** with IN1/IN4 → robot moves but does not turn → −2 marks
> 3. **No `pinMode()` in `setup()`** → digital pins float at random → −1 mark
> 4. **Using `digitalWrite(ENA, HIGH)` instead of `analogWrite(ENA, 200)`** → speed control will not work, only full ON/OFF → −2 marks
> 5. **Missing `delay()` between actions** → all movement actions blur into one → −1 mark
> 6. **Wrong polarity wiring** at motor terminals → robot reverses when code says forward → −1 mark
> 7. **In viva, students often claim the L298N "amplifies voltage"** — it does NOT; it only amplifies current. The motor voltage equals the battery voltage (minus ~2 V L298N dropout). Examiner deducts 1 mark for this misconception.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ An Arduino-based basic-movement robot is a **differential-drive, 2-wheel + caster** platform controlled by an **L298N H-Bridge**.
- ✅ The Arduino cannot directly drive motors due to **insufficient current (~40 mA)** — the L298N provides current amplification up to **2 A per channel**.
- ✅ **PWM** is used for speed control; Arduino's `analogWrite()` accepts values **0–255** (8-bit resolution) producing frequencies of **~490 Hz** or **~980 Hz**.
- ✅ The **average motor voltage** = supply voltage × duty cycle.
- ✅ **Direction control** uses 2 input pins per motor: `IN1=HIGH, IN2=LOW` = forward; `IN1=LOW, IN2=HIGH` = reverse; both LOW or both HIGH = stop.
- ✅ **Differential steering** is achieved by spinning left and right wheels in opposite directions for in-place turns.
- ✅ **Common GND** between Arduino and L298N is **MANDATORY** — never omit it.
- ✅ The **wheel linear velocity** is given by $v = \omega r$ where $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.
- ✅ The **PWM register value** for a desired speed % is $\text{value} = \dfrac{\text{speed\%}}{100} \times 255$.
- ✅ The **battery life** in hours is $t = \dfrac{\text{capacity in Ah}}{\text{total current draw}}$.
- ✅ For KTU lab exam, ensure the code has **well-named variables**, **clear comments**, and **structured functions** like `moveForward()`, `turnRight()`, etc. — examiners reward modularity.
- ✅ **Viva-favourite questions:** (1) What is an H-Bridge? (2) Why is PWM needed? (3) What is the role of ENA/ENB? (4) What happens if you reverse both motor wires? (5) Why is common ground needed?
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a simple program to control a robot's motion (e.g., moving forward, backward, turning) using programming commands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a simple program to control a robot's motion (e.g., moving forward, backward, turning) using programming commands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Robot Motion Control Programming** is the process of developing sequential, logic-based instruction sets (programs) that command an autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic system to execute specific locomotion tasks—such as translation (forward/backward motion), rotation (turning), and complex path traversal—by issuing low-level actuator signals (motor PWM, direction logic, encoder feedback) to the robot's drive mechanism.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPEL607)**, this experiment typically involves programming a **differential-drive mobile robot** (the most common educational platform) using a high-level logic loop that translates abstract motion commands ("MOVE FORWARD 1 METER", "TURN LEFT 90°") into discrete wheel actuation signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The lab focuses on *event-driven sequential logic*, not advanced AI path planning. The program must demonstrate clear control flow: command acquisition → kinematic computation → actuator actuation → verification (sensor feedback).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Remote-Control Toy Car with a Brain"

Imagine a toy car that you control not with a joystick, but by writing a **to-do list on a piece of paper** that the car reads every second:

1. *"Read my first instruction: drive forward at 50% speed for 3 seconds."*
2. *"Read my second instruction: stop both wheels."*
3. *"Read my third instruction: spin the left wheel backward and right wheel forward for 1 second — this makes me turn right."*
4. *"Done. Park the motors."*

That piece of paper is your **program**. The car's electric motors are its **actuators**. The list of instructions is the **control algorithm**. The car's wheels are its **end-effectors for locomotion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Parallel:** This is exactly how NASA's Mars Rover "Perseverance" receives its daily motion commands from Earth — a sequence of `(drive_distance, turn_angle, drive_distance...)` blocks that the rover executes autonomously between communication windows.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in KTU-evaluated robot motion programs:

- **Wheel Radius (r):** Typically **5 cm to 10 cm** for educational platforms (e.g., 65 mm for the popular 2WD Arduino chassis).
- **Wheelbase / Track Length (L):** Distance between the two drive wheels — typically **15 cm to 20 cm**.
- **Gear Ratio (G):** Ratio between motor shaft and wheel shaft — usually **1:48** for hobby DC gear motors.
- **Encoder Resolution (CPR):** Counts per revolution — typically **360 CPR** (single channel) for optical encoders.
- **Standard Turn Angle for Testing:** **90°** (quarter turn) — most common benchmark in KTU lab rubrics.
- **Standard Test Distance:** **1 meter** — typical straight-line benchmark.

> [!TIP]
> **Why these numbers?** The 90° turn and 1-meter distance are *test-bench standards* because they are easy to verify with a protractor and measuring tape in the lab, and they allow examiners to quickly grade the robot's accuracy (typically ±5° and ±5 cm tolerance).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Robot Motion Program — Structured Logic Breakdown

A well-engineered robot motion program (per KTU 2024 evaluation standards) consists of **five mandatory logical blocks**:

### Block 1: Initialization (Setup Phase)
- Configure GPIO pins for motor driver (e.g., L298N: IN1, IN2, IN3, IN4).
- Set PWM frequency for speed control (typically **490 Hz** or **980 Hz** on Arduino UNO).
- Define constants: `WHEEL_RADIUS`, `WHEELBASE`, `GEAR_RATIO`.
- Reset encoders to zero.
- **Why:** Prevents undefined-pin floating states that cause motors to twitch on startup (a common 1-mark deduction).

### Block 2: Command Input / State Definition
- Hard-code motion primitives as functions or macros:
  - `moveForward(speed, duration)`
  - `moveBackward(speed, duration)`
  - `turnLeft(angle)`
  - `turnRight(angle)`
  - `stopMotors()`
- **Why:** Modular programming is a **2-mark valuation point** under "Code Quality" in KTU rubrics.

### Block 3: Kinematic Translation (The Brain)
- Convert high-level commands (e.g., "turn 90°") into low-level actuator timings or encoder counts using the **differential drive equations** (see Section 3).
- **Why:** This is where the *robotics theory* meets *embedded programming* — the core learning outcome of this experiment.

### Block 4: Actuation Phase
- Write digital HIGH/LOW to motor direction pins.
- Write analog PWM values (0–255 on Arduino) to enable pins.
- **Why:** Direction control requires *two pins per motor* (H-bridge logic).

### Block 5: Feedback / Verification
- Read encoder counts to confirm distance traveled.
- Use `delay()` or non-blocking timers to approximate motion completion.
- **Why:** Open-loop control is acceptable for KTU labs, but feedback-loop awareness earns **bonus marks** for advanced learners.

## 2.2 The H-Bridge Direction Logic (Critical for Backward Motion)

To move **backward**, the polarity of voltage across the motor terminals must be **reversed**. This is achieved via the H-Bridge:

| IN1 (Motor A) | IN2 (Motor A) | Motor A Action |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| HIGH | LOW | **Forward** |
| LOW | HIGH | **Backward** |
| LOW | LOW | **Coast / Stop (free roll)** |
| HIGH | HIGH | **Brake / Lock** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Setting both IN1 and IN2 to the same state creates a *short-circuit through the H-bridge* in some driver topologies. Always ensure IN1 ≠ IN2 for active motion.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear velocity of wheel | $v = \omega \times r$ | m/s | Depends on motor RPM |
| 2 | Angular velocity (turn rate) | $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L}$ | rad/s | Zero for straight motion |
| 3 | Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) radius | $R = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$ | m | Infinity when $v_R = v_L$ (straight) |
| 4 | Time to turn angle $\theta$ (pivot turn) | $t_{turn} = \dfrac{\theta}{\omega} = \dfrac{\theta \cdot L}{2 \cdot r \cdot v_{wheel}}$ | s | For 90° at 0.2 m/s, L=0.15 m: t ≈ 0.33 s |
| 5 | Time to travel distance $d$ | $t_{run} = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{d}{r \cdot \omega_{wheel}}$ | s | For 1 m at 0.2 m/s: t = 5 s |
| 6 | Encoder counts for distance $d$ | $N = \dfrac{d}{2 \pi r} \times CPR$ | counts | Depends on encoder resolution |
| 7 | Motor RPM → Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi \times RPM}{60}$ | rad/s | For 200 RPM: ω ≈ 20.94 rad/s |
| 8 | PWM duty cycle → Speed scaling | $v_{actual} = v_{max} \times \dfrac{PWM}{255}$ | m/s | Linear approximation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Question 2 of Part A (3 marks) almost always tests the ICR radius formula or the time-to-turn calculation. Memorize the differential drive equations in **both directions** of inference (given time → find angle, and given angle → find time).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Amazon warehouse robots use identical differential drive kinematics for shelf transport.
- **AGRICULTURE:** Precision agriculture robots (e.g., RowBot) navigate crop rows using turn-angle calculations from this same mathematical foundation.
- **DOMESTIC:** Robot vacuum cleaners (Roomba) execute "edge-follow + 90° turn" routines that are direct descendants of this lab exercise.
- **AUTOMOTIVE VALIDATION:** Self-driving car parking maneuvers are decomposed into sequences of forward motion and pivot turns — the very building blocks of this experiment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Differential Drive Turn Radius

Consider a two-wheeled mobile robot with wheels separated by distance **L**. Let the left wheel have linear velocity **v_L** and the right wheel have linear velocity **v_R**, where **v_R > v_L** (causing a left turn).

**Step 1:** Because the robot is a rigid body, both wheels must travel along concentric circular arcs centered at the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**.

**Step 2:** The radius from the ICR to the right wheel is **R + L/2**, and to the left wheel is **R - L/2**.

**Step 3:** Both wheels share the same angular velocity $\omega$ about the ICR:

$$\omega = \dfrac{v_R}{R + L/2} = \dfrac{v_L}{R - L/2}$$

**Step 4:** Cross-multiply the two equalities:

$$v_R (R - L/2) = v_L (R + L/2)$$

**Step 5:** Expand the products:

$$v_R \cdot R - v_R \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = v_L \cdot R + v_L \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Collect R terms on the left side:

$$R (v_R - v_L) = \dfrac{L}{2} (v_R + v_L)$$

**Step 7:** Solve for R:

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}}$$

This is the **fundamental differential drive curvature equation**, derivable in a 7-mark KTU exam question.

## 3.2 Derivation of Time Required for a 90° Pivot Turn

A **pivot turn** is the special case where the robot rotates about its own center — i.e., **R = 0**. From Step 7 above, this requires $v_R = -v_L$ (equal magnitude, opposite direction).

**Step 1:** Set $v_R = +v$ and $v_L = -v$ (right wheel forward, left wheel backward for a right pivot turn).

**Step 2:** Substitute into the angular velocity formula:

$$\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \dfrac{v - (-v)}{L} = \dfrac{2v}{L}$$

**Step 3:** The angle to turn is $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ rad (for 90°).

**Step 4:** Time for constant angular velocity:

$$\boxed{t_{pivot} = \dfrac{\theta}{\omega} = \dfrac{\pi/2}{2v/L} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot L}{4v}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Lab Standard):**
For L = 0.15 m and v = 0.2 m/s:

$$t_{pivot} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.15}{4 \times 0.2} = \dfrac{0.4712}{0.8} \approx 0.589 \text{ seconds}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Motion Primitives to Actuator Actions

| Motion Command | Left Motor Direction | Right Motor Direction | Left PWM | Right PWM | Duration Formula |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Forward | Forward | Forward | $PWM$ | $PWM$ | $t = d / v$ |
| Backward | Backward | Backward | $PWM$ | $PWM$ | $t = d / v$ |
| Turn Left (pivot) | Backward | Forward | $PWM$ | $PWM$ | $t = \pi L / (4v)$ |
| Turn Right (pivot) | Forward | Backward | $PWM$ | $PWM$ | $t = \pi L / (4v)$ |
| Curve Left (smooth) | Forward | Forward (faster) | $0.5 \cdot PWM$ | $PWM$ | $t = \theta \cdot R / v_{avg}$ |
| Stop | Coast / Brake | Coast / Brake | 0 | 0 | Immediate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Note:** The examiner checks if you explicitly state *which wheel goes which direction* during turning. A vague "turn left command" without wheel-level detail loses **2 marks** out of 7 in a typical sub-question.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Programmatic Calculation (Pseudocode Logic)

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 5$ cm, wheelbase $L = 20$ cm. The motor runs at 150 RPM after gearing. The robot must travel forward 2 meters, turn right 90°, then travel forward 1 meter. Calculate the required PWM timing (assuming PWM=255 gives full speed).

**Step 1:** Convert RPM to linear velocity.

$$\omega_{wheel} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 150}{60} = 5\pi \approx 15.708 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Linear wheel velocity (this is the robot's max forward speed).

$$v_{max} = \omega_{wheel} \times r = 15.708 \times 0.05 = 0.7854 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Time for forward 2 m.

$$t_1 = \dfrac{2}{0.7854} \approx 2.546 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4:** Time for 90° pivot turn (using derived formula with v_L = -v_R).

$$t_2 = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.20}{4 \times 0.7854} = \dfrac{0.6283}{3.1416} \approx 0.200 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 5:** Time for forward 1 m.

$$t_3 = \dfrac{1}{0.7854} \approx 1.273 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 6:** Total mission time.

$$T_{total} = 2.546 + 0.200 + 1.273 = 4.019 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Notice the pivot turn takes only ~5% of the total mission time. In contrast, a smooth arc turn (e.g., R = 0.5 m) would take nearly 2 seconds for 90° — a 10× increase. This is why **pivot turns dominate KTU lab programs**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Program Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Program] --> B[Initialize GPIO Pins and PWM]
    B --> C[Define Constants: r, L, RPM]
    C --> D[Set Encoders to Zero]
    D --> E[Call moveForward 1m]
    E --> F[Stop Motors, Delay 500ms]
    F --> G[Call turnRight 90 deg]
    G --> H[Stop Motors, Delay 500ms]
    H --> I[Call moveForward 1m]
    I --> J[Stop Motors]
    J --> K[END Program - Idle Loop]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style K fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style E fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style G fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style I fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Robot Motion Control — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HOST[Host PC / User Command] --> COMPILER[IDE Upload Tool]
    COMPILER --> MCU[Microcontroller Unit]
    MCU --> DRIVER[H-Bridge Motor Driver L298N]
    DRIVER --> M1[Left DC Motor]
    DRIVER --> M2[Right DC Motor]
    M1 --> W1[Left Wheel with Encoder]
    M2 --> W2[Right Wheel with Encoder]
    W1 --> FB1[Encoder Feedback Signal]
    W2 --> FB2[Encoder Feedback Signal]
    FB1 --> MCU
    FB2 --> MCU

    subgraph CONTROL_LOOP[Closed Loop Control]
        MCU
        FB1
        FB2
    end
```

## 4.3 State Machine for Motion Primitives

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> FORWARD: command MOVE FORWARD
    IDLE --> BACKWARD: command MOVE BACKWARD
    IDLE --> TURN_LEFT: command TURN LEFT
    IDLE --> TURN_RIGHT: command TURN RIGHT
    FORWARD --> IDLE: duration elapsed
    BACKWARD --> IDLE: duration elapsed
    TURN_LEFT --> IDLE: angle reached
    TURN_RIGHT --> IDLE: angle reached
    IDLE --> [*]: shutdown
```

## 4.4 Kinematic Geometry of a Pivot Turn

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE[Before Turn]
        B1[Robot facing North]
    end
    subgraph DURING[During Pivot Turn - Both Wheels Spin]
        D1[Left wheel REVERSE]
        D2[Right wheel FORWARD]
        D3[ICR is at robot center]
    end
    subgraph AFTER[After 90 Degree Right Turn]
        A1[Robot now facing East]
    end
    BEFORE --> DURING --> AFTER

    style BEFORE fill:#e6f2ff
    style DURING fill:#ffe6e6
    style AFTER fill:#e6ffe6
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Read This Diagram for Your Exam:** When asked to "explain the robot motion," draw a top-view schematic showing wheel directions with arrows (← and →) and label the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR). This single act of diagrammatic explanation earns **3 marks** under the "Diagram" criterion in KTU rubrics.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, PCPEL607]**
**Define a "motion primitive" in the context of robot programming. List any four motion primitives used in a typical differential-drive robot.**

**Model Answer:**
A **motion primitive** is a pre-defined, parameterized, atomic motion command that can be composed with other primitives to form complex robot trajectories. Each primitive encapsulates the actuator-level instructions (wheel directions, PWM values, timing) required to execute one elemental motion.

Four standard motion primitives are:

1. **Move Forward(d, v)** — translates the robot by distance `d` at speed `v`.
2. **Move Backward(d, v)** — translates the robot in reverse by distance `d`.
3. **Turn Left(θ)** — rotates the robot counter-clockwise by angle `θ` (pivot or arc).
4. **Turn Right(θ)** — rotates the robot clockwise by angle `θ`.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCPEL607]**
**What is the role of an H-Bridge in a robot motion control program? Why is a single GPIO pin insufficient to control a DC motor's direction?**

**Model Answer:**
An **H-Bridge** is an electronic circuit (commonly the L298N IC) that uses four transistor switches arranged in an "H" topology to control the polarity of voltage applied to a DC motor. By selectively activating diagonal pairs of switches, the H-Bridge can reverse current flow, thus reversing the motor's rotation direction.

A **single GPIO pin** is insufficient because:
1. A GPIO pin outputs only a binary HIGH (+5V) or LOW (0V) — it cannot produce a *negative voltage* required to reverse the motor.
2. Direction reversal requires swapping the polarity of the two motor terminals simultaneously — this needs **two coordinated output pins** (IN1, IN2) per motor.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, PCPEL607]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a closed-loop robot motion control system. Identify the role of the microcontroller, motor driver, and encoder in this loop. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 4$ cm and wheelbase $L = 16$ cm. The motor driver receives a PWM signal of 180 out of 255, corresponding to a wheel angular velocity of 12 rad/s.
- **(i)** Calculate the robot's linear velocity. **(2 Marks)**
- **(ii)** Determine the time required to travel a straight-line distance of 1.5 meters. **(2 Marks)**
- **(iii)** Calculate the time required for a 90° pivot turn to the right. **(3 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Solution:**

The closed-loop robot motion control architecture consists of the following sequential blocks:

1. **Command Source (Host PC / Pre-programmed Logic):** Provides high-level motion intent ("go forward 1 m").
2. **Microcontroller Unit (MCU):** Translates the high-level command into kinematic calculations and generates PWM + direction signals. *Example: Arduino UNO, ESP32.*
3. **Motor Driver (H-Bridge, e.g., L298N):** Receives low-current logic signals from the MCU and amplifies them to high-current drive for the motors.
4. **DC Motors + Wheels:** Convert electrical energy to mechanical rotation.
5. **Encoders (Optical / Magnetic):** Mounted on the wheel shaft to measure actual rotation and feed it back to the MCU.
6. **Feedback Path:** The MCU compares *commanded* vs *actual* motion (via encoder counts) and corrects the PWM in real-time, forming a **closed PID-like loop**.

> This architecture ensures the robot achieves the *intended* motion despite real-world disturbances (wheel slip, battery voltage drop).

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**(i) Linear Velocity Calculation:**

$$v = \omega \times r = 12 \times 0.04 = 0.48 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Time for 1.5 m Straight Motion:**

$$t_1 = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{1.5}{0.48} = 3.125 \text{ seconds}$$

**(iii) Time for 90° Pivot Turn:**

Convert the desired angle to radians:

$$\theta = 90° = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad}$$

For a pivot turn, the angular velocity of the robot is:

$$\omega_{robot} = \dfrac{2v}{L} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.48}{0.16} = 6.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

Time required:

$$t_2 = \dfrac{\theta}{\omega_{robot}} = \dfrac{\pi/2}{6.0} = \dfrac{1.5708}{6.0} \approx 0.2618 \text{ seconds}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, PCPEL607]**

**(a)** Write a structured pseudocode program to execute the following motion sequence on a differential-drive robot: move forward 1 meter → turn right 90° → move forward 1 meter → stop. Clearly define all motion primitives as functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During lab demonstration, a student's robot overshoots its 90° turn by 10°. List **three probable causes** and **one corrective software modification** for each cause. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Pseudocode Solution:**

```text
PROGRAM Robot_Motion_Demo
    // ============ CONSTANTS ============
    CONST WHEEL_RADIUS  = 0.05     // meters
    CONST WHEELBASE     = 0.15     // meters
    CONST MAX_PWM       = 255
    CONST TEST_SPEED    = 150      // PWM value (out of 255)
    
    // ============ GPIO PIN MAP ============
    // Left Motor:  IN1 -> Pin 2, IN2 -> Pin 3, ENA -> Pin 9 (PWM)
    // Right Motor: IN3 -> Pin 4, IN4 -> Pin 5, ENB -> Pin 10 (PWM)
    
    // ============ MOTION PRIMITIVES ============
    
    FUNCTION moveForward(distance, speed):
        time = distance / (speed/MAX_PWM * MAX_LINEAR_VEL)
        SET IN1=HIGH, IN2=LOW      // Left motor forward
        SET IN3=HIGH, IN4=LOW      // Right motor forward
        WRITE PWM ENA = speed
        WRITE PWM ENB = speed
        DELAY time * 1000          // milliseconds
        stopMotors()
    END FUNCTION
    
    FUNCTION moveBackward(distance, speed):
        // [Similar structure with IN1/IN2 swapped]
    END FUNCTION
    
    FUNCTION turnRight(angle):
        time = (PI/2) * WHEELBASE / (4 * speed/MAX_PWM * MAX_LINEAR_VEL)
        SET IN1=LOW,  IN2=HIGH     // Left motor reverse
        SET IN3=HIGH, IN4=LOW      // Right motor forward
        WRITE PWM ENA = speed
        WRITE PWM ENB = speed
        DELAY time * 1000
        stopMotors()
    END FUNCTION
    
    FUNCTION turnLeft(angle):
        // [Opposite wheel directions from turnRight]
    END FUNCTION
    
    FUNCTION stopMotors():
        WRITE PWM ENA = 0
        WRITE PWM ENB = 0
        DELAY 100                  // 100 ms stabilization
    END FUNCTION
    
    // ============ MAIN SEQUENCE ============
BEGIN
    SETUP all pins as OUTPUT
    SETUP PWM frequency to 490 Hz
    
    CALL moveForward(1.0, TEST_SPEED)
    DELAY 500                     // Pause between actions
    
    CALL turnRight(90)
    DELAY 500
    
    CALL moveForward(1.0, TEST_SPEED)
    
    CALL stopMotors()
    
    LOOP FOREVER:
        // Idle state
    END LOOP
END
```

---

**Part (b) — Probable Causes and Corrective Software Fixes:**

| # | Probable Cause | Software Correction |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Wheel slippage** — wheels skid during the high-torque pivot turn, causing rotation beyond the commanded angle. | Reduce PWM speed during turns (e.g., use 60% PWM for turns instead of full speed). Add a 50 ms coast before braking. |
| 2 | **Inaccurate kinematic constants** — the programmed `WHEELBASE` or wheel radius does not match the actual robot dimensions. | Recalibrate constants by running a 10-trial test: measure actual 90° time, then update `t_pivot` constant with correction factor (e.g., `t_actual = 0.9 × t_theoretical`). |
| 3 | **No feedback / open-loop control** — the program blindly trusts the timing without verifying actual rotation. | Integrate encoder-based angle measurement: compute angle from left/right encoder counts and stop when target angle is reached. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to STOP motors between primitives:** A robot that doesn't call `stopMotors()` between commands drifts due to momentum — **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Using `delay()` inside the main loop without `stopMotors()`:** Causes chained motion overlap (e.g., turn starts before forward motion fully stops).
> 3. **Hard-coding values without constants:** Examiners deduct marks for *magic numbers* like `delay(589)` instead of `delay(computePivotTime())`. Use named constants — **1-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Skipping the kinematics derivation:** If asked to compute timing, students often write the code without showing the underlying math. Always **derive then code** — show the formula AND the numerical substitution.
> 5. **Confusing LEFT vs RIGHT wheel direction during turns:** A wrong polarity assignment causes the robot to turn in the opposite direction — a critical functional failure worth **3 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- ✅ **Definition:** A robot motion program is a sequence of motion primitives that translate high-level commands into wheel-level actuator signals.
- ✅ **Motion Primitives (must know all 4):** `moveForward`, `moveBackward`, `turnLeft`, `turnRight`, plus `stopMotors`.
- ✅ **H-Bridge Logic:** IN1=HIGH, IN2=LOW → Forward; IN1=LOW, IN2=HIGH → Backward.
- ✅ **Differential Drive ICR Formula:** $R = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$ (memorize).
- ✅ **Pivot Turn Condition:** $v_R = -v_L$ (equal and opposite wheel velocities).
- ✅ **Pivot Turn Time:** $t_{pivot} = \dfrac{\pi L}{4v}$ for 90°.
- ✅ **Forward Motion Time:** $t_{run} = d / v = d / (\omega \times r)$.
- ✅ **Motor RPM → Linear Velocity:** $v = \dfrac{2\pi \times RPM \times r}{60}$.
- ✅ **Standard KTU Test Parameters:** 90° turn (±5° tolerance), 1 m straight (±5 cm tolerance), 500 ms pause between primitives.
- ✅ **Code Structure Mandate:** INIT → PRIMITIVES → MAIN SEQUENCE → STOP → IDLE LOOP.
- ✅ **Always include a `stopMotors()` call** between every motion primitive to prevent drift.
- ✅ **Use named constants** for `WHEEL_RADIUS`, `WHEELBASE`, `PWM_SPEED` — never magic numbers.
- ✅ **Show derivation before code** in numerical sub-questions — earns full marks.
- ✅ **Mention feedback loop** (encoders + PID) if asked about "advanced" or "robust" control — earns bonus marks.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** "What if the robot overshoots?" → Answer: reduce PWM for turning / add encoder feedback / recalibrate wheelbase constant.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Wisdom:** In the 14-mark question, examiners allocate roughly **4 marks for theory (kinematics + diagram)**, **6 marks for numerical calculation**, and **4 marks for code quality + explanation**. A balanced answer covering all three dimensions is the only path to full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development of an obstacle avoidance robot using servo motors, ultrasonic and touch sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Development of an obstacle avoidance robot using servo motors, ultrasonic and touch sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Obstacle Avoidance Robot** is an autonomous, self-navigating electromechanical system designed to perceive physical obstructions in its trajectory through onboard sensor fusion, execute real-time path-planning decisions, and actuate its locomotion mechanism to circumvent the detected obstacle without human intervention. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this experiment integrates three principal hardware subsystems: an **HC-SR04 ultrasonic transducer** (non-contact ranging), a **micro-servo motor (SG90/MG90S)** (angular actuation for sensor scanning), and a **mechanical/contact touch sensor** (physical bump detection for fail-safe operation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPEL607):** This experiment evaluates Course Outcomes **CO2** (Design and implement sensor-based control algorithms) and **CO3** (Demonstrate actuation and motion control using PWM-driven devices). The robot must demonstrate autonomous decision-making using multi-modal sensor feedback.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "How a Blind Person Walks"

Imagine you are walking through a dark, unfamiliar room wearing a blindfold:

- Your **walking stick** corresponds to the **ultrasonic sensor** — it probes the space ahead and tells you "something is 50 cm in front of you" *before* you actually reach it.
- Your **neck (turning head side to side)** corresponds to the **servo motor** — instead of a single forward gaze, the sensor physically sweeps left (≈0°), center (≈90°), and right (≈180°) to build a 3-point situational map of the environment.
- Your **toe or shin** corresponds to the **touch sensor** — the *last line of defense*. If the walking stick fails or misjudges a transparent/soft obstacle, the physical impact triggers an immediate reverse-and-redirect maneuver.

This layered architecture — *proactive perception (ultrasonic) → directional scanning (servo) → reactive contact (touch)* — embodies the **Hierarchical Sensor Fusion** principle taught in KTU's automation curriculum.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following reference values are mandatory for all distance and timing calculations:

- **Speed of sound in air at 20°C:** $v = 343 \text{ m/s}$ (industry-standard value, $0.0343 \text{ cm/µs}$)
- **Servo PWM pulse-width boundaries:** $t_{\text{low}} = 1.0 \text{ ms}$ (0°), $t_{\text{mid}} = 1.5 \text{ ms}$ (90°), $t_{\text{high}} = 2.0 \text{ ms}$ (180°)
- **PWM refresh rate:** $f_{\text{PWM}} = 50 \text{ Hz}$ (period $T = 20 \text{ ms}$)
- **HC-SR04 effective range:** $2 \text{ cm} \leq d \leq 400 \text{ cm}$
- **HC-SR04 trigger pulse width:** $\geq 10 \text{ µs}$ (TTL-level, 5 V)

## 1.4 Block-Level Component Inventory

| Subsystem | Component | Role | Interface Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Controller | Arduino Uno (ATmega328P) | Master decision unit | Digital I/O, PWM |
| Range Sensor | HC-SR04 Ultrasonic | Non-contact distance | Trigger (OUT), Echo (IN) |
| Angular Actuator | SG90 Servo Motor | Sensor panning | PWM (D9/D10) |
| Contact Sensor | Tactile Push Button / Bumper | Fail-safe trigger | Digital Input |
| Locomotion | L298N + 2× DC Geared Motors | Differential drive | PWM + Direction |
| Power | 7.4 V Li-ion / 9 V battery | Energy source | DC |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Layered Sensor Fusion

The robot operates on a **three-tier decision hierarchy** that must be analyzed step-by-step:

### Tier 1 — Proactive Range Mapping (Ultrasonic)
The HC-SR04 operates on the **time-of-flight (ToF)** principle. It emits a 40 kHz burst when the TRIG pin is held HIGH for ≥10 µs, then sets the ECHO pin HIGH for a duration $t_{\text{echo}}$ proportional to the round-trip time of the acoustic wave.

### Tier 2 — Angular Sweep (Servo)
A single forward-facing ultrasonic beam is insufficient to "see" the surroundings. The SG90 servo physically rotates the sensor mount, enabling the controller to capture a **panoramic distance profile** $D(\theta)$ across the angular field of view.

### Tier 3 — Reactive Bumper (Touch)
If an obstacle is acoustically transparent (e.g., a soft cushion, glass pane at oblique angle) and passes through the ultrasonic cone undetected, the mechanical bumper switch closes a digital input, forcing an emergency stop-and-reverse subroutine.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The distance formula and servo pulse-width linearization are the **two most frequently tested equations** in viva and lab records for this experiment.

$$
d = \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{servo}} = \left( \frac{\text{PulseWidth} - 1.0 \text{ ms}}{1.0 \text{ ms}} \right) \times 90°
$$

$$
T_{\text{period}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{PWM}}} = \frac{1}{50 \text{ Hz}} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\text{Resolution}_{\text{angular}} = \frac{\Delta\theta}{N_{\text{steps}} \cdot \Delta t_{\text{scan}}}
$$

### 2.2.1 Master Formula Table (Pipe-Safe)

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time-of-Flight Distance | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | $d$ = distance (m), $v$ = 343 m/s, $t$ = echo time (s) | Ultrasonic ranging |
| 2 | Pulse-Width to Angle | $\theta = 90 \cdot \dfrac{\tau - 1.0}{1.0} + 0°$ | $\tau$ = pulse width (ms), $\theta$ in degrees | Servo positioning |
| 3 | Servo PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{\tau}{T} \times 100\%$ | $\tau$ = pulse (ms), $T$ = 20 ms | PWM generation |
| 4 | Motor Linear Velocity | $v_{\text{robot}} = \omega \cdot r$ | $\omega$ = wheel angular speed, $r$ = wheel radius | Locomotion analysis |
| 5 | Differential Turn Radius | $R = \dfrac{v_{\text{R}} + v_{\text{L}}}{v_{\text{R}} - v_{\text{L}}} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}$ | $L$ = wheelbase, $v_R$, $v_L$ = wheel speeds | Maneuvering |
| 6 | Temperature Correction | $v_{T} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | $T$ = ambient temperature (°C) | Acoustic velocity |
| 7 | Minimum Safe Distance | $d_{\text{safe}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{robot}} \cdot t_{\text{react}}}{2}$ | $t_{\text{react}}$ = processing delay | Collision avoidance threshold |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The architectural pattern demonstrated in this lab is the **foundational building block** of:
- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** — Amazon Kiva robots use LiDAR but follow the same scan-decide-act paradigm.
- **Autonomous Vacuum Cleaners** (iRobot Roomba) — employ 12+ infrared and bumper sensors in similar hierarchical fashion.
- **Self-Driving Cars** — LiDAR, radar, and ultrasonic park-assist sensors all use ToF ranging fused with bumper-style fail-safes.
- **Industrial Robotic Arms** — collaborative robots (cobots) use capacitive and force-tactile sensors analogous to our touch switch.

## 2.4 Sensor Working — Technical Deep Dive

### 2.4.1 HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor
- **Transmitter:** Piezoelectric diaphragm resonating at 40 kHz (above human hearing).
- **Receiver:** Matched piezoelectric sensor tuned to 40 kHz.
- **Beam angle:** ≈15° conical.
- **Blind zone:** Objects closer than 2 cm return ambiguous echoes due to ringing decay.

### 2.4.2 SG90 Servo Motor
- Internally contains a **DC motor, gear-train (plastic), potentiometer, and control PCB**.
- Uses **closed-loop position control** — the potentiometer feeds back the shaft angle to an internal comparator.
- Operates on a **fixed-frequency PWM (50 Hz)** where ONLY the duty cycle carries positional information.

### 2.4.3 Touch Sensor Module
- Contains a **tactile switch (SPST momentary)** with a debounce capacitor and comparator IC.
- Logic output: **HIGH when pressed, LOW when released** (active-LOW variant also common).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ultrasonic Distance from Echo Time

**Problem Statement:** The HC-SR04 returns an echo pulse of width $t_{\text{echo}} = 1470 \text{ µs}$ at 20°C room temperature. Compute the obstacle distance.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing physics.**

The ultrasonic burst travels from the transmitter to the obstacle, reflects, and returns to the receiver. Hence the measured time $t_{\text{echo}}$ represents a **round trip**, while the desired distance $d$ is the **one-way** path length. This necessitates division by 2.

**Step 2 — State the fundamental relationship.**

The distance covered by the acoustic wave is:

$$
\text{Path}_{\text{total}} = v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{echo}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the one-way correction.**

Since the wave traverses the distance twice (forward + return):

$$
d = \frac{\text{Path}_{\text{total}}}{2} = \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the known values.**

We use $v_{\text{sound}} = 343 \text{ m/s}$ and $t_{\text{echo}} = 1470 \text{ µs} = 1470 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$:

$$
d = \frac{343 \text{ m/s} \cdot 1470 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
343 \cdot 1470 \times 10^{-6} = 504{,}210 \times 10^{-6} = 0.50421 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the division.**

$$
d = \frac{0.50421}{2} = 0.25210 \text{ m} \approx 25.2 \text{ cm}
$$

**Result:** The obstacle is **25.2 cm** in front of the robot. The Arduino's `pulseIn()` function returns time in microseconds, so the working formula coded in the firmware is:

$$
d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{t_{\text{echo,µs}}}{58.2}
$$

This simplified form arises because:

$$
\frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.0343 \text{ cm/µs} = 0.01715 \text{ cm/µs} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{0.01715} \approx 58.2
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Servo Pulse Width to Angular Position

**Problem Statement:** Compute the required PWM pulse width to orient the ultrasonic sensor at 45° (left of center).

**Step 1 — Establish the linear pulse-to-angle mapping.**

Manufacturers specify two calibration points:
- At 0° → pulse width = 1.0 ms
- At 180° → pulse width = 2.0 ms

The relationship is **linear** (verified by the internal potentiometer feedback law).

**Step 2 — Compute the slope.**

$$
m = \frac{\theta_{180} - \theta_{0}}{\tau_{180} - \tau_{0}} = \frac{180° - 0°}{2.0 \text{ ms} - 1.0 \text{ ms}} = \frac{180°}{1.0 \text{ ms}} = 180°/\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Derive the generic equation.**

Starting from the 0° reference point and using point-slope form:

$$
\theta - 0° = 180°/\text{ms} \cdot (\tau - 1.0 \text{ ms})
$$

$$
\theta = 180 \cdot (\tau - 1.0)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\tau$ at $\theta = 45°$.**

$$
45 = 180 \cdot (\tau - 1.0)
$$

$$
\tau - 1.0 = \frac{45}{180} = 0.25
$$

$$
\tau = 1.25 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Express as duty cycle of the 20 ms PWM period.**

$$
D = \frac{1.25 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} \times 100\% = 6.25\%
$$

For Arduino's `Servo.h` library, the API expects **angle in degrees directly** (`servo.write(45)`), internally generating the 1.25 ms pulse. The intermediate pulse-width calculation is essential for viva questions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Safe Obstacle Threshold

**Problem Statement:** The robot moves at $v_{\text{robot}} = 0.3 \text{ m/s}$ and the control loop has a reaction latency of $t_{\text{react}} = 80 \text{ ms}$. Compute the minimum forward clearance required to guarantee safe stop.

**Step 1 — Identify the stopping dynamics.**

Upon detecting an obstacle, the robot must (a) complete the current instruction cycle, (b) command the motors to stop, and (c) the motors must physically decelerate. The worst-case overshoot distance is:

$$
d_{\text{overshoot}} = v_{\text{robot}} \cdot t_{\text{react}}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert the values.**

$$
d_{\text{overshoot}} = 0.3 \text{ m/s} \cdot 0.080 \text{ s} = 0.024 \text{ m} = 2.4 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 3 — Add a 50% engineering safety margin (KTU standard practice).**

$$
d_{\text{safe}} = 1.5 \cdot d_{\text{overshoot}} = 1.5 \cdot 2.4 = 3.6 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 4 — Set the trigger threshold in firmware.**

The conditional statement becomes:

$$
\text{if } (d_{\text{measured}} \leq 15 \text{ cm}) \text{ then EXECUTE AVOIDANCE}
$$

The 15 cm threshold (greater than the 3.6 cm minimum) provides a comfortable margin for varying floor friction and battery voltage sag.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic Decomposition

| Layer | Sensor Trigger | Control State | Action Executed | Termination Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L0 Idle | No obstacle | $d > 15$ cm | Move forward (PWM 200/255) | Obstacle detected |
| L1 Detect | Ultrasonic $d \leq 15$ cm | STOP, scan 0°→180° | Sweep servo + record $D(\theta)$ | Scan complete |
| L2 Choose | Compare $D_L$, $D_C$, $D_R$ | Compute max direction | Steer toward max-distance side | Re-orient complete |
| L3 Maneuver | Direction chosen | Differential drive turn | Left/right wheel speed asymmetry | Turn angle reached |
| L4 Resume | Path clear | Forward motion | Drive forward | Loop to L0 |
| L5 Emergency | Touch switch ACTIVE | Hard stop + reverse | Halt, reverse 10 cm, retry L1 | All clear |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete signal flow from physical-world stimuli to motor actuation, showing the closed-loop perception-decision-action chain that defines the experiment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Environment Obstacle] -->|Acoustic Echo| B[HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor]
    C[Physical Contact] -->|Mechanical Force| D[Touch Bumper Switch]
    B -->|Echo Pulse - Digital| E[Arduino Uno - ATmega328P]
    D -->|Digital HIGH or LOW| E
    E -->|PWM 50 Hz Duty Cycle| F[SG90 Servo Motor]
    F -->|Sweeps 0 to 180 deg| B
    E -->|PWM H-Bridge Signals| G[L298N Motor Driver]
    G -->|DC Voltage - 7.4V| H[Left DC Motor]
    G -->|DC Voltage - 7.4V| I[Right DC Motor]
    H --> J[Robot Differential Drive Chassis]
    I --> J
    J -->|Displacement| A
    K[7.4V Li-ion Battery] -->|Power Rail| G
    K -->|Power Rail| E
    E -.->|Serial Monitor 9600 baud| L[PC Debug Terminal]
```

## 4.2 Algorithm Flowchart — Autonomous Avoidance State Machine

The Mermaid state diagram below formalizes the decision logic derived in Section 3.4. This is the **canonical reference answer** expected by KTU examiners when students are asked to "draw the obstacle avoidance flowchart" in lab records and viva.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Power ON - Initialize]) --> Init[Set Servo to 90 deg center position]
    Init --> Loop{Read Touch Sensor}
    Loop -->|TOUCHED| Emergency[STOP Motors Immediately]
    Emergency --> Reverse[Reverse 10 cm at PWM 150]
    Reverse --> Retract[Servo to 0 deg scan left]
    Retract --> Loop
    Loop -->|NOT TOUCHED| ReadUS[Trigger Ultrasonic - Read Echo]
    ReadUS --> Calc[Compute Distance d in cm]
    Calc --> Check{d greater than 15 cm}
    Check -->|YES - Clear Path| Forward[Drive Forward - Both Motors PWM 200]
    Forward --> Loop
    Check -->|NO - Obstacle Found| Stop1[STOP Both Motors]
    Stop1 --> ServoL[Servo to 0 deg - Measure dL]
    ServoL --> ServoR[Servo to 180 deg - Measure dR]
    ServoR --> Compare{dL greater than dR}
    Compare -->|YES - Turn Left| TurnL[Left Motor Reverse, Right Motor Forward - 300 ms]
    Compare -->|NO - Turn Right| TurnR[Right Motor Reverse, Left Motor Forward - 300 ms]
    TurnL --> Center[Servo to 90 deg]
    TurnR --> Center
    Center --> Loop
```

## 4.3 Hardware Wiring Schematic (Pin-Map Table)

| Component | Component Pin | Arduino Uno Pin | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| HC-SR04 | VCC | 5V | Power |
| HC-SR04 | GND | GND | Ground |
| HC-SR04 | TRIG | D7 | OUTPUT |
| HC-SR04 | ECHO | D6 | INPUT |
| SG90 Servo | Red (VCC) | 5V | Power |
| SG90 Servo | Brown (GND) | GND | Ground |
| SG90 Servo | Orange (Signal) | D9 | PWM OUTPUT |
| Touch Sensor | VCC | 5V | Power |
| Touch Sensor | GND | GND | Ground |
| Touch Sensor | SIG | D2 | INPUT (Interrupt) |
| L298N | IN1 / IN2 | D8 / D9 | Motor A Direction |
