| L298N | IN3 / IN4 | D10 / D11 | Motor B Direction |
| L298N | ENA / ENB | D5 / D6 | PWM Speed Control |
| L298N | 12V / GND | Battery + / − | Power Input |

## 4.4 Timing Diagram — Echo Pulse Behavior

```
   TRIG  ____|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|____________
                       
   ECHO  ________________|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|____
                        |<-- t_echo -->|
                        |               |
   t = 0              10 µs           t_end
   (Start)          (Trigger)    (Echo received)

   Obstacle at 25 cm  →  t_echo ≈ 1470 µs
   Obstacle at 100 cm →  t_echo ≈ 5828 µs
```

The **echo pulse width is the sole measurement variable** from which the firmware derives the entire distance metric.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] — Define the time-of-flight principle used in the HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor and write the distance formula.**

**Model Answer:**
The **time-of-flight (ToF)** principle measures distance by emitting an ultrasonic burst (40 kHz) and timing how long the reflected echo takes to return to the receiver. The total path is twice the obstacle distance, so the formula is:

$$
d = \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot t_{\text{echo}}}{2}
$$

where $v_{\text{sound}} = 343 \text{ m/s}$ at 20°C. The factor of 2 accounts for the round-trip propagation. (3 marks: definition 1, formula 1, variable explanation 1)

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] — Why is a servo motor used in conjunction with the ultrasonic sensor instead of mounting three fixed sensors?**

**Model Answer:**
A single ultrasonic sensor has a narrow **15° conical beam**, giving a limited field of view. Mounting three fixed sensors would increase cost, weight, and wiring complexity. A **servo motor** (SG90) sweeps the single sensor across a **180° arc** through PWM-driven angular positioning, creating a **virtual multi-sensor array** with software-side flexibility — angles can be reprogrammed instantly, and fewer components mean lower power consumption and higher reliability. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** — With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the obstacle avoidance robot developed in your lab. List all sensors and actuators with their functions.

**Model Solution:**

The obstacle avoidance robot is built around an **Arduino Uno** controller that integrates three sensor modalities and two actuation systems. The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1) shows the closed-loop signal flow.

**Sensors:**
1. **HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor (3 marks)** — emits 40 kHz pulses via the TRIG pin and measures the returning echo on the ECHO pin. The pulse width $t_{\text{echo}}$ is converted to distance $d$ using $d = v \cdot t / 2$. Used for **proactive non-contact obstacle detection** within 2–400 cm range.
2. **Touch / Bumper Sensor (2 marks)** — a SPST mechanical switch with debounce circuit. Logic HIGH indicates physical contact. Serves as a **fail-safe** for transparent or soft obstacles missed by the ultrasonic beam.

**Actuators:**
3. **SG90 Servo Motor (2 marks)** — rotates the ultrasonic sensor mount across 0°–180° at 50 Hz PWM, enabling panoramic scanning $D(\theta)$.
4. **L298N-driven DC Geared Motors (2 marks)** — differential-drive left/right wheels executing forward, reverse, and turning maneuvers.

The Arduino loops continuously: reads touch sensor → if pressed, emergency reverse; else, read ultrasonic → if $d \leq 15$ cm, scan left/right → choose open direction → turn and resume forward motion.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** — Derive the relationship between servo PWM pulse width and angular position. Compute the duty cycle for 135°.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calibration Points (2 marks)**

The SG90 datasheet specifies:
- 0° → pulse width $\tau_0 = 1.0$ ms
- 180° → pulse width $\tau_{180} = 2.0$ ms

**Step 2 — Linear Mapping (3 marks)**

Assuming a linear relationship $\theta = m \cdot \tau + c$, applying both points:

$$
m = \frac{180° - 0°}{2.0 - 1.0} = 180°/\text{ms}, \quad c = -180°
$$

Hence:

$$
\theta = 180 \cdot (\tau - 1.0)
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Solution for 135° (2 marks)**

$$
135 = 180 \cdot (\tau - 1.0) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau - 1.0 = 0.75
$$

$$
\tau = 1.75 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Duty Cycle Computation**

$$
D = \frac{1.75 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} \times 100\% = 8.75\%
$$

**Final Answer:** The pulse width is **1.75 ms**, corresponding to a **8.75% duty cycle** at 50 Hz.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** — Explain the role of sensor fusion in the obstacle avoidance robot. Differentiate between proactive and reactive obstacle detection methods used in this experiment.

**Model Solution:**

**Sensor Fusion Definition (2 marks):**
Sensor fusion is the integration of data from multiple heterogeneous sensors to produce a more accurate, reliable, and comprehensive understanding of the environment than any single sensor can provide alone.

**Proactive vs. Reactive — Comparative Analysis (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Proactive (Ultrasonic) | Reactive (Touch Bumper) |
|---|---|---|
| Detection Range | 2 – 400 cm | 0 cm (contact only) |
| Principle | Time-of-flight acoustic | Mechanical switch closure |
| Response Time | ~30 ms (one measurement) | < 5 ms (instantaneous) |
| Object Material | Reflects sound (hard, dense) | All materials (physical impact) |
| Power Consumption | ~15 mA active | ~5 mA |
| Failure Mode | Transparent/angled surfaces | Requires actual collision |

**Why Fusion is Mandatory (bonus, 1 mark):**
Ultrasonic sensors can fail to detect soft fabric, oblique glass, or surfaces beyond 15° from the perpendicular normal. The touch sensor compensates by triggering emergency maneuvers on physical contact — a classic **complementary redundancy** architecture used in aerospace flight control systems.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** — The robot's echo pulse width is measured as $t_{\text{echo}} = 2914 \text{ µs}$. Compute (i) the obstacle distance, and (ii) the new pulse width if the obstacle moves 50 cm closer.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Distance Calculation (3 marks)**

Using $v = 343 \text{ m/s} = 0.0343 \text{ cm/µs}$:

$$
d = \frac{0.0343 \cdot 2914}{2} = \frac{99.95}{2} = 49.97 \text{ cm} \approx 50 \text{ cm}
$$

**Part (ii) — New Pulse Width at 0 cm (4 marks)**

After moving 50 cm closer, the new distance is $d_{\text{new}} = 0$ cm, which is the **blind zone** of HC-SR04 (< 2 cm). However, formally:

$$
t_{\text{new}} = \frac{2 \cdot d_{\text{new}}}{v} = \frac{2 \cdot 0}{0.0343} = 0 \text{ µs}
$$

Since this is below the 2 cm sensor floor, **the ultrasonic sensor will return an invalid reading**, and the **touch sensor takes over** as the active detection modality. This demonstrates why **layered sensor fusion is critical** — no single sensor covers all operational regimes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the distance formula (round-trip vs. one-way). Costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Using $v = 330$ m/s** instead of 343 m/s — both are accepted but you must **state the temperature assumption** (20°C). Examiners deduct marks if the assumption is missing.
> 3. **Pulse-width formula sign errors** — confusing $\theta = 180(\tau - 1.0)$ with $\theta = 180(2.0 - \tau)$. Both mathematically valid, but you must clearly state your reference (0° at 1 ms vs. 180° at 1 ms).
> 4. **Not drawing the block diagram** in part (a) of 7-mark questions — block diagram is **mandatory** and carries 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Missing the touch sensor explanation** — many students describe only the ultrasonic and ignore the bumper, losing 1–2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- The robot uses **layered (hierarchical) sensor fusion**: ultrasonic (proactive) + servo scan (directional) + touch (reactive).
- The **HC-SR04 distance formula** is $d = v \cdot t / 2$ with $v = 343$ m/s at 20°C; simplified firmware form is $d_{\text{cm}} = t_{\text{µs}} / 58.2$.
- The **SG90 servo** uses 50 Hz PWM (20 ms period) with pulse width ranging from **1.0 ms (0°) to 2.0 ms (180°)** — linear mapping.
- The **touch sensor** is a fail-safe for obstacles the ultrasonic cannot detect (transparent, soft, or below the 2 cm blind zone).
- The **minimum safe distance threshold** accounts for robot velocity and processing latency, typically set at 15 cm for a 0.3 m/s robot.
- The **algorithm** is a finite state machine: Forward → Detect → Scan → Choose Direction → Turn → Resume Forward.
- The **L298N motor driver** receives direction bits (HIGH/LOW) and PWM speed signals from Arduino digital/PWM pins.
- The **trigger pin** of HC-SR04 needs a **≥10 µs pulse** to initiate ranging; the **echo pin** goes HIGH for a duration equal to the round-trip time.
- For viva: remember to state the **ambient temperature** when using the speed of sound, and the **PWM period** when computing duty cycle.
- The **beam angle of HC-SR04** is 15° — justify why a single forward sensor is insufficient and a servo sweep is necessary.
- Differentiate clearly between **proactive** (predictive, no contact) and **reactive** (contact-triggered) sensing in your answer.
- Always include the **block diagram** for any 7-mark question on robot architecture.
- The servo library function `servo.write(angle)` accepts degrees directly — useful for viva: explain how Arduino maps this to the 1–2 ms pulse internally.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of conveyor automation system using PLC, SCADA and electrical drive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL607 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION AND ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design of conveyor automation system using PLC, SCADA and electrical drive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Conveyor Automation System** is an integrated electromechanical arrangement in which a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) supervises the actuation of an **electrical drive** (typically a 3-phase induction motor coupled to a VFD/AC drive or a DC servo drive) that propels a belt/roller conveyor, while a **SCADA** (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) layer provides real-time visualization, alarm management, and remote supervisory control through a Human-Machine Interface (HMI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** The lab experiment validates the student's ability to (i) design the I/O mapping of a conveyor, (ii) develop **ladder logic** for sequential control, (iii) parameterize a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD), and (iv) configure a SCADA tag database and HMI screens.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Think of an **airport baggage carousel**:

- The **belt** is the conveyor itself, moving suitcases from check-in to the aircraft.
- The **motors hidden underneath** are the electrical drive — they decide how fast the belt spins.
- The **PLC** is the unseen "brain" inside the control panel that decides "start when green light is on, stop if a bag jams, reverse to remove a stuck item."
- The **SCADA screen** at the supervisor's desk is the live CCTV-style display showing belt speed, motor current, and alarm count.

If you replace suitcases with water bottles in a bottling plant, you get the classic **lab conveyor experiment**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metrics** typically used in conveyor design:
> - Belt speed **v = 0.1 – 2.5 m/s**
> - Motor rating **P = 0.37 kW – 7.5 kW** (for lab-scale conveyors)
> - Roller diameter **D = 50 mm – 200 mm**
> - Throughput **Q = 10 – 200 items/min** (discrete) or **kg/s** (bulk)

## 1.3 Block-Level System View

A conveyor automation system is decomposed into four functional layers:

1. **Field Layer** — Sensors (Inductive proximity, Photoelectric, Limit switch) and Actuators (Motor, Solenoid, Indicator lamps).
2. **Control Layer** — PLC (CPU, Digital/Analog I/O, Communication port).
3. **Drive Layer** — VFD/Soft-starter → 3-φ IM (Induction Motor).
4. **Supervisory Layer** — SCADA HMI ↔ PLC via industrial protocol (Modbus RTU/TCP, Profibus, Ethernet/IP).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Decomposition of the Conveyor System

The conveyor system operation can be broken into five logical stages:

### Stage 1 — Sensing (Field Layer)
- **Proximity Sensor (PXS1)**: Detects an object arriving at the loading zone.
- **Proximity Sensor (PXS2)**: Detects the object at the unloading zone for counting.
- **Limit Switches (LS_FWD, LS_REV)**: Mechanical end-of-travel indication (used when reverse jog is implemented).
- **Emergency Stop (E-STOP)**: Hardwired safety input — must be **NC (Normally Closed)** in series with the contactor coil.
- **Thermal Overload (OL)**: Protects motor windings; PLC input for fault annunciation.

### Stage 2 — Decision (PLC Control Layer)
- The PLC executes a **ladder logic** program in **cyclic scan** mode:
  1. Read all inputs → **Input Image Table (IIT)**.
  2. Solve the logic network top-to-bottom, left-to-right.
  3. Update outputs → **Output Image Table (OIT)**.
  4. Perform housekeeping (timer update, comm. housekeeping).
  5. Repeat. Typical scan time **t_scan = 5 – 20 ms** for small PLCs.

### Stage 3 — Actuation (Drive Layer)
- The PLC output energizes a **contactor** or sends an analog reference (4–20 mA / 0–10 V) to the **VFD**.
- VFD controls motor speed using **V/f = constant** law (scalar control) or **vector control**.
- Motor torque is regulated by adjusting the slip frequency.

### Stage 4 — Supervision (SCADA Layer)
- SCADA polls PLC registers cyclically (typically every **250 – 1000 ms**).
- Tags: Motor Run Status, Speed Setpoint, Current, Object Count, Alarm Log.

### Stage 5 — Operator Interface
- HMI pushbuttons mirror the physical pushbuttons.
- Trend charts display current vs. time.
- Pop-up alarms for overload, jam, E-stop activation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the table below as a single-page revision sheet before the lab exam/viva.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Belt linear speed | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | D = pulley dia (m), N = pulley rpm |
| 2 | Motor rpm from belt speed | $N = \dfrac{60\,v}{\pi D}$ | v (m/s) |
| 3 | Motor shaft power | $P = \dfrac{F_{total} \cdot v}{\eta}$ | F in N, v in m/s, η = 0.85–0.95 |
| 4 | Torque on motor shaft | $T = \dfrac{9550 \cdot P_{kW}}{N_{rpm}}$ | T in N·m |
| 5 | Mass flow rate (bulk) | $Q_m = A \cdot v \cdot \rho$ | A = cross-section (m²), ρ = kg/m³ |
| 6 | Throughput (discrete) | $Q_n = \dfrac{60}{t_{cycle}}$ | t_cycle = time between 2 objects (s) |
| 7 | V/f ratio (constant) | $\dfrac{V}{f} = \dfrac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}$ | V in V, f in Hz |
| 8 | Slip of induction motor | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | N_s = synchronous rpm |
| 9 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{p}$ | p = number of poles |
| 10 | PLC scan time | $t_{scan} = t_{IIT} + t_{logic} + t_{OIT} + t_{comm}$ | All in ms |
| 11 | Belt tension (horizontal) | $T_1 = T_2 + F_{friction}$ | Tensions in N |
| 12 | Conveyor drive power incl. incline | $P = \dfrac{(m g \sin\theta + \mu m g \cos\theta) \cdot v}{\eta}$ | θ = angle, μ = friction coeff. |
| 13 | Stopping distance (ramp-down) | $s_{stop} = \dfrac{v^2}{2 a_{dec}}$ | a_dec = deceleration (m/s²) |
| 14 | Counter (object) | $C = C + 1$ on rising edge of PXS2 | C is integer tag |
| 15 | SCADA poll time | $T_{poll} = N_{tags} \cdot t_{per\,tag}$ | t_per_tag depends on protocol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU lab exam, you are expected to know at least **formulas 1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 9, and 15** by heart. The rest are application-specific.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Conveyor automation systems are the **backbone of Industry 4.0** in:

- **Automotive assembly lines** — body shop conveyor synchronized with robotic welders.
- **Food & Beverage** — bottle filling, capping, and labelling lines.
- **Pharmaceutical packaging** — blister pack counting and sorting.
- **E-commerce warehouses** — Amazon-style sortation conveyors with cross-belt diverters.
- **Mining & Bulk Handling** — long-distance overland conveyors up to **>10 km**.

> The lab experiment simulates a **miniaturized Industry 4.0 cell** — mastering it prepares you for real plant commissioning roles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Belt Speed and Motor RPM Relationship

**Given:** A conveyor has a head pulley of diameter **D = 0.2 m** driven by a 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor through a **gear reduction ratio of G = 5:1** (motor turns 5 times for every 1 pulley revolution).

**Find:** (a) The synchronous speed of the motor, (b) The motor speed at 4% slip, (c) The pulley speed, (d) The belt linear speed.

### Step 1 — Synchronous Speed
The synchronous speed of an induction motor is given by:

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{p}
$$

Substituting **f = 50 Hz** and **p = 4 poles**:

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

### Step 2 — Rotor (Actual) Speed at s = 0.04
Using the slip relation $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$:

$$
N_r = N_s \cdot (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96 = 1440 \text{ rpm}
$$

### Step 3 — Pulley Speed via Gear Ratio
The motor drives the pulley with a 5:1 reduction. Hence:

$$
N_{pulley} = \frac{N_{motor}}{G} = \frac{1440}{5} = 288 \text{ rpm}
$$

### Step 4 — Belt Linear Speed
Using $v = \pi D N / 60$:

$$
v = \frac{\pi \times 0.2 \times 288}{60} = \frac{180.96}{60} = 3.016 \text{ m/s}
$$

> **Engineering Comment:** A belt speed of ~3 m/s is typical for **medium-throughput** FMCG conveyors. If the lab conveyor specification requires **v ≤ 1.5 m/s**, the gear ratio must be increased to **G = 10:1**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Motor Power Required to Drive a Loaded Conveyor

**Given:**
- Belt speed **v = 1.5 m/s**
- Total mass on belt **m = 50 kg** (objects + belt + idlers lumped)
- Horizontal conveyor (θ = 0°)
- Coefficient of friction **μ = 0.3**
- Overall drive efficiency **η = 0.85**

**Find:** Motor shaft power required.

### Step 1 — Total Resistance Force
On a horizontal belt, the dominant force is the friction between the loaded belt and the idlers:

$$
F_{friction} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g
$$

Substituting μ = 0.3, m = 50 kg, g = 9.81 m/s²:

$$
F_{friction} = 0.3 \times 50 \times 9.81 = 147.15 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2 — Mechanical Power at the Belt
Power transmitted to the belt:

$$
P_{belt} = F_{friction} \times v = 147.15 \times 1.5 = 220.7 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 3 — Motor Shaft Power (Account for Losses)
Including gear and motor efficiency:

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{belt}}{\eta} = \frac{220.7}{0.85} \approx 259.7 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 4 — Selection of Standard Motor Rating
Always round **up** to the next standard IEC frame size. The closest ratings are **0.37 kW (0.5 HP)** or **0.25 kW**. Select **0.37 kW** for a **20–30 % service factor** margin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Always select a motor with a **service factor ≥ 1.15** when the load involves start-stop cycles — conveyors are precisely such a load.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — VFD V/f Reference Calculation

**Given:** Motor rating — 3-phase, **415 V**, **50 Hz**, **4-pole**.

**Find:** (a) Rated V/f, (b) V/f at 25 Hz, (c) Voltage to be applied at 25 Hz in V/f mode.

### Step 1 — Rated V/f
$$
\left(\frac{V}{f}\right)_{rated} = \frac{415}{50} = 8.3 \text{ V/Hz}
$$

### Step 2 — Voltage at 25 Hz (Maintaining V/f = 8.3)
$$
V_{25Hz} = 8.3 \times 25 = 207.5 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 3 — Speed at 25 Hz (Open-loop, ignoring slip)
Synchronous speed scales linearly with frequency:

$$
N_{s,25} = \frac{120 \times 25}{4} = 750 \text{ rpm}
$$

Hence the **speed ratio** is exactly **25/50 = 0.5**, i.e., motor runs at **half speed**.

> This is the foundation of **multi-speed conveyor** recipes (e.g., slow speed for delicate bottles, high speed for empty cartons) implemented in the SCADA recipe manager.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Lab Experiment Mapping

| Lab Sub-task | Sensor / Hardware | PLC I/O Address | Ladder Element | SCADA Tag |
|-------------|-------------------|-----------------|----------------|-----------|
| Start the conveyor | NO Push-button (Green) | I0.0 | NO contact — Seal-in | `MOTOR_CMD` |
| Stop the conveyor | NC Push-button (Red) | I0.1 | NC contact in series | `MOTOR_CMD` |
| Emergency stop | NC Mushroom-head PB | I0.2 | NC in series with coil | `E_STOP_STATUS` |
| Detect object at PXS1 | Inductive PNP NO | I0.3 | Rising-edge (one-shot) | `OBJ_DETECT_LD` |
| Detect object at PXS2 | Photoelectric PNP NO | I0.4 | Counter input | `OBJ_COUNT` |
| Motor overload fault | NO contact of OL relay | I0.5 | NC in rung | `MOTOR_FAULT` |
| Run forward contactor | Contactor coil (KM1) | Q0.0 | Output rung | `KM1_STATUS` |
| Run reverse contactor | Contactor coil (KM2) | Q0.1 | Interlocked with KM1 | `KM2_STATUS` |
| VFD speed reference | Analog output 4–20 mA | AQ0 | Scaling block | `VFD_SPEED_SP` |
| Tower lamp — Green | Lamp (Run indicator) | Q0.2 | Parallel with KM1 | `LAMP_GREEN` |
| Tower lamp — Red | Lamp (Fault indicator) | Q0.3 | Motor fault condition | `LAMP_RED` |
| Buzzer | Piezo buzzer | Q0.4 | One-second pulse | `BUZZER` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PXS1[Prox Sensor 1] -->|Object arrival| PLC1
    PXS2[Prox Sensor 2] -->|Object at unload| PLC1
    PB_S[Start PB] --> PLC1
    PB_T[Stop PB] --> PLC1
    PB_E[E Stop PB] --> PLC1
    OL[Overload Relay] --> PLC1
    PLC1[PLC CPU] -->|Digital out| KM[Contactor KM1]
    PLC1 -->|Analog 4-20 mA| VFD[VFD AC Drive]
    KM --> M[3 Phase Induction Motor]
    VFD --> M
    M --> CONV[Conveyor Belt]
    CONV -->|Feedback| PXS1
    CONV -->|Feedback| PXS2
    PLC1 <-->|Modbus TCP| SCADA[SCADA HMI]
    SCADA -->|Display| OP[Operator]
    PLC1 -->|Alarms| BUZ[Buzzer]
    PLC1 -->|Indication| LMP[Tower Lamp]
```

## 4.2 PLC Ladder Logic Flow (Rung-by-Rung Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Rung 1 - Master Seal In] --> R2
    R2[Rung 2 - Forward Run] --> R3
    R3[Rung 3 - Reverse Jog with Interlock] --> R4
    R4[Rung 4 - Object Counter on PXS2] --> R5
    R5[Rung 5 - Auto Stop at Count equals Setpoint] --> R6
    R6[Rung 6 - Fault Latch and Reset] --> R7
    R7[Rung 7 - VFD Speed Reference Scaling] --> R8
    R8[Rung 8 - Tower Lamp Driver]
```

## 4.3 Ladder Logic — Textual Representation (Ladder Diagram View)

**Rung 1 — Master Seal-In (Run Latch)**

```
   PB_S ─┐
         ├──[ ]──┬──────────( M_RUN )──┐
   M_RUN ┘      │                       │
                │                       │
   PB_T ───────[NC]─────────────────────┘
                │
   PB_E ───────[NC]─────────────────────
                │
   OL_FLT ─────[NC]─────────────────────
```

**Rung 2 — Forward Contactor Driver**

```
   M_RUN ────[ ]──┬───────────────( KM1_FWD )──
                  │
   M_REV ───────[NC]──────────────────────────
                  │
   T_ACC ────────[ ]──┬─────────( KM1_FWD )  (seal)
```

**Rung 3 — Counter on PXS2**

```
   PXS2 ────[ P ]─────────( CTU C1, PV=10 )──
```

> **Note:** `P` denotes a **rising-edge (positive transition) contact** that triggers only on the 0→1 transition — preventing multiple counts on a single object.

**Rung 4 — VFD Speed Reference**

```
   SPD_DI ─[MW10]───[SCALE 0-100 to 0-4095]──( AQW0 )──
```

## 4.4 SCADA HMI Screen Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SCR1[Main Mimic Screen] --> SCR2[Control Popup]
    SCR1 --> SCR3[Trend Screen]
    SCR1 --> SCR4[Alarm Summary]
    SCR1 --> SCR5[Recipe Manager]
    SCR1 --> SCR6[User Login]
    SCR2 -->|Speed SP| PLC[PLC Tags]
    SCR2 -->|Start Stop| PLC
    SCR3 -->|Motor Current| PLC
    SCR3 -->|Belt Speed| PLC
    SCR4 -->|Alarm Ack| PLC
    SCR5 -->|Recipe Download| PLC
    SCR6 -->|Auth Tags| PLC
```

## 4.5 Electrical Power Wiring Schematic (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCB[3-Phase MCB] --> OLR[Thermal Overload Relay]
    OLR --> COIL[Contactor KM1 Main Contacts]
    COIL --> VFD_IN[VFD Input L1 L2 L3]
    VFD_IN --> VFD_OUT[VFD Output U V W]
    VFD_OUT --> MOTOR[3-Phase IM]
    PLC_DO[PLC DO Q0.0] --> RELAY[Interface Relay 24V DC]
    RELAY --> KM_COIL[KM1 Coil 230V AC]
    PLC_AO[PLC AO 4-20 mA] --> VFD_AI[VFD AI Reference]
    PE[Protective Earth] --> MOTOR
    PE --> VFD
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Set A]
**Q: List any three advantages of using a VFD for conveyor speed control over a conventional star-delta starter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Continuous speed variation** across a wide range (0–50/60 Hz), enabling matching of conveyor speed to product type — not possible in fixed star-delta (½ mark).
2. **Soft start and soft stop** through programmable ramp-up/ramp-down, reducing mechanical shock and belt wear (½ mark).
3. **Energy savings** of up to 30–50 % at partial loads, since VFD reduces both voltage and frequency (½ mark).
4. **Built-in motor protection** — overcurrent, overvoltage, phase loss, earth fault (½ mark).
5. **Easy integration with PLC/SCADA** via analog reference and digital status feedback (½ mark).
6. **Improved process accuracy** through closed-loop speed control using encoder feedback (½ mark).

*(Any 3 → 3 marks)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define SCADA and state its four primary functions in a conveyor automation system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) is a software system that allows real-time monitoring, data acquisition, and remote control of industrial processes from a central HMI.

- **Four primary functions (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
  1. **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** — Poll PLC registers and display motor speed, current, object count.
  2. **Supervisory Control** — Send start/stop and speed setpoint commands from HMI to PLC.
  3. **Alarm Management** — Detect, log, and acknowledge abnormal conditions (overload, E-stop).
  4. **Historical Trending & Reporting** — Archive process data for analysis and audit.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Design a PLC-based conveyor system with the following requirements:**
- Forward run with start PB, stop PB, E-stop.
- Object counting at the unloading zone using a photoelectric sensor.
- Auto-stop after counting 10 objects.
- Indication: Green lamp during run, Red lamp on fault, Buzzer on E-stop.
- VFD speed control via analog output 4–20 mA.
- SCADA screen showing motor current, speed, and count.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Draw the I/O assignment table, the Ladder Logic (rungs), and the SCADA HMI screen mock-up.

**Model Answer:**

**1. I/O Assignment Table (2 marks)**

| Address | Type | Device | Function |
|---------|------|--------|----------|
| I0.0 | DI | NO PB | Start |
| I0.1 | DI | NC PB | Stop |
| I0.2 | DI | NC PB (E-Stop) | Emergency |
| I0.3 | DI | PNP PXS | Object at unload |
| I0.4 | DI | NO contact (OL) | Motor fault |
| Q0.0 | DO | Contactor KM1 | Motor ON |
| Q0.1 | DO | Lamp Green | Run indicator |
| Q0.2 | DO | Lamp Red | Fault indicator |
| Q0.3 | DO | Buzzer | Alarm |
| AQW0 | AO | VFD AI | 4–20 mA ref |
| AIW0 | AI | VFD AO | Motor current |

**2. Ladder Logic — Rung Description (3 marks)**

- **Rung 1:** Latch run with seal-in: `I0.0 OR M0.0` AND `I0.1 NC AND I0.2 NC AND I0.4 NC` → `M0.0` (master run bit).
- **Rung 2:** `M0.0` → `Q0.0` (motor) AND `Q0.1` (green lamp).
- **Rung 3:** `I0.3` (rising edge) → `CTU C1, PV = 10`. Resets to 0 when reached.
- **Rung 4:** `C1.DN` (done bit) → resets `M0.0` → conveyor stops.
- **Rung 5:** `M0.0` OR `I0.2 NOT` → analog scaling block to drive `AQW0` from `MW10` (setpoint).
- **Rung 6:** `I0.4` (overload) OR `I0.2` (E-stop) → `Q0.2` (red lamp) AND `Q0.3` (buzzer pulsed at 1 Hz by a timer).

**3. SCADA HMI Layout (2 marks)**
- Top banner: title, date-time, user.
- Center: belt animation, current motor speed gauge, current ammeter.
- Right panel: object count display, setpoint entry, start/stop pushbuttons.
- Bottom: alarm banner with red flashing "OVERLOAD" / "E-STOP" labels.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Calculate the belt speed and motor power for a 50 kg loaded belt with pulley diameter 150 mm, motor rpm 1440, gear ratio 4:1, μ = 0.3, η = 0.85.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pulley speed**

$$
N_{pulley} = \frac{N_{motor}}{G} = \frac{1440}{4} = 360 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Belt linear speed**

$$
v = \frac{\pi D N_{pulley}}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.15 \times 360}{60} = 2.827 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Friction force**

$$
F = \mu m g = 0.3 \times 50 \times 9.81 = 147.15 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical power at belt**

$$
P_{belt} = F \times v = 147.15 \times 2.827 = 416.0 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Motor shaft power**

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{belt}}{\eta} = \frac{416.0}{0.85} = 489.4 \text{ W} \approx 0.49 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Standard motor selection**
Round up to next standard frame → **0.55 kW (0.75 HP), 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> - **Forgetting to apply efficiency η** while computing motor power — leads to *under-rated* motor selection (lose 1 mark).
> - **Using pulley rpm directly in v = πDN/60 without considering gear ratio** — common when the question gives motor rpm. Always check whether rpm refers to the motor or the pulley.
> - **Selecting the exact calculated kW** instead of rounding up to the next standard IEC frame — a real engineer never specifies a non-standard motor.
> - **Ignoring the analog scaling** between PLC integer (0–27648 for Siemens, 0–4095 for Delta) and the 4–20 mA loop — causes incorrect VFD reference.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Design a conveyor with auto-reverse on jam detection. The system should:**
- Run in forward direction on Start PB.
- If PXS1 stays HIGH for more than 5 s (jam condition), reverse the motor for 2 s, then resume forward.
- An emergency stop overrides all logic.
- Log the number of jam events on the SCADA screen.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Develop the Ladder Logic for jam detection and auto-reverse.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Rung 1 — Forward run latch (1 mark)**

```
   I_Start ─┐
            ├──[ ]──┬────( M_FWD )──┐
   M_Fwd    ┘       │               │
                    │               │
   I_Stop ──────────[NC]────────────┘
                    │
   I_ESTOP ─────────[NC]────────────
```

**Rung 2 — Jam detection timer (2 marks)**
- Use **TON (On-Delay Timer)** T1 with preset PT = 5 s.
- Enable input = `PXS1 AND M_Fwd`.
- When T1 completes → `T1.DN` flag set → sets bit `M_JAM`.

$$
t_{jam} \ge 5 \text{ s} \Rightarrow M_{JAM} = 1
$$

**Rung 3 — Reverse run (auto) with time limit (2 marks)**
- `M_Jam` → Start another timer `T2` with PT = 2 s.
- `M_Jam AND NOT T2.DN` → energize reverse contactor `KM2_REV`.
- Forward contactor `KM1_FWD` is interlocked (NC in series).
- After 2 s, `T2.DN` resets `M_Jam` and allows forward to resume.

**Rung 4 — Jam counter and SCADA tag (1 mark)**
- `M_Jam` rising edge → `CTU JamCnt, PV = 65535` (max).
- SCADA tag = `JamCnt.CV`.

**Rung 5 — E-stop override (1 mark)**
- `I_ESTOP` is wired in series with **all output rungs** (KM1, KM2, BUZ, LMP).
- It is also used to **reset** the jam counter and timers on operator command.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Calculate VFD parameters for a 1.5 kW, 415 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole motor driving the conveyor. Assume the load demands 60 % speed.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rated V/f (1 mark)**
$$
\left(\frac{V}{f}\right)_{rated} = \frac{415}{50} = 8.3 \text{ V/Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Frequency for 60 % speed (2 marks)**
$$
f_{60\%} = 0.6 \times 50 = 30 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage to be set on VFD (1 mark)**
$$
V_{30Hz} = 8.3 \times 30 = 249 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Speed at 30 Hz (assuming s = 0.04) (2 marks)**
$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 30}{4} = 900 \text{ rpm}
$$

$$
N_r = 900 \times (1 - 0.04) = 864 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 5 — Analog reference from PLC (1 mark)**
For 4–20 mA mapping to 0–50 Hz:
- Required current for 30 Hz: $I = 4 + (30/50) \times 16 = 4 + 9.6 = 13.6$ mA.
- In PLC integer (0–27648, Siemens): $D = (13.6 - 4)/16 \times 27648 = 16589$ → load to `AQW0`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks (Question B-B):**
> - **Forgetting the interlock** between KM1 and KM2 — this can cause a **short circuit** across the supply. This is a *fatal design error* that costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Using TOF (Off-Delay) instead of TON** for jam detection. Jam is a *continuous-high* condition → ON-delay is correct.
> - **Not specifying the E-stop wiring** as a **hardwired (fail-safe) loop** outside the PLC. Examiner expects E-stop to be a *Category-1* safety stop per IEC 60204.
> - **Neglecting to mention mechanical interlock** between the two contactors in addition to the electrical interlock — both are required by KTU best practice.
> - **Not stating PLC scan-time budget** — for timers ≤ 1 s, scan time < 50 ms is acceptable. Always mention.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Read this section **aloud** during the last 5 minutes before entering the lab exam hall.

### A. Hardware Checklist
- [ ] **3-phase MCB** (short-circuit protection)
- [ ] **Thermal Overload Relay (OLR)** calibrated to **motor FLC × 1.1**
- [ ] **Contactor(s) KM1 (forward), KM2 (reverse)** with **mechanical + electrical interlock**
- [ ] **VFD** with V/f or vector control mode
- [ ] **3-phase Induction Motor** (typically TEFC, Class F insulation, IP55)
- [ ] **Proximity / Photoelectric sensors** — PNP, NO, 24 V DC
- [ ] **Emergency Stop** — mushroom-head, NC, twist-release, **red on yellow background**
- [ ] **Tower lamp** — 3-stack (Red = Fault, Amber = Caution, Green = Run)
- [ ] **Buzzer / Hooter** — 24 V DC, 90 dB

### B. PLC I/O Checklist
- [ ] **Digital Inputs:** Start, Stop, E-Stop, PXS1, PXS2, OLR fault.
- [ ] **Digital Outputs:** KM1, KM2, Green Lamp, Red Lamp, Buzzer, VFD Enable.
- [ ] **Analog Output:** 4–20 mA to VFD reference.
- [ ] **Analog Input (optional):** VFD feedback current/voltage.

### C. Software Checklist
- [ ] **Ladder Logic** implemented in cyclic scan.
- [ ] **Seal-in (latching)** for Start PB.
- [ ] **Edge-triggered (one-shot)** for object counting — avoids double count.
- [ ] **Counter with preset (CTU)** for batch-stop logic.
- [ ] **TON / TOF timers** for jam detection and reverse duration.
- [ ] **Analog scaling block** to map PLC integer → 4–20 mA → Hz.
- [ ] **Fault latch** with manual reset.

### D. SCADA Tags Checklist
- [ ] `Motor_Run_Status` (BOOL)
- [ ] `Motor_Speed_Hz` (REAL)
- [ ] `Motor_Current_A` (REAL)
- [ ] `Object_Count` (INT)
- [ ] `Jam_Count` (INT)
- [ ] `E_Stop_Status` (BOOL)
- [ ] `VFD_Speed_SP` (REAL)
- [ ] `System_Alarm` (BOOL with priority)

### E. Viva-Voce Quick-Fire Questions
1. *What is the scan time of your PLC and how did you measure it?*
2. *Why is V/f kept constant in scalar VFD control?*
3. *What is the difference between PNP and NPN sensors, and which one does your lab use?*
4. *Why must the E-Stop be NC and not NO?*
5. *What happens if both KM1 and KM2 close simultaneously? How do you prevent it?*
6. *What is the role of OLR? Why not use a fuse?*
7. *If the conveyor must be reversed on jam, why do we need a time limit?*
8. *How does the SCADA poll the PLC — which protocol and at what rate?*
9. *Why use rising-edge contact for the counter?*
10. *What is the difference between hardwired and software interlock?*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Mantra for the Lab Exam:**
> *"Sensors → PLC → Drive → Motor → Conveyor → SCADA. Always trace the signal flow in that exact order when explaining your design."*
